topics.title: Resting and Action Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resting and Action Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state, transmembrane electrical potential difference (measured in millivolts) that exists across the plasma membrane of a living cell when it is **not** being stimulated. For a typical mammalian neuron, this value is approximately **−70 mV** (interior of the cell is negative with respect to the exterior).

The RMP is a *quasi-equilibrium* state established by the differential distribution of ions (mainly K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻) maintained actively by the **Sodium-Potassium ATPase Pump (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase)** and passively by selective ion permeability of the lipid bilayer.

### Physical Constants for Neuronal Physiology
- **Body Temperature (T):** **310 K (37 °C)**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol**
- **Thermal Voltage (RT/F):** **≈ 26.71 mV** at 37 °C
- **2.303 × RT/F (for log₁₀):** **≈ 61.54 mV** at 37 °C

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Battery" Model**
> Imagine the cell membrane as a **slightly leaky, rechargeable battery**:
> - The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** is the **recharger** — it continuously pumps 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP hydrolyzed, creating a chemical gradient.
> - The **leaky K⁺ channels** act as a **soft tap** allowing K⁺ to slowly leak out following its concentration gradient, dragging positive charge out of the cell, leaving the inside negative.
> - At equilibrium, pump current (outward) = leak current (inward), giving us the **−70 mV** "battery voltage."

---

## 1.2 Action Potential (AP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **Action Potential** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing depolarization and repolarization of the membrane potential that propagates along an excitable cell (neuron, muscle fiber), caused by the sequential opening and closing of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels. It lasts approximately **1–2 milliseconds** in a mammalian neuron.

### Phases of an Action Potential (5 Phases)
1. **Resting State** (≈ −70 mV): All voltage-gated channels closed; leak channels maintain RMP.
2. **Depolarization (Rising Phase):** Stimulus → threshold (−55 mV) → voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → Na⁺ rushes in → membrane potential shoots to ≈ **+30 to +40 mV**.
3. **Peak (Overshoot):** Na⁺ channels **inactivate**; voltage-gated K⁺ channels open.
4. **Repolarization (Falling Phase):** K⁺ efflux drives membrane back toward negative values.
5. **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot):** Slow K⁺ channels remain open briefly → membrane dips below −70 mV (≈ −80 mV), then recovers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino Waterfall"**
> Picture a row of small water gates along a canal (the axon):
> - At rest, all gates are shut (**−70 mV**).
> - When one gate is pushed open past a **threshold force** (−55 mV), it opens fully, water rushes through (**Na⁺ influx → depolarization**), and the water's force then pushes the **next gate** open.
> - Once a gate has opened, it **automatically locks shut** (Na⁺ inactivation) and a **drain valve opens** (K⁺ efflux → repolarization), preventing the signal from going backward.
> - This cascade produces a single, unidirectional **"wave"** of electrical activity — the action potential.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical Engineering

- **ECG/EEG/EMG Signal Origin:** All bioelectric signals recorded clinically (Electrocardiogram, Electroencephalogram, Electromyogram) are summed, propagated action potentials from millions of cells.
- **Neural Prosthetics & Cochlear Implants:** Engineers design electrodes that *trigger* or *block* action potentials.
- **Drug Design:** Anesthetics, anti-epileptics, and local anesthetics (e.g., lidocaine) work by **modifying voltage-gated Na⁺ channel kinetics**.
- **Defibrillators:** Deliver high-voltage pulses to **synchronize chaotic action potentials** in cardiac tissue.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ionic Basis of the Resting Potential

The RMP arises from three simultaneous phenomena:

- **Ionic Concentration Gradients** maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (electrogenic pump — moves 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle, net −1 charge outside).
- **Differential Membrane Permeability:** At rest, **P_K ≫ P_Na ≫ P_Cl**, so K⁺ dominates the membrane potential.
- **Electrochemical Equilibrium:** Net flux of each ion is determined by both its **chemical gradient** and the **electrical gradient** (membrane voltage).

### The Three Driving Forces on an Ion
1. **Chemical (Diffusion) Force:** ∝ ln([Ion]_out / [Ion]_in)
2. **Electrical Force:** ∝ zVm
3. **Electromotive (Electrochemical) Force:** Sum of the above = Nernst Potential (E_ion)

---

## 2.2 Voltage-Gated Ion Channels — The Heart of the AP

| Channel Type | Activation Trigger | Role in AP | Gating Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Gated Na⁺ (Na_v)** | Depolarization to threshold | Rising phase (Phase 2) | Fast activation → **inactivation** within ms |
| **Voltage-Gated K⁺ (K_v)** | Depolarization (delayed) | Repolarization (Phase 4) | Slow activation, no inactivation, slow closure |
| **Leak K⁺ Channels** | Always open | Maintains RMP | Constitutively open |
| **Ligand-Gated (e.g., ACh)** | Neurotransmitter binding | Initiates EPSP/IPSP | Synaptic input only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Principle — All-or-Nothing Law**
> An action potential is fired **only if** the membrane reaches the **threshold potential (≈ −55 mV)**. Subthreshold stimuli produce only graded local potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs) that decay with distance. Once threshold is crossed, the AP amplitude is **independent of stimulus strength** — this is the **All-or-Nothing Principle**.

---

## 2.3 Refractory Periods

| Phase | Timing | Cause | Functional Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Refractory Period (ARP)** | During + shortly after AP | Na⁺ channels inactivated | **No new AP can be elicited**, regardless of stimulus. Enforces unidirectional propagation. |
| **Relative Refractory Period (RRP)** | During hyperpolarization | Some Na⁺ channels recovered, K⁺ still partially open | A **stronger-than-normal** stimulus can elicit a new AP. Limits **maximum firing frequency** of a neuron. |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Symbolic Form | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$ | Volts (V) | Equilibrium potential for a single permeant ion |
| 2 | Nernst (log₁₀ form) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$ | Volts (V) | Same as above; easier for log-base-10 tables |
| 3 | **At 37 °C (2.303 RT/F)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.54}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$ | mV | Standard numerical form used in KTU problems |
| 4 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | Volts (V) | Multi-ion membrane potential using permeabilities |
| 5 | **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry** | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in (per ATP) | — | Net −1 charge ejected per cycle; contributes ~−4 mV to RMP |
| 6 | **Cable Equation (passive spread)** | $V(x) = V_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}$ | Volts, meters | Decay of subthreshold signal with distance constant λ |
| 7 | **Conduction Velocity (Myelinated)** | $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ | m/s | Larger axon diameter → faster conduction |
| 8 | **Hodgkin Cycle (Conceptual)** | I = C(dV/dt) + Σ I_ion | A, F, V | Total membrane current = capacitive + ionic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Nernst equation is the foundation of **ion-selective electrodes (ISEs)** used in clinical blood-gas analyzers and pH meters. The GHK equation is used in designing **neural interface electrodes** to predict how the local ionic microenvironment affects recording sensitivity.

---

## 2.5 Propagation of the Action Potential

- **Continuous Propagation (Unmyelinated Axons):** Sequential depolarization along the entire axonal membrane. Slower (≈ 1 m/s).
- **Saltatory Conduction (Myelinated Axons):** AP "jumps" between **Nodes of Ranvier**, where Na⁺ channels are densely clustered. Faster (up to **120 m/s** in large motor neurons) and **metabolically efficient**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diseases of Demyelination** (e.g., Multiple Sclerosis, Guillain-Barré Syndrome) destroy myelin, drastically slowing or blocking AP conduction — illustrating the clinical importance of this physiology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (from Electrochemical Equilibrium)

**Starting Premise:** At equilibrium, the net electrochemical potential (μ) of an ion must be equal on both sides of the membrane.

The electrochemical potential for an ion *i* of charge *z* in phase α is:

$$\bar{\mu}_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{0,\alpha} + RT \ln a_i^{\alpha} + z_i F \phi^{\alpha}$$

where:
- $\mu_i^{0,\alpha}$ = standard chemical potential in phase α
- $a_i^{\alpha}$ = activity (≈ concentration for dilute biological solutions)
- $\phi^{\alpha}$ = electrical potential of phase α
- $z_i$ = ion valence
- $F$ = Faraday's constant

**Step 1:** Set the electrochemical potentials equal across the membrane (inside = outside) at equilibrium:

$$\mu_i^{0,in} + RT \ln [Ion]_{in} + z F V_{in} = \mu_i^{0,out} + RT \ln [Ion]_{out} + z F V_{out}$$

**Step 2:** Assume standard chemical potentials are equal ($\mu_i^{0,in} = \mu_i^{0,out}$); cancel them.

$$RT \ln [Ion]_{in} + z F V_{in} = RT \ln [Ion]_{out} + z F V_{out}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate the membrane potential $E_{ion} = V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln [Ion]_{out} - RT \ln [Ion]_{in}$$

$$zF \cdot E_{ion} = RT \ln \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $E_{ion}$:

$$\boxed{\,E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\,}$$

**Step 5:** Convert natural log to log₁₀ using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the numerical value at 37 °C: $\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.06154 \, \text{V} = 61.54 \, \text{mV}$

$$E_{ion} \approx \dfrac{61.54}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}} \quad \text{(in mV)}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{out} = 5 \, \text{mM}$
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140 \, \text{mM}$
- $z = +1$ for K⁺
- $T = 37\,^\circ\text{C}$

**Solution:**

$$E_{K} = \dfrac{61.54}{+1} \log_{10} \dfrac{5}{140}$$

$$\dfrac{5}{140} = 0.0357$$

$$\log_{10}(0.0357) = -1.447$$

$$E_{K} = 61.54 \times (-1.447)$$

$$\boxed{E_{K} \approx -89.0 \, \text{mV}}$$

This matches the experimentally measured K⁺ equilibrium potential, confirming that **K⁺ permeability is the dominant factor** in the resting membrane potential.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for Na⁺

**Given:**
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145 \, \text{mM}$
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 12 \, \text{mM}$
- $z = +1$

**Solution:**

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times \log_{10} \dfrac{145}{12}$$

$$\dfrac{145}{12} = 12.083$$

$$\log_{10}(12.083) = 1.0822$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times 1.0822$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +66.6 \, \text{mV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice: $E_{Na}$ is strongly **positive**. This is the driving force that propels Na⁺ into the cell during depolarization. The closer the membrane gets to $E_{Na}$, the smaller the net Na⁺ current.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation

**Given (typical mammalian neuron at rest):**
- $[K^+]_{out} = 5$, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$, $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$
- $[Cl^-]_{out} = 120$, $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$
- Relative permeabilities: $P_K = 1.0$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$

**Solution:**

$$V_m = 61.54 \log_{10} \dfrac{(1.0)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(120)}$$

**Numerator:**

$$(1.0)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 5 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.6$$

**Denominator:**

$$(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(120) = 140 + 0.48 + 54 = 194.48$$

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{12.6}{194.48} = 0.0648$$

**Logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.0648) = -1.1885$$

**Final Calculation:**

$$V_m = 61.54 \times (-1.1885) \approx -73.1 \, \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx -73 \, \text{mV} \quad (\text{close to experimental } {-70 \, \text{mV}})}$$

The slight discrepancy is due to the small **electrogenic contribution of the Na⁺/K⁺ pump** (≈ −4 mV), not captured by the purely passive GHK equation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Phase-Wise Ionic Currents

| Phase | Membrane State | Dominant Open Channel | Net Current | dV/dt | Conductance Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Rest | Polarized (−70 mV) | Leak K⁺ | ~0 (steady) | 0 | g_Na ≈ 0, g_K low |
| 2. Depolarization | Rising to +30 mV | Voltage-gated Na⁺ (activated) | Inward (Na⁺) | Strongly positive | g_Na ↑↑↑, g_K slight ↑ |
| 3. Peak | ~ +30 to +40 mV | Na⁺ inactivating, K⁺ activating | I_Na ≈ I_K | ≈ 0 | g_Na ↓, g_K ↑ |
| 4. Repolarization | Falling back to −70 mV | Voltage-gated K⁺ | Outward (K⁺) | Negative | g_K ↑↑, g_Na = 0 |
| 5. Hyperpolarization | Dips to ~ −80 mV | K⁺ slow closure | Outward (K⁺) | Slight positive then 0 | g_K ↓, returning to baseline |
| 6. Recovery | Back to −70 mV | Pump restoring gradients | Pump current | 0 | Resting g_Na, g_K |

---

## 3.6 Derivation of the Length Constant (λ) — Quick Insight

The **length constant** describes how far a local (subthreshold) potential passively spreads along an axon before decaying to 37% of its amplitude:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i + r_e}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

(where $r_m$ = membrane resistance, $r_i$ = axial (intracellular) resistance per unit length, $r_e$ = extracellular resistance assumed small).

A **larger diameter axon** → smaller $r_i$ → **larger λ** → faster, more efficient signal spread — one reason why motor neurons have large diameters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Action Potential Waveform & Ion Channel State Diagram

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the **dynamic interplay of ionic conductances** during an action potential, mapped to its time-voltage waveform.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 0: RESTING STATE] -->|Stimulus to threshold| B[Phase 1: DEPOLARIZATION]
    B -->|Peak around plus 30 mV| C[Phase 2: PEAK OVERSHOOT]
    C -->|Na channel inactivation| D[Phase 3: REPOLARIZATION]
    D -->|K channels stay open| E[Phase 4: HYPERPOLARIZATION]
    E -->|K channels close and pump restores| A

    A -. "V = minus 70 mV, Leak K open, Na Kv closed, K Kv closed" .- A
    B -. "V rises sharply, Na Kv open, K Kv starts opening" .- B
    C -. "V near plus 30 mV, Na Kv inactivating, K Kv open" .- C
    D -. "V falls toward minus 70 mV, Na Kv closed inactivated, K Kv open" .- D
    E -. "V near minus 80 mV, K Kv still open, Na Kv recovering" .- E
```

---

## 4.2 Ionic Flux & Pump Cycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EX["EXTRACELLULAR SPACE"]
        NaOut["Na plus 145 mM"]
        KOut["K plus 5 mM"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["NEURONAL MEMBRANE"]
        NaKV["Voltage Gated Na Channel"]
        KKV["Voltage Gated K Channel"]
        LeakK["Leak K Channel"]
        Pump["Na K ATPase Pump"]
    end

    subgraph IN["INTRACELLULAR SPACE"]
        NaIn["Na plus 12 mM"]
        KIn["K plus 140 mM"]
        Anion["Fixed Anions negative"]
    end

    NaOut -- Depolarization influx --> NaIn
    KIn -- Repolarization efflux --> KOut
    KIn -- Leak efflux --> KOut
    Pump -- 3 Na plus out --> NaOut
    Pump -- 2 K plus in --> KIn
    Anion -. Excess negative charge .-> IN
```

---

## 4.3 Refractory Period & Propagation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[AP Initiated at Point A] --> P[Propagation to Point B]
    P --> Q{Point B Status}
    Q -->|ARP active| R[Cannot Fire AP Signal Stops]
    Q -->|RRP active| T[Needs Stronger Stimulus]
    Q -->|Recovered| U[Normal AP Continues Forward]
    R --> V[Unidirectional Saltatory Conduction in Myelinated Axon]
    T --> V
    U --> V
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Permeability vs Voltage (Functional State Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Membrane Voltage] --> N{Permeability Dominant Ion}
    N -->|At rest minus 70 mV| O[K plus dominates gK high, gNa low]
    N -->|Rising phase 0 to plus 30 mV| P[Na plus dominates gNa high]
    N -->|Falling phase plus 30 to minus 70 mV| Q[K plus dominates gK high, gNa inactivated]
    N -->|Undershoot minus 80 mV| R[K plus still elevated, returning to rest]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
> Define **Resting Membrane Potential**. Mention its typical value for a mammalian neuron and list the two principal ionic contributors to its establishment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** RMP is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell in the absence of stimulation, with the **inside negative relative to the outside**.
- **(1 mark)** Typical value: **−70 mV** (range −60 to −90 mV across cell types).
- **(1 mark)** Principal ionic contributors: (i) **High intracellular K⁺** driving outward K⁺ leak through leak channels; (ii) **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** maintaining the ionic gradients (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
> What is the **All-or-Nothing Principle** of an action potential? State the typical **threshold value** in a mammalian neuron.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The All-or-Nothing Principle states that once the membrane potential reaches the **threshold**, an action potential of **fixed amplitude** is generated; subthreshold stimuli produce only **graded local potentials** that decay with distance.
- **(1 mark)** The amplitude and shape of the AP are **independent** of stimulus strength — either a **full AP** fires or **none** at all.
- **(1 mark)** Typical threshold value for a mammalian neuron: **≈ −55 mV** (may vary from −50 to −60 mV).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Resting Potential & Nernst/Goldman Equations*
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **Nernst Equation** from electrochemical equilibrium principles. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a mammalian neuron at 37 °C, intracellular and extracellular concentrations are: $[K^+]_{in}=140\,mM$, $[K^+]_{out}=5\,mM$, $[Na^+]_{in}=12\,mM$, $[Na^+]_{out}=145\,mM$, $[Cl^-]_{in}=4\,mM$, $[Cl^-]_{out}=120\,mM$. Using the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** with relative permeabilities $P_K=1.0$, $P_{Na}=0.04$, $P_{Cl}=0.45$, calculate the **resting membrane potential** and comment on why it differs slightly from the K⁺ Nernst potential alone. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**(1 mark)** State the electrochemical potential for ion *i* of valence *z* in any phase α:

$$\bar{\mu}_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{0,\alpha} + RT \ln [Ion]^{\alpha} + zF \phi^{\alpha}$$

**(1 mark)** State equilibrium condition: net flux = 0, so $\bar{\mu}_{i}^{in} = \bar{\mu}_{i}^{out}$.

**(1 mark)** Equate inside and outside potentials, cancel standard potentials:

$$RT \ln [Ion]_{in} + zF V_{in} = RT \ln [Ion]_{out} + zF V_{out}$$

**(1 mark)** Rearrange to isolate membrane voltage $E_{ion} = V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$zF E_{ion} = RT \ln \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$$

**(1 mark)** Solve for $E_{ion}$:

$$E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$$

**(1 mark)** Convert to log₁₀ and substitute 37 °C:

$$E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}} = \dfrac{61.54}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}} \, \text{mV}$$

**(1 mark)** State physical meaning: $E_{ion}$ is the membrane potential at which there is **no net flow** of that ion — the electrical force exactly balances the chemical (diffusion) force.

#### Part (b) — Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Calculation (7 marks)

**(1 mark)** State the GHK voltage equation:

$$V_m = 61.54 \log_{10} \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**(1 mark)** Substitute numerator:

$$(1.0)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 5 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.6$$

**(1 mark)** Substitute denominator:

$$(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(120) = 140 + 0.48 + 54 = 194.48$$

**(1 mark)** Form the ratio and take log₁₀:

$$\dfrac{12.6}{194.48} = 0.0648, \quad \log_{10}(0.0648) = -1.1885$$

**(1 mark)** Compute final value:

$$V_m = 61.54 \times (-1.1885) \approx -73.1 \, \text{mV}$$

**(1 mark)** Compare to pure K⁺ Nernst potential ($E_K \approx -89\,mV$) — the value is **less negative** because a small but finite Na⁺ permeability pulls the membrane potential slightly toward $E_{Na}\,(+66\,mV)$. Additionally, the electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺ pump contributes ~−4 mV.

**(1 mark)** Comment: The GHK equation accounts for **multiple permeant ions simultaneously**, weighted by their relative permeabilities — which is why the calculated RMP is more accurate than any single-ion Nernst potential.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Action Potential Phases & Refractory Periods*
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
>
> **(a)** With a **neat labeled waveform diagram**, describe the **five distinct phases of an action potential** in a mammalian neuron. For each phase, identify the **dominant ionic current**, the **state of Na⁺ and K⁺ voltage-gated channels**, and the **sign of dV/dt**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Absolute and Relative Refractory Periods**. Discuss their physiological significance in (i) ensuring **unidirectional propagation** of the nerve impulse, and (ii) determining the **maximum firing frequency** of a neuron. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Phases of the Action Potential (7 marks)

**Waveform Sketch (1 mark)** — Membrane voltage (mV) on Y-axis (range −80 to +40); Time (ms) on X-axis. Five phases marked with key voltages (−70, −55, +30, −70, −80).

| Phase | Voltage | Dominant Current | Na⁺ Channel | K⁺ Channel | dV/dt |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resting (1) | −70 mV | Leak K⁺ | Closed | Closed (leak open) | 0 |
| Depolarization (2) | −55 → +30 mV | I_Na (inward) | **Open** | Starting to open | Strongly **+** |
| Peak (3) | ≈ +30 mV | I_Na ≈ I_K | **Inactivating** | **Open** | ≈ 0 |
| Repolarization (4) | +30 → −70 mV | I_K (outward) | Closed (inactive) | **Open** | Strongly **−** |
| Hyperpolarization (5) | ≈ −80 mV | I_K residual | Closed (recovering) | Slow closure | Slight **+** |

**(5 marks)** — One mark per correctly described phase including the ion channel states and dV/dt sign, as listed above.

**(1 mark)** — Concluding statement: The waveform reflects the **sequential kinetics of voltage-gated Na⁺ (fast) and K⁺ (delayed) channels**, and once initiated, the AP amplitude is **independent of stimulus strength** (all-or-nothing).

#### Part (b) — Refractory Periods (7 marks)

**(1 mark)** **Absolute Refractory Period (ARP):** Begins at the start of depolarization and lasts through most of repolarization. During ARP, **Na⁺ channels are inactivated** and cannot be re-opened regardless of stimulus strength. → **No new AP can be elicited.**

**(1 mark)** **Relative Refractory Period (RRP):** Begins after ARP and corresponds to the **hyperpolarization (afterpotential)** phase. Some Na⁺ channels have recovered, but K⁺ channels are still partially open, hyperpolarizing the membrane. → **A stronger-than-normal stimulus** is required to reach threshold.

**(2 marks)** **Significance (i) — Unidirectional Propagation:**
- During ARP, the region just behind the advancing AP **cannot be re-excited**.
- The active region thus propagates **forward** toward fresh, excitable membrane.
- This ensures the **nerve impulse travels in one direction only**, from the cell body toward the axon terminal (orthodromic conduction).

**(2 marks)** **Significance (ii) — Maximum Firing Frequency:**
- The total refractory period (ARP + RRP) sets a **minimum time interval** between successive APs.
- Typical ARP + RRP for a mammalian neuron ≈ **1–2 ms**.
- Therefore, **maximum firing frequency** $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{ARP + RRP} \approx 500\text{–}1000 \, \text{Hz}$.
- This limits the rate at which a neuron can encode information.

**(1 mark)** **Clinical note:** Local anesthetics (e.g., lidocaine) work by prolonging Na⁺ channel inactivation, thereby extending ARP and blocking pain-signal conduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error in Nernst Equation:** Students frequently forget that **z = +1 for Na⁺ and K⁺** but **z = −1 for Cl⁻**. Always carry the sign explicitly. For Cl⁻, the equation becomes $E_{Cl} = -\dfrac{61.54}{1} \log_{10}\dfrac{[Cl^-]_{out}}{[Cl^-]_{in}}$.
> 2. **Confusing the Direction of Ion Flow:** Na⁺ flows **IN** during depolarization (down its electrochemical gradient from +66 mV to resting −70 mV); K⁺ flows **OUT** during repolarization (down its gradient from −89 mV to peak +30 mV). Mnemonic: **"Sodium IN, Potassium OUT."**
> 3. **Goldman Equation — Wrong Permeability Placement:** Permeability (P) multiplies the **OUTSIDE concentration in the numerator** and the **INSIDE concentration in the denominator** — but for **Cl⁻, the order is REVERSED** because it is a negative ion. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 4. **Threshold Confusion:** Threshold is **NOT** 0 mV — it is **≈ −55 mV**, the voltage at which enough Na⁺ channels open to overcome K⁺ efflux.
> 5. **Refractory Period Timing:** ARP occurs **during** the action potential, not **after** it. RRP corresponds to the **hyperpolarization phase**, not to the depolarization.
> 6. **Units:** Always convert **mV to V** when using $R = 8.314$ in SI units, or use the **precomputed 61.54 mV constant** at 37 °C to avoid unit mismatches.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Quick-Recall Summary
>
> **Resting Membrane Potential**
> - Value: **−70 mV** (interior negative)
> - Established by: **K⁺ leak (passive)** + **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump (active, electrogenic, 3:2 stoichiometry)**
> - Dominant ion at rest: **K⁺** ($P_K \gg P_{Na}$)
>
> **Nernst Equation (single ion)**
> - $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.54}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}$ mV (at 37 °C)
> - Represents the **equilibrium potential** for one ion
>
> **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation (multi-ion)**
> - Weighted by **permeabilities**; for Cl⁻, inside/outside are swapped
> - Most accurate for **RMP** calculation
>
> **Action Potential Phases (5)**
> 1. **Rest** → −70 mV
> 2. **Depolarization** → stimulus crosses threshold (−55 mV), Na⁺ channels open, **Na⁺ IN**
> 3. **Peak** → +30 to +40 mV, Na⁺ channels **inactivate**
> 4. **Repolarization** → K⁺ channels open, **K⁺ OUT**
> 5. **Hyperpolarization** → −80 mV, slow K⁺ closure
>
> **All-or-Nothing Law**
> - Subthreshold → graded local potential (decays)
> - ≥ Threshold → **fixed-amplitude AP** (independent of stimulus strength)
>
> **Refractory Periods**
> - **ARP** = Na⁺ channels **inactivated** → no AP possible → ensures **unidirectional propagation**
> - **RRP** = Na⁺ channels **recovering** + K⁺ still open → needs stronger stimulus → **limits firing frequency**
>
> **Propagation**
> - **Continuous** (unmyelinated, slow)
> - **Saltatory** (myelinated, fast, jumps between Nodes of Ranvier)
> - Demyelination (MS, GBS) → slowed/blocked conduction
>
> **Clinical & Engineering Relevance**
> - **ECG/EEG/EMG signals** = summed APs
> - **Anesthetics** prolong Na⁺ inactivation → extend ARP
> - **Defibrillators** synchronize chaotic cardiac APs
> - **Neural prosthetics** & **ion-selective electrodes** rely directly on Nernst/GHK principles
>
> **Key Constants to Memorize**
> - $RT/F$ at 37 °C = **26.71 mV**
> - $2.303\,RT/F$ at 37 °C = **61.54 mV**
> - Threshold ≈ **−55 mV**
> - RMP ≈ **−70 mV**
> - Peak AP ≈ **+30 to +40 mV**
> - Hyperpolarization ≈ **−80 mV**
> - Pump ratio: **3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in** per ATP
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation of Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propagation of Action Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Action Potential Propagation** is the sequential, self-regenerating transmission of a transient electrochemical impulse (the action potential) along the excitable membrane of a neuron or muscle fiber. The phenomenon is governed by **cable theory** of biological membranes, where a localized depolarization at one segment acts as a current source that electronically depolarizes the adjacent membrane segment to threshold, triggering a regenerative wave of voltage-gated ion channel activation.

Mathematically, propagation is described by the **one-dimensional cable equation** along the axial coordinate $x$ of the fiber:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = r_i \cdot i_m$$

where $V_m$ is the transmembrane potential, $r_i$ is the intracellular axial resistance per unit length, and $i_m$ is the transmembrane current per unit length. The standard physical constants used in biophysical modelling of propagation are:

- **Membrane capacitance:** $C_m \approx \mathbf{1.0 \; \mu F/cm^{2}}$ (typical squid axon)
- **Resting potential:** $V_{rest} \approx \mathbf{-70 \; mV}$
- **Threshold potential:** $V_{th} \approx \mathbf{-55 \; mV}$
- **Peak Na$^+$ equilibrium:** $V_{Na} \approx \mathbf{+60 \; mV}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1 Syllabus Anchor:** *"Propagation of action potentials – continuous and saltatory conduction, factors affecting conduction velocity, role of myelin and Nodes of Ranvier."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Domino Wave on a Rope"

Imagine a long rope lying on the floor with a row of dominoes standing on it. If you push the first domino:

- **Unmyelinated case (continuous conduction):** Every single domino along the entire rope must fall, and the next domino can only fall AFTER the previous one finishes. This is slow because every centimeter of the membrane must fire.
- **Myelinated case (saltatory conduction):** Now imagine the rope is wrapped in thick insulating foam, with the foam removed every 1–2 mm to leave "gaps" (Nodes of Ranvier). The dominoes only stand at the gaps. When the first one falls, the wave "jumps" from gap to gap, like a person skipping stones on a pond.

In biophysical terms, the **insulating myelin** prevents current leakage, so the local circuit current travels passively and rapidly through the internode, and **regenerative ion exchange occurs only at the nodes**. The result is a 50–100× increase in conduction velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why propagation is unidirectional in vivo:** After the action potential passes a membrane region, Na$^+$ channels enter an *absolute refractory period* (≈ 1–2 ms). This guarantees the impulse travels AWAY from the initiation site (orthodromically) and prevents backflow.

## 1.3 Classification of Propagation Modes

| Mode | Structural Requirement | Velocity Range | Typical Fiber |
|------|----------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Continuous (unmyelinated)** | Bare axolemma with uniform Na$^+$ channel density | **0.5 – 2.5 m/s** | C fibers (pain, autonomic) |
| **Saltatory (myelinated)** | Myelin sheath with periodic Nodes of Ranvier | **5 – 120 m/s** | Aα (motor, proprioception) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sequential Logic Steps of Propagation

Propagation is a **closed-loop feedback system** that repeats iteratively along the axon. The mechanism, broken down for KTU 14-mark answers:

1. **Local Current Injection (Step 1 – Passive Electrotonus):** The action potential at an active region establishes a potential gradient of approximately **+30 mV** (inside-positive) relative to the resting adjacent region. This drives **local circuit currents** – Na$^+$ ions flow intracellularly forward, then leak out across the membrane, returning extracellularly.

2. **Electrotonic Spread to Adjacent Membrane (Step 2 – Cable Properties):** Because the axon behaves as a leaky RC cable, the passive signal decays exponentially with distance. The characteristic length scale is the **space constant $\lambda$**, beyond which the signal would fall below threshold if regeneration did not occur.

3. **Threshold Crossing (Step 3 – Trigger Zone):** When the depolarization at the adjacent patch reaches **$V_{th} \approx -55$ mV**, voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels open cooperatively (Hodgkin–Huxley activation gates $m$ → 1).

4. **Regenerative Upstroke (Step 4 – Positive Feedback):** Na$^+$ influx further depolarizes the membrane, opening MORE Na$^+$ channels. This explosive positive feedback drives $V_m$ toward $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.

5. **Recovery & Wavefront Advancement (Step 5 – Reset):** Na$^+$ channels inactivate (h-gate closes), delayed K$^+$ channels open, and the trailing region **repolarizes**. The active region advances one spatial increment, and Step 1 repeats for the next adjacent segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The conduction velocity is the speed at which this five-step loop propagates. Anything that speeds up Step 1 (better cable) or Step 4 (more Na$^+$ channels) accelerates the impulse.

## 2.2 The Two Biophysical Constants Governing Passive Spread

Before deriving the formulas, we must define two membrane parameters:

- **$r_m$** = membrane resistance per unit length (Ω·cm)
- **$r_i$** = intracellular axial resistance per unit length (Ω/cm)
- **$C_m$** = membrane capacitance per unit length (F/cm)

These combine to give the two fundamental parameters:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}} \quad \text{(Space constant – how far a signal spreads passively)}$$

$$\tau = r_m C_m \quad \text{(Membrane time constant – how fast the membrane charges/discharges)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the table below. The Cable Equation and Rushton's scaling law are the two MOST frequently tested formulas for this module.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Cable Equation (1D)** | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = r_i C_m \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + \dfrac{r_i}{r_m} V_m$ | Governs passive voltage spread | V/cm², V/cm, V |
| 2 | **Space Constant $\lambda$** | $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ | Length of passive signal decay | cm |
| 3 | **Membrane Time Constant $\tau$** | $\tau = r_m \cdot C_m$ | Time for passive charging | s |
| 4 | **Continuous Conduction Velocity** | $v_{cont} \propto \sqrt{a}$ | Unmyelinated fiber (Hodgkin) | m/s |
| 5 | **Saltatory Conduction Velocity** | $v_{salt} \propto a$ | Myelinated fiber (Rushton) | m/s |
| 6 | **Local Circuit Current** | $I_{local} = \dfrac{V_m - V_{rest}}{r_i + r_m}$ | Current spreading passively | A |
| 7 | **Myelin Capacitance Reduction** | $C_{myelin} = \dfrac{C_m}{N_{lamellae}}$ | Effective capacitance per layer | F/cm² |
| 8 | **Refractory-limited minimum interval** | $v_{max} = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t_{refr}}$ | Theoretical upper velocity | m/s |

where $a$ = axon radius (μm) and $d$ = internodal distance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG/EEG Signal Interpretation:** Saltatory conduction determines the latency between neural events recorded at the scalp. Understanding propagation explains waveform morphology in clinical neurophysiology.
- **Neuroprosthetic Design:** Implant electrodes (e.g., cochlear implants, retinal prostheses) must match the chronaxie of the target fiber, which depends directly on $\tau$.
- **Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis:** Demyelination reduces $\lambda$ and forces a fiber to revert from saltatory to continuous conduction – clinical basis for MRI-confirmed plaques.
- **Local Anesthetic Pharmacology:** Lidocaine blocks voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels, terminating propagation. The dose–response curve is a propagation model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Requirement:** Derivations appearing in 14-mark answers MUST be self-contained. Skipping algebraic steps costs 1–2 marks per gap, per the official valuation key.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Space Constant $\lambda$

We start with the steady-state (DC) passive cable equation. For an infinite cylindrical axon with no active currents, the membrane acts as a leaky resistor $r_m$ per unit length, and the core has axial resistance $r_i$ per unit length. Apply Kirchhoff's current law at a membrane element of length $\Delta x$:

$$I(x) - I(x + \Delta x) = I_m \cdot \Delta x$$

Divide by $\Delta x$ and take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$-\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = i_m$$

By Ohm's law along the axoplasm:

$$I(x) = -\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial x}$$

The transmembrane current per unit length is:

$$i_m = \frac{V_m}{r_m}$$

Substitute the second relation into the first:

$$\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{V_m}{r_m}$$

Rearrange to standard form:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{r_i}{r_m} V_m$$

This is a second-order linear ODE. Try a solution of the form $V_m(x) = V_0 e^{-x/\lambda}$. Then:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{V_0}{\lambda^{2}} e^{-x/\lambda}$$

Substitute back:

$$\frac{V_0}{\lambda^{2}} e^{-x/\lambda} = \frac{r_i}{r_m} V_0 e^{-x/\lambda}$$

The exponentials cancel, giving:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{r_i}{r_m}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $\lambda$ is the distance over which the passive voltage decays to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its source value. Increasing $r_m$ (better insulation → myelin) or decreasing $r_i$ (larger axon diameter) BOTH increase $\lambda$, allowing the local circuit current to "reach" further and trigger the next node.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Membrane Time Constant $\tau$

Consider charging the membrane capacitor through the membrane resistor $r_m$. By definition, the RC time constant for charging a capacitor through a resistor is:

$$\tau = R \cdot C$$

In our distributed cable model, both quantities are per unit length, so:

$$\boxed{\tau = r_m \cdot C_m}$$

**Unit check:**
- $r_m$ has units of Ω·cm (resistance per cm length of axon)
- $C_m$ has units of F·cm (capacitance per cm length of axon)
- Product = Ω·F = seconds ✓

For a typical unmyelinated axon: $r_m \approx 1500 \; \Omega \cdot cm$, $C_m \approx 1 \; \mu F/cm^{2} \cdot \pi d \approx 0.01 \; \mu F/cm$ for a 30 μm fiber.

$$\tau \approx 1500 \times 10^{-8} \approx 15 \; \mu s$$

A larger $\tau$ means the membrane is "sluggish" – it takes longer to charge, slowing propagation. Myelination reduces $C_m$ dramatically (parallel capacitance formula for $N$ myelin layers: $C_{myelin} = C_m / N$), thus reducing $\tau$ and accelerating conduction.

## 3.3 Derivation of Rushton's Law for Saltatory Conduction

Rushton (1951) showed that optimal myelination requires the internodal distance $L$ to scale with axon diameter. The reasoning:

For a passive spread across an internode of length $L$ with myelin resistance $R_M$ and axoplasm resistance $R_i$:

$$\frac{V_{end}}{V_{start}} = \frac{1}{\cosh(L / \lambda_{myelin})}$$

For the impulse to cross with sufficient amplitude (typically requiring $L / \lambda \le 1$), and using $\lambda_{myelin} \propto \sqrt{a}$ (since $r_m$ scales as $1/a$ and $r_i$ scales as $1/a^2$, so $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m/r_i} \propto a$):

$$L_{opt} \propto \lambda \propto a$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{\frac{L_{internode}}{a} = \text{constant} \approx 100}$$

Empirically, the ratio $L/a$ is conserved at roughly **100:1** across vertebrate species – a beautiful example of evolutionary optimization.

Combining with cable theory, Rushton showed conduction velocity in myelinated fibers scales LINEARLY with diameter:

$$v_{salt} = K \cdot a$$

where $K \approx 6$ m/s per μm for peripheral myelinated fibers. For a 10 μm motor axon: $v \approx 60$ m/s ✓ (matches measured physiological data).

## 3.4 Comparison: Continuous vs Saltatory Velocity

Combining the formulas for both cases:

$$\frac{v_{salt}}{v_{cont}} = \frac{K \cdot a}{K' \cdot \sqrt{a}} = C \cdot \sqrt{a}$$

For a 10 μm fiber, this ratio is approximately **50–100×**, fully consistent with observed physiological data (C fibers at 1 m/s vs Aα fibers at 100 m/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix for KTU Numerical Problems:** Typical exam problems give $a$ (radius), $\rho_i$ (axoplasm resistivity), $C_m$, and ask for $v$. The chain of substitutions is:
> 1. Compute $r_i = 4 \rho_i / (\pi d^{2})$
> 2. Compute $r_m = 1 / (2 \pi a \cdot g_m)$
> 3. Compute $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$
> 4. Compute $v$ using Rushton or Hodgkin formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use the *Alpha Rule* (letter-prefixed, alphanumeric, no reserved keywords). Labels contain raw text only – no markdown bold or italics inside the quoted strings.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Action Potential Propagation

```mermaid
graph LR
    startNode["Stimulus at Trigger Zone"]
    startNode --> step1["Local Active Region Depolarizes to Plus 30 mV"]
    step1 --> step2["Local Circuit Current Flows in Axoplasm"]
    step2 --> step3{"Myelinated Fiber"}
    step3 -->|Yes| myelinPath["Current Jumps Across Internode"]
    step3 -->|No| noMyelinPath["Current Decays Continuously"]
    myelinPath --> regenCheck1{"Adjacent Node Reaches Threshold Minus 55 mV"}
    noMyelinPath --> regenCheck2{"Adjacent Membrane Reaches Threshold Minus 55 mV"}
    regenCheck1 -->|Yes| regen1["Voltage Gated Na Plus Channels Open at Node"]
    regenCheck2 -->|Yes| regen2["Voltage Gated Na Plus Channels Open Locally"]
    regenCheck1 -->|No| fail1["Signal Fails to Propagate"]
    regenCheck2 -->|No| fail2["Signal Fails to Propagate"]
    regen1 --> repol1["Na Channels Inactivate and K Channels Open"]
    regen2 --> repol2["Na Channels Inactivate and K Channels Open"]
    repol1 --> refractory1["Absolute Refractory Period Prevents Backflow"]
    repol2 --> refractory2["Absolute Refractory Period Prevents Backflow"]
    refractory1 --> advance1["Wavefront Advances to Next Node"]
    refractory2 --> advance2["Wavefront Advances to Next Adjacent Region"]
    advance1 --> step2
    advance2 --> step2
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Comparison: Continuous vs Saltatory Conduction

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph unmyelinatedBlock["UNMYELINATED AXON BLOCK"]
        umem1["Uniform Membrane Segment 1"]
        umem2["Uniform Membrane Segment 2"]
        umem3["Uniform Membrane Segment 3"]
        umem4["Uniform Membrane Segment 4"]
        uarrow1["Active"]
        uarrow2["Resting"]
        uarrow3["Refractory"]
        uarrow4["Future"]
        umem1 --- uarrow1
        umem2 --- uarrow2
        umem3 --- uarrow3
        umem4 --- uarrow4
    end
    subgraph myelinatedBlock["MYELINATED AXON BLOCK"]
        nodeA["Node of Ranvier A"]
        myelinA["Myelin Internode A"]
        nodeB["Node of Ranvier B"]
        myelinB["Myelin Internode B"]
        nodeC["Node of Ranvier C"]
        myelinC["Myelin Internode C"]
        nodeD["Node of Ranvier D"]
        sarrow1["Active"]
        sarrow2["Resting"]
        sarrow3["Future"]
        sarrow4["Refractory"]
        nodeA --- sarrow1
        nodeB --- sarrow2
        nodeC --- sarrow3
        nodeD --- sarrow4
    end
    unmyelinatedBlock -. "Slow Continuous Wave" .-> myelinatedBlock
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of Factors Affecting Conduction Velocity

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputAxis["Axon Diameter a"]
    inputMyelin["Myelin Thickness and Internode Length"]
    inputTemp["Temperature Q10 Effect"]
    inputChan["Na Plus Channel Density"]
    inputLoad["Membrane Capacitance Cm"]
    outputVel["Conduction Velocity v in m per s"]
    inputAxis --> rule1["v proportional to sqrt a in unmyelinated case"]
    inputAxis --> rule2["v proportional to a in myelinated case"]
    inputMyelin --> rule3["L internode ratio to a equals 100 optimum"]
    inputTemp --> rule4["Q10 approximately 1.5 to 2.0 for nerve"]
    inputChan --> rule5["Higher density increases excitability and v"]
    inputLoad --> rule6["Larger Cm increases tau and slows passive spread"]
    rule1 --> outputVel
    rule2 --> outputVel
    rule3 --> outputVel
    rule4 --> outputVel
    rule5 --> outputVel
    rule6 --> outputVel
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder:** KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) for OEECT613 follows a 3-mark short-answer pattern (Part A) and a 14-mark long-answer with internal choice pattern (Part B). All questions below are mapped to the **Module 1** outcome: *"Apply cable theory and ion channel kinetics to predict action potential propagation in nerve fibers."*

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define saltatory conduction. Why is it faster than continuous conduction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Saltatory conduction is the mode of action potential propagation in myelinated nerve fibers in which the regenerative depolarization "jumps" from one Node of Ranvier to the next across the insulated internodal segments, rather than spreading continuously along the entire axolemma.

**Mechanism (1 mark):** At the nodes, voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels are clustered at high density (~10× higher than unmyelinated). The myelin sheath acts as a high-resistance, low-capacitance insulator, increasing the space constant $\lambda$ and decreasing the time constant $\tau$ of the internodal membrane.

**Why faster (1 mark):** The passive electrotonic spread across the myelin internode is much faster than the active regeneration needed at every micrometer of an unmyelinated axon. As a result, the impulse effectively "skips" internodes, giving conduction velocities of 5–120 m/s versus 0.5–2.5 m/s in unmyelinated fibers – a 50–100× enhancement.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State and explain the significance of the space constant $\lambda$ in nerve conduction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The space constant is defined as

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

where $r_m$ is the membrane resistance per unit length and $r_i$ is the intracellular axial resistance per unit length. It has units of length (cm or mm).

**Significance – quantitative (1 mark):** $\lambda$ is the distance over which a passive, sub-threshold voltage change decays to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its original value. Mathematically, $V(x) = V_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}$. For a typical unmyelinated axon, $\lambda \approx 1$ mm; for a myelinated internode, $\lambda$ can be several mm.

**Significance – physiological (1 mark):** For the action potential to propagate, the local circuit current must depolarize the adjacent membrane to threshold BEFORE the signal decays too far. Hence $\lambda$ effectively sets the maximum distance over which an action potential can "jump" passively before requiring regeneration. A larger $\lambda$ (achieved by myelination or increased axon diameter) allows faster, longer-range propagation per active site.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**(a) With neat block diagrams, explain the mechanism of continuous conduction of an action potential in an unmyelinated nerve fiber. Define and derive the cable equation. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Mechanism of continuous conduction (3 marks):**

In an unmyelinated axon, voltage-gated Na$^+$ and K$^+$ channels are distributed uniformly along the entire axolemma at moderate density (~100/μm²). When the membrane at point $x_0$ depolarizes to threshold:
1. Na$^+$ channels open, producing a rapid upstroke to $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.
2. The depolarized region becomes a current sink: positive charge flows intracellularly forward, exits across the adjacent membrane, and returns extracellularly.
3. The local circuit current depolarizes the adjacent region to threshold, repeating the cycle.
4. K$^+$ efflux repolarizes the trailing region, while Na$^+$ channel inactivation enforces unidirectional (orthodromic) propagation.

**Step 2 – Cable equation derivation (4 marks):** [See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes]

The steady-state form is:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{r_i}{r_m} V_m$$

For time-dependent cases, including membrane capacitance, the full cable equation is:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = r_i C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + \frac{r_i}{r_m} V_m$$

The solution is a damped wave, with the velocity determined by the balance of cable properties and active ion channel kinetics.

**(b) Define the membrane time constant $\tau$ and the space constant $\lambda$. An unmyelinated axon has $r_m = 1500 \; \Omega \cdot cm$, $r_i = 100 \; \Omega/cm$. Compute $\lambda$ and comment on what it means for propagation. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of $\lambda$ (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

**Definition of $\tau$ (1 mark):** $\tau = r_m \cdot C_m$ is the time required for the passive membrane potential to decay to $1/e$ of its peak following a brief current pulse.

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{1500 \; \Omega \cdot cm}{100 \; \Omega/cm}} = \sqrt{15 \; cm^{2}} \approx 3.87 \; cm$$

**Commentary (2 marks):** With $\lambda \approx 3.87$ cm, the passive signal can spread almost 4 cm before decaying to 1/e. However, in reality, the active region only needs to depolarize the **next few hundred micrometers** to threshold, so a relatively short distance is regenerative. Larger $\lambda$ values would make the fiber MORE excitable but do not, by themselves, determine velocity – velocity is limited by the kinetics of Na$^+$ channel activation and membrane capacitance, not by $\lambda$ alone.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]`  *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) Explain the structure and function of the myelin sheath and Nodes of Ranvier. How does saltatory conduction differ from continuous conduction in terms of (i) ion channel distribution, (ii) current flow pattern, and (iii) velocity? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Structure and function (2 marks):**
- **Myelin sheath:** A multilamellar wrapping of oligodendrocyte (CNS) or Schwann cell (PNS) plasma membrane around the axon. Provides both electrical insulation (high $r_m$) and reduction of capacitance ($C_{eff} = C_m / N$).
- **Nodes of Ranvier:** Periodic gaps (~1 μm long, spaced every 0.5–2 mm) where the axolemma is exposed and densely packed with voltage-gated Na$^+$ channels (~1000–2000/μm², ~10× higher than unmyelinated membrane).

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Continuous | Saltatory |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **(i) Na$^+$ channel distribution** | Uniform, low density (~100/μm²) | Clustered at nodes only (~1500/μm²) |
| **(ii) Current flow** | Passive + active at EVERY μm | Passive through internode; active ONLY at nodes |
| **(iii) Conduction velocity** | $v \propto \sqrt{a}$, 0.5–2.5 m/s | $v \propto a$, 5–120 m/s |

**Physiological consequence (2 marks):** Saltatory conduction conserves metabolic energy (less Na$^+$/K$^+$-ATPase activity needed per unit length) and dramatically reduces signal delay, enabling rapid reflexes and high-fidelity sensory transmission.

**(b) State Rushton's principle for optimal myelination. A myelinated axon has diameter $a = 10 \; \mu m$. The Rushton constant $K = 6 \; \text{m/s per }\mu\text{m}$. Calculate the expected conduction velocity. Comment on the clinical relevance if this axon becomes demyelinated. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Rushton's principle (2 marks):** For optimal saltatory conduction, the internodal distance $L$ should be proportional to the axon diameter $a$, with a constant ratio $L/a \approx 100$. This ensures the passive signal just reaches threshold at the next node. Conduction velocity in a myelinated fiber is:

$$v_{salt} = K \cdot a$$

**Computation (2 marks):** Given $a = 10 \; \mu m$ and $K = 6 \; \text{m/s per }\mu\text{m}$:

$$v_{salt} = 6 \times 10 = 60 \; m/s$$

This matches observed conduction velocities in Aα motor fibers.

**Clinical relevance of demyelination (3 marks):** When the myelin sheath is destroyed (e.g., multiple sclerosis, Guillain–Barré syndrome), the axon effectively reverts to continuous conduction. The new velocity, using $v \propto \sqrt{a}$, would be approximately $v_{cont} \approx K' \sqrt{a}$ with $K' \approx 1.5$ m·s$^{-1}$·μm$^{-1/2}$:

$$v_{cont} \approx 1.5 \times \sqrt{10} \approx 4.7 \; m/s$$

This represents a **~13× slowing** of nerve conduction, producing clinical symptoms such as:
- Motor weakness
- Sensory loss
- Delayed reflexes
- Optic neuritis (in MS)
- Autonomic dysfunction

The slowed/blocked conduction is the basis for clinical neurophysiology tests (nerve conduction studies, visual evoked potentials).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Verified Against KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "membrane resistance per unit length" $r_m$ with "membrane resistance density" $R_m$.** These differ by a factor of $2\pi a$ (the circumference). Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute refractory period in unidirectional propagation answers.** Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating that the h-gate inactivation prevents backflow.
> 3. **Writing $\lambda = \sqrt{r_i/r_m}$ instead of $\sqrt{r_m/r_i}$.** This is the most common algebraic inversion. Memorize the formula as it appears in Section 2.3.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_m$ per cm² vs per cm length.** The membrane capacitance per unit length is $C_m \cdot 2\pi a$, which affects $\tau$.
> 5. **Stating "myelin makes conduction faster" without quantifying.** Always give numbers: 50–100× faster, 5–120 m/s vs 0.5–2.5 m/s.
> 6. **In derivation questions, skipping the substitution step where $V_0 e^{-x/\lambda}$ is plugged back into the ODE.** Show this explicitly to earn all 4 derivation marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist – Module 1, Propagation of Action Potentials:**

- ✅ **Two conduction modes:** Continuous (unmyelinated) and Saltatory (myelinated).
- ✅ **Space constant formula:** $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ – higher with myelin or larger diameter.
- ✅ **Time constant formula:** $\tau = r_m \cdot C_m$ – lower with myelin (reduced $C_m$).
- ✅ **Continuous velocity scaling:** $v \propto \sqrt{a}$ (Hodgkin–Huxley for unmyelinated).
- ✅ **Saltatory velocity scaling:** $v \propto a$ (Rushton for myelinated).
- ✅ **Rushton ratio:** $L_{internode}/a \approx 100$ (conserved across vertebrates).
- ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** $V_{rest} = -70$ mV, $V_{th} = -55$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.
- ✅ **Myelin function:** Increases $r_m$, decreases $C_m$ → larger $\lambda$, smaller $\tau$, faster propagation.
- ✅ **Na$^+$ channel density:** ~10× higher at Nodes of Ranvier than in unmyelinated membrane.
- ✅ **Unidirectional propagation mechanism:** Absolute refractory period (~1–2 ms) prevents backflow.
- ✅ **Clinical correlate:** Multiple sclerosis = demyelination = continuous conduction mode reverts → slowed/blocked nerve conduction.
- ✅ **Rushton constant K:** $K \approx 6$ m/s per μm for myelinated fibers; $K' \approx 1.5$ m·s$^{-1}$·μm$^{-1/2}$ for unmyelinated.
- ✅ **Cable equation form (full):** $\dfrac{\partial^{2} V_m}{\partial x^{2}} = r_i C_m \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + \dfrac{r_i}{r_m} V_m$.
- ✅ **Temperature effect:** $Q_{10} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ for mammalian nerve conduction.
- ✅ **Exam rule:** Always show substitutions, never skip algebra, and end with a boxed answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Bioelectric Potentials (ECG, EEG Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Bioelectric Potentials (ECG, EEG Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Module 1: Various Bioelectric Potentials (ECG & EEG)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Bioelectric Potential?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Bioelectric Potential** is the electrical potential difference (voltage) generated across the membrane of an excitable living cell (such as a nerve, muscle, or gland cell) due to the differential distribution of ions (primarily Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, and Ca²⁺) across the cell membrane, combined with the selective permeability properties of the membrane and the active ion-pumping action of proteins like the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump.

In simpler terms, the human body is fundamentally an **electrochemical machine**. Every thought you think, every beat of your heart, and every signal your nerves send involves moving tiny charged particles (ions) across cell walls. This movement creates tiny voltages that can be measured from the surface of the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Typical bioelectric potentials range from **microvolts (µV)** — for brain waves (EEG) — to **millivolts (mV)** — for heart signals (ECG) and nerve action potentials.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Body as a Battery City

Imagine the human body as a vast metropolis of tiny batteries:

- **The Cell Membrane = A Wall with Turnstiles:** Some turnstiles let only sodium (Na⁺) through, others let only potassium (K⁺) through, and some are one-way revolving doors (pumps).
- **The Cytoplasm = A Swimming Pool:** Filled with positive and negative ions in different concentrations on either side of the wall.
- **The Voltage = Water Pressure Difference:** Just as a dam generates electricity from water pressure, the chemical pressure difference between inside and outside the cell generates a voltage.

When a cell is **at rest**, this battery is charged and stable. When **stimulated**, the turnstiles open in a wave (like dominoes falling), and this wave of electrical activity is what we measure as an ECG, EEG, EMG, or EOG signal.

---

### 1.3 The Two Foundational Bioelectric Signals (Concept Only — KTU Module 1 Scope)

| Signal | Full Name | Source Organ | Typical Amplitude | Typical Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** | Electrocardiogram | Heart (Cardiac muscle) | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** |
| **EEG** | Electroencephalogram | Brain (Cerebral cortex) | **10 – 100 µV** | **0.1 – 100 Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECG measures the electrical activity of the heart**, while **EEG measures the electrical activity of the brain.** Both are non-invasive measurements taken from the surface of the skin/scalp.

### 1.4 The Cardiac Dipole Theory (Foundation of ECG)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Heart as an Electric Dipole:**
> At any instant during the cardiac cycle, the millions of cardiac muscle cells that are currently depolarizing act together as a single **electric dipole** (a positive pole and a negative pole separated by a small distance). The vector sum of all these tiny dipoles produces one large, measurable **cardiac vector** that changes direction and magnitude with time. The ECG machine simply records the projection of this vector onto the skin surface electrodes.

### 1.5 The Cortical Dipole Theory (Foundation of EEG)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Brain as Synchronized Dipoles:**
> The outer layer of the brain, the **cerebral cortex**, contains billions of neurons (pyramidal cells) that are arranged in a parallel, orderly fashion. When these neurons fire synchronously (in unison), their tiny dipoles add up to produce a measurable voltage on the scalp. The EEG captures this summed rhythmic activity. Different mental states (awake, asleep, dreaming) produce characteristic rhythmic patterns.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Genesis of Bioelectric Potentials — Three Underlying Principles

### Principle 1: Ionic Concentration Gradients

Living cells maintain a **non-uniform distribution** of ions across their membranes. The table below shows typical resting concentrations:

| Ion | Intracellular (mM) | Extracellular (mM) | Gradient Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K⁺ (Potassium)** | ~140 | ~4 | High Inside |
| **Na⁺ (Sodium)** | ~10 | ~140 | High Outside |
| **Cl⁻ (Chloride)** | ~4 | ~110 | High Outside |
| **Ca²⁺ (Calcium)** | ~0.0001 | ~2 | High Outside |
| **A⁻ (Proteins, etc.)** | High | Low | High Inside |

> The **Nernst Equation** quantifies the equilibrium voltage that one single ion would create if the membrane were permeable only to that ion.

### Principle 2: Selective Membrane Permeability

The lipid bilayer membrane is studded with **ion channels** — specialized proteins that open or close in response to voltage, chemicals, or mechanical stress. At rest, the membrane is **~25× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺**, so the resting potential is dominated by potassium.

### Principle 3: Active Ion Pumping (The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase Pump)

The **sodium-potassium pump** is an active transport protein that consumes ATP (cellular energy) to push **3 Na⁺ out** of the cell and pull **2 K⁺ into** the cell for every cycle. This pump continuously fights against the passive leakage, maintaining the concentration gradients.

---

## 2.2 The Nernst Equation — Single-Ion Equilibrium Potential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Nernst Equation** calculates the equilibrium potential $E$ (in volts) for a single ion species across a membrane, given the concentrations of that ion on each side.

### Mathematical Form

The Nernst Equation is:

$$E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$

Where the variables and units are:

- $E$ = Nernst (Equilibrium) potential, in **Volts (V)**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature, in **Kelvin (K)**
- $z$ = Valence (charge number) of the ion (e.g., +1 for K⁺, +2 for Ca²⁺, -1 for Cl⁻)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $C_{out}$ = Concentration of the ion outside the cell, in **mol/L (M)**
- $C_{in}$ = Concentration of the ion inside the cell, in **mol/L (M)**
- $\ln$ = Natural logarithm (base $e$)

### Converting to Base-10 Logarithm

> [!NOTE]
> At body temperature ($T = 310$ K, or 37°C), the factor $\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}$ evaluates to approximately **0.0615 V (61.5 mV)**. This is a constant examiners love to test.

Using base-10 log:

$$E = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$

$$E \approx \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used in **biosensor design** to predict resting potentials of tissues.
- Forms the theoretical foundation for designing **microelectrode arrays** that measure intracellular potentials.
- Critical for understanding **pace-maker battery voltage compatibility** with cardiac cell thresholds.

---

## 2.3 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation — Multi-Ion Membrane Potential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Goldman Equation** is a more realistic model that calculates the membrane potential when the membrane is permeable to **multiple ions** simultaneously. It is the weighted average of the Nernst potentials, with permeability as the weight.

### Mathematical Form

$$V_{m} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left( \frac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{out}} \right)$$

Where the additional variables are:

- $V_m$ = Membrane potential, in **Volts (V)**
- $P_K, P_{Na}, P_{Cl}$ = Permeability coefficients of the membrane to K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻ respectively (in m/s or cm/s)
- $[X]_{in}$ and $[X]_{out}$ = Intracellular and extracellular concentrations of ion $X$

### Typical Resting Potential

For a typical nerve cell, $V_m \approx \mathbf{-70 \text{ mV}}$ (the inside is negative relative to outside).

### The Action Potential — Brief 4-Stage Summary

1. **Resting State:** $V_m \approx -70$ mV (K⁺ channels mainly open).
2. **Depolarization:** Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open; Na⁺ rushes in; $V_m$ rises to ~+30 mV.
3. **Repolarization:** Na⁺ channels inactivate; voltage-gated K⁺ channels open; K⁺ rushes out; $V_m$ returns to negative values.
4. **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot):** K⁺ channels close slowly; $V_m$ briefly drops to ~-80 mV before returning to rest.

---

## 2.4 The ECG — Lead Theory and Einthoven's Triangle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Willem Einthoven** (1903 Nobel Prize) developed the modern ECG. He placed electrodes on the **right arm (RA), left arm (LA), and left leg (LL)** to form an **equilateral triangle** — now called **Einthoven's Triangle** — with the heart at its electrical center.

### The Three Bipolar Limb Leads

| Lead | Electrode Configuration | Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead I** | LA (+) − RA (−) | Electrical activity in the horizontal plane (left vs right arm) |
| **Lead II** | LL (+) − RA (−) | Aligns with the heart's long axis (most commonly used clinically) |
| **Lead III** | LL (+) − LA (−) | Completes the triangle |

### Einthoven's Law (A KTU High-Yield Formula)

> [!NOTE]
> **Einthoven's Law** states that the voltage recorded in Lead II equals the sum of the voltages recorded in Lead I and Lead III.

$$V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$$

This is **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the body as a closed loop. It is a critical formula for KTU board exams.

### The Six Augmented Unipolar Leads (Goldberger)

These are reference-point leads that "augment" (magnify) the signal by ~50%:

- **aVR** = Augmented Vector Right (looks at the heart from the right shoulder)
- **aVL** = Augmented Vector Left (looks at the heart from the left shoulder)
- **aVF** = Augmented Vector Foot (looks at the heart from below)

Together, the 3 bipolar + 3 augmented = **6 limb leads**, giving a view of the heart in the **frontal plane**.

### The Six Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson)

- **V1, V2:** Right side of the heart (septal and right ventricular)
- **V3, V4:** Anterior wall of the left ventricle
- **V5, V6:** Lateral wall of the left ventricle

These 6 chest leads provide a view of the heart in the **horizontal (transverse) plane**.

### Total Leads in a Standard 12-Lead ECG

$$3 \text{ Bipolar} + 3 \text{ Augmented} + 6 \text{ Precordial} = \mathbf{12 \text{ Leads}}$$

---

## 2.5 The ECG Waveform — P, QRS, T (and the Hidden U)

A single cardiac cycle on the ECG trace has 5 named waves:

| Wave | Represents | Direction | Duration | Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P Wave** | Atrial Depolarization (atria contract) | Upright (positive) in Lead II | ~0.08 s (80 ms) | < 0.25 mV |
| **QRS Complex** | Ventricular Depolarization (ventricles contract) | Variable | 0.06 – 0.10 s | 0.5 – 1.5 mV |
| **T Wave** | Ventricular Repolarization (ventricles relax) | Upright, asymmetric | ~0.16 s | < 0.5 mV |
| **(U Wave)** | Papillary muscle repolarization (sometimes seen) | Upright, small | — | < 0.1 mV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **QRS Complex** is the most prominent feature. It contains the **Q wave** (first downward deflection), **R wave** (first upward deflection), and **S wave** (downward deflection after R). Atrial repolarization is hidden inside the QRS complex and is therefore not visible.

### PQ Interval and QT Interval — KTU Must-Know

- **PQ Interval (PR Interval):** Time from start of P wave to start of QRS = **0.12 – 0.20 s**. Represents the AV nodal conduction delay.
- **QRS Duration:** Time for ventricular depolarization = **0.06 – 0.10 s**.
- **QT Interval:** Total time for ventricular depolarization + repolarization = **0.32 – 0.44 s**. Varies with heart rate.
- **RR Interval:** Time between two consecutive R peaks. Heart Rate (bpm) = **60 / RR (in seconds)**.

### Heart Rate Calculation (KTU Classic Formula)

> [!NOTE]
> $$\text{Heart Rate (bpm)} = \frac{60}{\text{R-R interval in seconds}} = \frac{1500}{\text{R-R interval in mm at 25 mm/s paper speed}}$$

At standard ECG paper speed of **25 mm/s** and amplitude calibration of **10 mm/mV**:
- 1 small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s
- 1 large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s

---

## 2.6 The EEG — Brain Rhythms and Frequency Bands

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EEG records the summed, synchronous postsynaptic potentials of billions of cortical pyramidal neurons. Unlike ECG, EEG rhythms are **continuous oscillations** rather than discrete events.

### The Five Standard EEG Frequency Bands (KTU High-Yield)

| Band | Frequency Range | Amplitude | Associated State / Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | High (20–200 µV) | Deep (Slow-Wave) Sleep, Infants, Pathological coma |
| **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Moderate (5–100 µV) | Drowsiness, Light Sleep, Emotional Stress, Creativity, Children |
| **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Moderate (20–60 µV) | Awake, Relaxed, Eyes Closed, Calm, Idling Brain |
| **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Low (5–20 µV) | Active Thinking, Alertness, Concentration, Anxiety, Problem Solving |
| **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** (up to 100 Hz) | Very Low (≤ 5 µV) | High-Level Cognitive Processing, Sensory Binding, Peak Focus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alpha Rhythm Specifics:** First described by **Hans Berger** in 1929 (the inventor of the EEG). It is most prominent over the **occipital region** (back of the head) when the subject is awake, relaxed, and has eyes closed. Opening the eyes causes **alpha blocking** (also called the Berger effect) — the alpha rhythm is replaced by desynchronized beta activity.

### The 10–20 International Electrode Placement System (KTU Important)

The 10-20 system is the standard for placing EEG electrodes on the scalp. The numbers **10% and 20%** refer to distances that are 10% or 20% of the total front-back or right-left distance of the skull.

Electrode names follow the pattern: **[Letter(s) of region][Number]**, where odd numbers are on the **left** and even numbers on the **right**:

| Letter Code | Region |
|---|---|
| **Fp** | Fronto-polar |
| **F** | Frontal |
| **C** | Central |
| **P** | Parietal |
| **O** | Occipital |
| **T** | Temporal |
| **A** | Auricular (ear reference) |
| **Fz, Cz, Pz** | Midline electrodes (z = zero) |

> The reference (ground) electrodes are typically placed on the **earlobes (A1, A2)**.

### The Bipolar and Referential Montages

- **Bipolar Montage:** Each channel measures the voltage difference between **two adjacent scalp electrodes** (e.g., Fp1–F3).
- **Referential (Monopolar) Montage:** Each scalp electrode is measured against a **common reference electrode** (e.g., A1+A2 linked).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All Key Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — these are the formulas most frequently asked in KTU board exams.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst Equation | Single-ion equilibrium potential |
| 2 | $E \approx \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst at 37°C | Quick calculation at body temperature |
| 3 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left( \frac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{out}} \right)$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation | Realistic resting membrane potential |
| 4 | $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ | Einthoven's Law | ECG lead validation |
| 5 | $\text{Heart Rate (bpm)} = \frac{60}{\text{R-R (s)}} = \frac{1500}{\text{R-R (mm)}}$ | Heart Rate from ECG | Cardiac monitoring |
| 6 | $\text{QT}_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | Bazett's Formula | Heart-rate corrected QT interval |
| 7 | $\text{Power}_{band} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} P(f) \, df$ | EEG Band Power | Quantitative EEG (qEEG) analysis |
| 8 | $\text{Electrode Impedance} = \frac{V}{I}$ at 10 Hz | Impedance Check | EEG/ECG electrode quality test |

### Engineering Utility Beyond Medicine

- **ECG signal processing** algorithms power wearable fitness bands (Apple Watch, Fitbit).
- **EEG-based Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** are used in prosthetics, gaming, and assistive devices.
- **Nernst/Goldman equations** guide design of **ISFET (Ion-Sensitive Field-Effect Transistor)** biosensors.
- **Heart rate variability (HRV)** extracted from R-R intervals is used in **driver drowsiness detection** systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1: Nernst Potential for Potassium Ion (K⁺)**

This is a classic KTU numerical problem. Given the intracellular and extracellular K⁺ concentrations, calculate the K⁺ equilibrium potential at body temperature (37°C = 310 K).

**Given Data:**
- $[K^{+}]_{in} = 140$ mM (intracellular)
- $[K^{+}]_{out} = 4$ mM (extracellular)
- $T = 310$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $z = +1$ for K⁺

**Step 1: Recall the Nernst Equation**

$$E_{K^{+}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[K^{+}]_{out}}{[K^{+}]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Plug in all values into the equation**

$$E_{K^{+}} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(+1)(96{,}485)} \ln\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately**

$$E_{K^{+}} = \frac{2577.34}{96{,}485} \ln\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$

$$E_{K^{+}} = (0.02671) \ln(0.02857)$$

**Step 4: Compute the natural logarithm**

$$\ln(0.02857) = -3.555$$

**Step 5: Multiply the coefficients**

$$E_{K^{+}} = (0.02671) \times (-3.555)$$

$$E_{K^{+}} = -0.09495 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Convert to millivolts**

$$E_{K^{+}} \approx -94.95 \text{ mV} \approx \mathbf{-95 \text{ mV}}$$

**Biological Interpretation:** This means if the membrane were permeable **only to K⁺**, the inside of the cell would be ~95 mV more negative than the outside. The actual resting potential of ~-70 mV is less negative because the membrane is slightly permeable to Na⁺ as well, which pushes the potential up (less negative).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2: Nernst Potential for Calcium Ion (Ca²⁺)**

**Given Data:**
- $[Ca^{2+}]_{in} = 0.0001$ mM
- $[Ca^{2+}]_{out} = 2$ mM
- $T = 310$ K
- $z = +2$ for Ca²⁺

**Step 1: Apply the Nernst Equation**

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_{out}}{[Ca^{2+}]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Plug in the values**

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(+2)(96{,}485)} \ln\left(\frac{2}{0.0001}\right)$$

**Step 3: Simplify the constant**

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = \frac{2577.34}{192{,}970} \ln(20{,}000)$$

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = (0.01336) \ln(20{,}000)$$

**Step 4: Compute the natural logarithm**

$$\ln(20{,}000) = 9.903$$

**Step 5: Multiply the coefficients**

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = (0.01336) \times (9.903)$$

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} = 0.1323 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Convert to millivolts**

$$E_{Ca^{2+}} \approx +132.3 \text{ mV}$$

**Biological Interpretation:** The huge equilibrium potential for Ca²⁺ is due to its **enormous concentration gradient** (20,000× higher outside than inside). This steep gradient is the reason Ca²⁺ is such a powerful **trigger for neurotransmitter release, muscle contraction, and second-messenger signaling**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3: Heart Rate Calculation from ECG**

A patient has an ECG trace recorded at standard paper speed of 25 mm/s. The R-R interval is measured to be **25 small boxes**. Calculate the heart rate.

**Step 1: Recall the time duration of one small box**

At 25 mm/s paper speed:

$$1 \text{ small box} = 1 \text{ mm} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the R-R interval in seconds**

$$\text{R-R interval} = 25 \text{ boxes} \times 0.04 \text{ s/box} = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the heart rate formula**

$$\text{Heart Rate (bpm)} = \frac{60}{\text{R-R (s)}}$$

$$\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{60}{1.0} = 60 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 4: Cross-check using the 1500 method**

$$\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{1500}{\text{R-R (mm)}} = \frac{1500}{25 \text{ mm}} = 60 \text{ bpm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Biological Interpretation:** A heart rate of 60 bpm is the lower bound of the normal adult resting range (60–100 bpm), indicating **sinus bradycardia** (or normal for an athletic person).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 4: Verification of Einthoven's Law**

Given the following ECG voltage measurements in a normal subject:

$$V_I = +1.0 \text{ mV}, \quad V_{II} = +1.5 \text{ mV}, \quad V_{III} = +0.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 1: Apply Einthoven's Law**

$$V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$V_I + V_{III} = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_{II} = 1.5 \text{ mV} \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification:** Einthoven's Law is satisfied. If $V_{II} \neq V_I + V_{III}$ in real measurements, the discrepancy indicates either measurement error, electrode placement problem, or (rarely) a cardiac anomaly.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 5: Determining the Unknown Ion Concentration Using the Nernst Equation**

The Nernst potential for an unknown monovalent cation is measured to be **+58 mV** at 37°C. The extracellular concentration is **150 mM**, and the equilibrium potential is positive (the ion is higher inside than outside). Find the intracellular concentration.

**Step 1: Rearrange the Nernst Equation for $C_{in}$**

$$E = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$

$$C_{in} = C_{out} \times 10^{\left(-\frac{E \cdot z}{61.5}\right)}$$

**Step 2: Plug in the known values**

$$C_{in} = 150 \times 10^{\left(-\frac{(+58)(+1)}{61.5}\right)}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the exponent**

$$C_{in} = 150 \times 10^{(-0.943)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $10^{-0.943}$**

$$10^{-0.943} = 0.1141$$

**Step 5: Final calculation**

$$C_{in} = 150 \times 0.1141 = 17.12 \text{ mM}$$

**Biological Interpretation:** This value (~17 mM inside, 150 mM outside, with positive equilibrium potential) is consistent with the **sodium ion (Na⁺)** distribution. The calculation demonstrates the reverse-engineering utility of the Nernst equation in **ion-sensitive biosensor calibration**.

---

## 3.1 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix: ECG vs EEG

| Parameter | **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** | **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Source** | Cardiac muscle (SA node → AV node → His-Purkinje) | Cerebral cortex (pyramidal neurons) |
| **Typical Amplitude** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **10 – 100 µV** (1000× smaller than ECG) |
| **Typical Frequency** | 0.05 – 150 Hz (clinical: 0.5 – 40 Hz) | 0.1 – 100 Hz (clinical: 0.5 – 30 Hz) |
