Its output is: $$V_{out} = V_{o1} - V_{o2}$$ Substituting Step 8: $$\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)}$$ Therefore, the **overall differential gain** is: $$\boxed{A_D = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of CMRR for Resistor Mismatch in the Output Bridge > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** In a real IA, R2 and R3 in the output bridge are not exactly equal. Let the actual values be R₂(1 + ε) and R₃(1 − ε), where ε ≪ 1 represents the fractional mismatch. ### Step 1 — Common-Mode Gain of Mismatched Bridge The common-mode gain of the difference amplifier stage is given by: $$A_{CM,\text{bridge}} = \dfrac{R_3(1 - \varepsilon)}{R_2(1 + \varepsilon) + R_3(1 - \varepsilon)} - \text{(balanced terms)}$$ The full first-order expansion yields: $$A_{CM} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot \dfrac{2\varepsilon}{1 + R_3/R_2} \approx \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$ For the matched case where R₂ = R₃, this simplifies to: $$A_{CM} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{2}$$ ### Step 2 — Effective CMRR The overall CMRR is then: $$\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{A_D}{\vert A_{CM} \vert} = \dfrac{1 + 2R_1/R_g}{\varepsilon / 2} = \dfrac{2(1 + 2R_1/R_g)}{\varepsilon}$$ Converting to decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{2(1 + 2R_1/R_g)}{\varepsilon}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Given:** R₁ = 25 kΩ, Rg = 1 kΩ, resistor tolerance ε = 0.001 (0.1%). > **Find:** The CMRR in dB. **Solution:** First, compute the differential gain: $$A_D = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 25{,}000}{1{,}000} = 1 + 50 = 51$$ Then the CMRR ratio: $$\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{2 \times 51}{0.001} = 102{,}000$$ In decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(102{,}000) = 20 \times 5.0086 = 100.17 \text{ dB}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the −3 dB Bandwidth vs. Closed-Loop Gain > [!NOTE] > **Theory:** Every op-amp has a fixed **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW or f_T)**. As closed-loop gain A_CL rises, the usable bandwidth shrinks. For a single-pole op-amp model with open-loop gain A_OL(s) = A_0 / (1 + s/ω₁), the closed-loop −3 dB frequency is: $$f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{f_T}{A_{CL}} = \dfrac{f_T}{1 + 2R_1/R_g}$$ For an INA with f_T = **10 MHz** (typical for AD620) and A_D = **51**: $$f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{51} \approx 196 \text{ kHz}$$ This is far above the ECG bandwidth (150 Hz), so the IA is *not* the bandwidth bottleneck — the **anti-aliasing filter** is. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Table) | Feature | 1-Op-Amp Difference Amp | Instrumentation Amplifier | Isolation Amplifier | |---|---|---|---| | **Differential Gain Formula** | $A_D = R_2/R_1$ | $A_D = 1 + 2R_1/R_g$ | $A_D = $ Set by IA + iso | | **Input Impedance** | Medium (≈ R₁) | **Very High** (> 1 GΩ) | Very High | | **CMRR** | Limited by R-matching | **Very High** (> 100 dB) | High + isolation barrier | | **Adjust Gain w/o Breaking CMRR** | **No** (changes both) | **Yes** (single Rg) | Yes | | **Galvanic Isolation** | No | No | **Yes (4000 V)** | | **Power-Line Rejection** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | | **Cost / Complexity** | Low | Medium | **High** | | **Application** | Lab, non-medical | **ECG/EEG/EMG front-end** | **Patient-connected monitor** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Op-Amp IA in an ECG Front-End The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **signal flow** from electrodes through the IA, isolation barrier, ADC, and microcontroller in a typical KTU-referenced biomedical acquisition chain. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[RA Electrode
Right Arm] A2[LA Electrode
Left Arm] A3[RL Electrode
Right Leg Drive] B1[IA Stage 1
Buffer A1 High Zin] B2[IA Stage 2
Buffer A2 High Zin] B3[Rg Gain Resistor
Sets Differential Gain] C1[IA Stage 3
Difference Amp A3] D1[REF Level Shift
Mid Supply 2.5V] E1[Anti-Alias Filter
2nd Order LPF 150 Hz] F1[Isolation Barrier
ISO124 or AD215] G1[ADC 16 bit
ADS129x Series] H1[Microcontroller
ARM Cortex M4] I1[Display Wifi
Patient Monitor] J1[DRL Op Amp
Common Mode Feedback] A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 B1 --- B3 B2 --- B3 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 C1 --> D1 D1 --> E1 E1 --> F1 F1 --> G1 G1 --> H1 H1 --> I1 J1 -.-> A3 J1 -. C1 ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (3-Op-Amp Topology) ```mermaid flowchart TB IN1((V1 Electrode +)) IN2((V2 Electrode -)) Rg[Rg Gain Resistor] R1A[R1 Buffer A1] R1B[R1 Buffer A2] R2A[R2 Bridge Top] R2B[R2 Bridge Bottom] R3A[R3 Bridge Top] R3B[R3 Bridge Bottom] A1OP[Op-Amp A1
Buffer Stage] A2OP[Op-Amp A2
Buffer Stage] A3OP[Op-Amp A3
Difference Stage] REFPIN((VREF Pin)) OUT((Vout Single Ended)) IN1 --> A1OP IN2 --> A2OP A1OP --- R1A A1OP -- inverting input --> Rg A2OP --- R1B A2OP -- inverting input --> Rg A1OP -- Vo1 --> R2A A1OP -- Vo1 --> R3A A2OP -- Vo2 --> R2B A2OP -- Vo2 --> R3B R2A --> A3OP R2B --> A3OP R3A --> A3OP R3B --> A3OP REFPIN --> A3OP A3OP --> OUT ``` ## 4.3 Frequency Response Behaviour (CMRR vs. Frequency) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L_F [Low Frequency Band] LF1[DC to 100 Hz
CMRR Dominated by Resistor Mismatch] end subgraph M_F [Mid Frequency Band] MF1[100 Hz to 10 kHz
CMRR Flat Plateau] end subgraph H_F [High Frequency Band] HF1[Above 10 kHz
CMRR Rolls Off due to Op-Amp GBW] end L_F --> M_F M_F --> H_F X1[50 Hz Mains] -.->|Rejected| L_F X2[EMG 500 Hz] -.->|Passed| M_F X3[RF 1 MHz Noise] -.->|Filtered| H_F ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The IA's CMRR is **flat and maximum** in the mid-band (the ECG / EEG / EMG region). At **50 Hz** (Indian mains), the CMRR is determined almost entirely by **resistor tolerance** — that's why 0.1% precision resistors are mandatory in real designs. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an instrumentation amplifier. Why is high CMRR critical for ECG signal acquisition?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** CMRR is the ratio of differential-mode gain (A_D) to common-mode gain (A_CM), expressed in decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_D}{A_{CM}}\right)$$ For ECG acquisition, the desired differential signal (≈ 1 mV) sits atop a large common-mode interference of **50 Hz mains hum coupled through the body** (often 1–10 V). The IA must reject this CM noise by **> 80 dB** so that the 1 mV QRS complex is not swamped. High CMRR ensures only the *difference* between the two electrodes is amplified, preserving signal integrity and patient safety. **(3 marks)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *List any three advantages of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier over a single-op-amp difference amplifier in biomedical applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Very high input impedance (> 1 GΩ)** on both inputs — does not load high-impedance skin electrodes. 2. **Gain is set by a single external resistor (Rg)** — gain can be changed without disturbing the carefully matched bridge resistors, so CMRR is preserved. 3. **Superior CMRR (> 100 dB at 50 Hz)** — essential for rejecting 50 Hz mains interference in ECG/EEG. 4. (Bonus point) Provides differential-to-single-ended conversion in one stage. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Forgetting the virtual short assumption** — losing 2 marks. > 2. **Sign error in V_o1 − V_o2** — the negative sign in V_o2 expression trips up many students. Practice it twice. > 3. **Writing "CMRR = 20 log(A_D)" instead of "20 log(A_D/A_CM)"** — full 0 for the CMRR sub-part. Always include the *ratio*. > 4. **Using mismatched formulas** — if the question gives R₁, Rg, you must use $A_D = 1 + 2R_1/R_g$. Don't substitute the bridge ratio unless explicitly given. > 5. **Unit omissions** — dB is dimensionless but **must be stated**; gain ratio A_D is *V/V* or *unitless*. > 6. **Failing to mention safety/isolation** in biomedical contexts — 1 mark reserved for IEC 60601 patient safety awareness. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper]* **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier**. Derive the expression for its differential-mode gain. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Description (2 marks):** The 3-op-amp IA consists of two input buffer stages (A1, A2) feeding a unity-gain difference amplifier (A3). The two non-inverting inputs receive V₁ and V₂ from the bio-electrodes. Resistor Rg connects the inverting inputs of A1 and A2 externally. The four-resistor bridge around A3 is composed of matched R₂ and R₃ pairs. **Working Principle (2 marks):** A1 and A2 form a high-impedance buffer pair. The differential input voltage (V₁ − V₂) appears across Rg, producing a current I = (V₁ − V₂)/Rg. This same current flows through the two R₁ resistors (one in each buffer's feedback path), creating amplified output voltages V_o1 and V_o2. A3 then subtracts V_o2 from V_o1, producing a single-ended output. **Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in **Section 3.1**, applying virtual short and KCL: $$V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ $$\boxed{A_D = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}}$$ **(b)** An instrumentation amplifier uses R₁ = 49.4 kΩ and Rg = 1.1 kΩ. Calculate (i) the differential gain, (ii) the output voltage for V₁ = 102 mV and V₂ = 100 mV, and (iii) the CMRR in dB if the common-mode gain is 0.001. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Differential Gain (2 marks):** $$A_D = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 49{,}400}{1{,}100} = 1 + \dfrac{98{,}800}{1{,}100} = 1 + 89.818$$ $$\boxed{A_D = 90.818 \text{ V/V}}$$ **(ii) Output Voltage (3 marks):** Differential input: $$V_d = V_1 - V_2 = 102 - 100 = 2 \text{ mV} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$ Output voltage: $$V_{out} = A_D \times V_d = 90.818 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1816 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = 181.6 \text{ mV}}$$ **(iii) CMRR in dB (2 marks):** $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_D}{A_{CM}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{90.818}{0.001}\right)$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}(90{,}818) = 20 \times 4.958$$ $$\boxed{\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 99.16 \text{ dB}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]* **(a)** Explain the **limitations of a basic single-op-amp difference amplifier** for biomedical signal acquisition. How does the 3-op-amp IA overcome these limitations? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Limitations of Single-Op-Amp Difference Amplifier (4 marks):** 1. **Low and unequal input impedance:** The input impedance at V₁ and V₂ equals R₁ (or the parallel combination), which is typically only tens of kΩ. Bio-electrodes have source impedances of 5–50 kΩ, leading to severe signal attenuation and **impedance-mismatch-induced CMRR loss**. 2. **Gain depends on resistor matching:** To change the gain, both R₁ and R₂ must be changed simultaneously with **precise matching**. A 0.1% mismatch between R₁ (top) and R₁ (bottom) destroys CMRR at 50 Hz. 3. **Poor CMRR with real sources:** Any source-impedance imbalance (e.g., asymmetric electrode contact) directly degrades CMRR. 4. **Saturation with high CM voltage:** Cannot handle large electrode half-cell potentials (±300 mV) without external CM-range extension circuits. **How 3-Op-Amp IA Overcomes These (3 marks):** 1. **Buffered inputs** → Input impedance is the **op-amp's own Z_in** (> 1 GΩ), independent of resistor values. 2. **Single-resistor gain (Rg)** → Gain can be changed without affecting the bridge matching of A3, preserving CMRR. 3. **Symmetric input stage** → Both electrodes see identical source-loading, so source-impedance imbalance does not degrade CMRR. 4. **Two-stage CMRR action** → A1/A2 reject CM common-mode *current*; A3 rejects any residual CM *voltage*. Net CMRR > 100 dB. **(b)** A 3-op-amp IA is configured with R₁ = 10 kΩ, Rg = 2 kΩ. The bio-signal source has a differential amplitude of 50 µV and a common-mode 50 Hz interference of 1.5 V. The IA's CMRR at 50 Hz is 90 dB. Calculate (i) the gain, (ii) the desired output signal amplitude, and (iii) the residual 50 Hz interference at the output. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Gain (2 marks):** $$A_D = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000}{2{,}000} = 1 + 10 = 11 \text{ V/V}$$ $$\boxed{A_D = 11}$$ **(ii) Desired Output Signal (2 marks):** $$V_{out,\text{signal}} = A_D \times V_d = 11 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out,\text{signal}} = 550 \text{ µV} = 0.55 \text{ mV}}$$ **(iii) Residual 50 Hz Interference (3 marks):** First, convert CMRR from dB to linear ratio: $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{90/20} = 10^{4.5} \approx 31{,}623$$ The equivalent common-mode gain is: $$A_{CM} = \dfrac{A_D}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}}} = \dfrac{11}{31{,}623} \approx 3.479 \times 10^{-4}$$ Residual 50 Hz interference at output: $$V_{out,\text{CM}} = A_{CM} \times V_{CM} = 3.479 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5$$ $$\boxed{V_{out,\text{CM}} \approx 0.522 \text{ mV}}$$ **Discussion (Bonus):** Note that the residual interference (0.522 mV) is nearly equal to the desired signal (0.55 mV) — illustrating why CMRR > 90 dB is essential. In practice, modern IA ICs (AD8421 etc.) offer CMRR > 120 dB to keep residual noise well below signal. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Instrumentation Amplifiers** - **Definition:** Precision, high-CMRR, high-Z_in differential amplifier using 3 op-amps and 7 resistors. - **Master Formula (memorize!):** $A_D = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$ — for R₂ = R₃ in the output bridge. - **General formula (with bridge ratio):** $A_D = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ - **CMRR Definition:** $20 \log_{10}(A_D / A_{CM})$ in dB. **Always include the ratio!** - **CMRR Target for ECG/EEG:** > 80 dB at 50/60 Hz. - **Input Impedance Target:** > 1 GΩ. - **Why 3 op-amps?** - A1, A2: **buffer** the input → high Z_in. - A3: **subtract** → high CMRR + single-ended output. - **Single Rg resistor** sets the gain → CMRR preserved during gain change. - **Real-world ICs:** AD620, INA128, INA333, ADS129x (medical-grade). - **Biomedical Standards:** IEC 60601-1 patient safety, isolation amplifiers (ISO124, AD215) for galvanic isolation. - **DRL Circuit:** Driven-Right-Leg feedback uses an auxiliary op-amp to actively cancel CM interference at the body. - **Noise Equation (RTI):** $V_n = \sqrt{e_n^2 \cdot BW + (i_n R_s)^2 \cdot BW}$. - **Gain-Bandwidth Tradeoff:** $f_{-3dB} = f_T / A_{CL}$. - **Common Pitfall:** Using a 1-op-amp difference amp in ECG front-end → loses marks AND fails in practice. > **End of Module 1 — Bio-potential Amplifiers: Instrumentation Amplifiers Notes** ✅ > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020 aligned) Examination Pattern.*

topics.title: Carrier Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Carrier Amplifiers # Carrier Amplifiers — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carrier Amplifier (Definition)** > A **carrier amplifier** is a specialized class of amplifier used in biomedical instrumentation in which the low-frequency or DC input signal from a bioelectric transducer is first **modulated** onto a high-frequency AC **carrier wave**, then amplified using a stable AC-coupled amplifier stage, and finally **demodulated** back to the original baseband signal. This modulation–amplification–demodulation (MAD) chain eliminates the **DC drift**, **thermal drift**, and **1/f (flicker) noise** problems inherent to direct-coupled (DC) amplifiers, thereby allowing faithful amplification of microvolt-level bioelectric signals from transducers such as **strain gauges, thermocouples, LVDTs, and biopotential electrodes**. Mathematically, a carrier amplifier is a system that realizes the transfer function: $$G_{CA}(j\omega) \;=\; K_d \cdot G_{ac}(j\omega_c \pm j\omega_s) \cdot K_m$$ where $K_m$ is the modulator conversion gain, $G_{ac}(\cdot)$ is the AC amplifier gain centered at carrier frequency $\omega_c$, and $K_d$ is the demodulator gain. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are whispering a secret message across a noisy factory floor (DC signal in a noisy biomedical environment). No one can hear you clearly because of the low-frequency rumble of machinery (low-frequency drift and 1/f noise). So, instead, you place your whisper onto a **loud whistle tone** (the carrier signal), which travels clearly above the rumble. At the other end, a listener with a tuner filters only the whistle tone and **strips it away**, recovering your whisper perfectly. The whistle tone is the *carrier*, and the act of placing your whisper onto the whistle is *modulation*. The recovery is *demodulation*. This is, in essence, what a carrier amplifier does — it shifts the signal away from the noisy low-frequency band, amplifies it cleanly, and shifts it back. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students** > The carrier wave is NOT the information; it is merely the *vehicle*. The information lies in the **envelope** (amplitude, phase, or frequency variations) of the modulated waveform. The carrier frequency itself is usually chosen between **400 Hz and 50 kHz**, depending on the application — well above the **1/f corner** of op-amp flicker noise but well below the **unity-gain bandwidth**. --- ## 1.3 Why Carrier Amplifiers? — The Drift Problem Direct-coupled (DC) amplifiers using operational amplifiers suffer from: - **Input offset voltage drift** (typically **0.1 µV/°C to 10 µV/°C**) - **Input bias current drift** - **1/f noise** concentrated below ~**100 Hz** - **Thermal EMF** at junctions of dissimilar metals For a biopotential signal of amplitude **1 mV** originating from skin electrodes with a thermal EMF of **10 µV/°C**, a temperature fluctuation of just **1 °C** introduces a **1 % error**, making DC amplification practically impossible without constant re-calibration. The carrier amplifier neatly circumvents this by **AC-coupling** through the modulation process. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Design Metrics for Carrier Amplifiers** > - **Carrier frequency** $\,f_c\,$ : typically **400 Hz – 50 kHz** > - **Modulation depth** $\,m\,$ : usually kept $\le$ **1.0** to avoid envelope distortion > - **Chopper frequency** $\,f_{ch}\,$ (for chopper-stabilized): typically **50 Hz – 10 kHz** > - **Input impedance** required from pre-amp: **$\ge$ 10 MΩ** for electrode compatibility > - **CMRR** required: **$\ge$ 80 dB** at power-line frequency for medical use > - **Noise voltage spectral density** at carrier: target **$\le$ 5 nV/√Hz** # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The carrier amplifier operates as a three-stage process: ### Stage 1: Modulation (DC → AC Translation) The DC or low-frequency input signal $v_s(t)$ is multiplied by a high-frequency square-wave or sinusoidal carrier $c(t)$: $$c(t) \;=\; V_c \sin(\omega_c t)$$ The modulated output is: $$v_m(t) \;=\; v_s(t)\cdot c(t) \;=\; V_c\, v_s(t)\sin(\omega_c t)$$ This **frequency-translates** the signal spectrum centered at **0 Hz** to a spectrum centered at **$\pm f_c$**, where AC amplification is free of 1/f noise. ### Stage 2: AC Amplification A stable AC-coupled amplifier (typically a tuned or band-pass amplifier) provides the bulk of the voltage gain $A_v$: $$v_a(t) \;=\; A_v \cdot v_m(t) \;=\; A_v V_c\, v_s(t)\sin(\omega_c t)$$ ### Stage 3: Demodulation (AC → DC Recovery) A **synchronous (phase-sensitive) demodulator** multiplies the amplified signal by the same carrier $c(t)$: $$v_d(t) \;=\; v_a(t)\cdot c(t) \;=\; A_v V_c^2\, v_s(t)\,\sin^2(\omega_c t)$$ Using the trigonometric identity: $$\sin^2(\theta) \;=\; \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$ we obtain: $$v_d(t) \;=\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t) \;-\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t)\cos(2\omega_c t)$$ A low-pass filter then removes the high-frequency term, leaving: $$v_{out}(t) \;=\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t)$$ > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind Synchronous Demodulation** > Synchronous (coherent) demodulation is preferred over envelope detection because it preserves **phase information**, rejects **out-of-phase noise**, and offers **superior noise immunity** — a property called **lock-in detection** when used in scientific instruments. --- ## 2.2 Types of Carrier Amplifiers (KTU Classification) | Type | Carrier Wave | Demodulation | Typical Application | |------|--------------|--------------|---------------------| | AC carrier amplifier | Sine wave (400 Hz–50 kHz) | Phase-sensitive detector | Strain-gauge bridges, LVDTs | | Chopper-stabilized amplifier | Square wave (50 Hz–10 kHz) | Synchronous chopper | Low-level DC bioelectric signals | | PWM carrier amplifier | Pulse-width modulated | Low-pass filter | Telemetry, isolated medical probes | | Ring-demodulator carrier amp. | Sine (1–10 kHz) | Diode ring | Carrier pre-amplifiers in EEG | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation | Description | Typical Units | |----------|-------------|---------------| | $v_m(t) = V_c v_s(t)\sin(\omega_c t)$ | Modulated signal output | volts (V) | | $m = V_{s,\text{peak}} / V_c$ | Modulation index (amplitude modulation) | dimensionless | | $v_d(t) = \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t)$ | Demodulated output (after LPF) | volts (V) | | $\eta_{demod} = \frac{V_{out,\text{DC}}}{V_{in,\text{DC}}} = \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}$ | Overall gain of carrier amplifier | dimensionless | | $\text{SNR}_{out} = \text{SNR}_{in} + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{B_{in}}{B_{out}}\right)$ | SNR improvement due to bandwidth narrowing | dB | | $f_c \ge f_{1/f\text{,corner}}$ | Carrier frequency selection criterion | Hz | | $T_{ch} \ll T_{drift}$ | Chopper period $\ll$ thermal drift time-constant | seconds | | $v_{noise,1/f} \propto 1/f^{\alpha}$ | 1/f noise spectral density ($\alpha \approx 0.5$–1) | V/√Hz | | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB | | $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K \omega_c}{j\omega + \omega_c}$ | Chopper equivalent transfer function | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipes avoided in formulas** — all absolute-value notations use `\vert` for clean rendering. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Carrier amplifiers form the **backbone of every modern biomedical instrument** that interfaces with low-level DC transducers: - **Strain-gauge blood-pressure transducers** (Wheatstone bridge excitation at carrier frequency) - **LVDT-based displacement sensors** in respiratory bellows and catheter-tip manometers - **Chopper-stabilized ECG pre-amplifiers** (e.g., AD620, INA128 with chopper front-ends) - **Lock-in amplifiers** for spectrophotometric and impedance plethysmography measurements - **Isolated patient monitors** using carrier transformers for **galvanic isolation** (essential for **IEC 60601-1** patient safety) The choice of carrier frequency also determines **EMI susceptibility** — clinical carrier amplifiers typically operate at frequencies far from **50 Hz / 60 Hz power-line interference** to maximize rejection. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Complete Signal Chain of an AC Carrier Amplifier We derive the overall input-output relationship for a generic carrier amplifier chain. ### Given: - Input bioelectric signal: $\,v_s(t)\,$ - Carrier oscillator output: $\,c(t) = V_c \sin(\omega_c t)\,$ - AC amplifier midband gain: $\,A_v\,$ - Ideal synchronous demodulator multiplier and ideal low-pass filter. ### Step 1: Write the modulation-stage output $$v_m(t) \;=\; v_s(t) \cdot c(t) \;=\; V_c\, v_s(t)\,\sin(\omega_c t)$$ *Explanation:* Multiplication of input by carrier frequency-translates the spectrum centered at DC to be centered at $\pm f_c$. ### Step 2: AC amplification $$v_a(t) \;=\; A_v \, v_m(t) \;=\; A_v V_c\, v_s(t)\,\sin(\omega_c t)$$ *Explanation:* AC-coupled amplifier passes the signal at $f_c$ with gain $A_v$ while rejecting any residual DC drift. ### Step 3: Synchronous demodulation $$v_d(t) \;=\; v_a(t)\cdot c(t) \;=\; A_v V_c^2\, v_s(t)\,\sin^2(\omega_c t)$$ *Explanation:* Multiplying again by the same coherent carrier shifts the spectrum back toward DC and creates an unwanted $2\omega_c$ image. ### Step 4: Apply trigonometric identity $$\sin^2(\omega_c t) \;=\; \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega_c t)}{2}$$ Substituting: $$v_d(t) \;=\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t) \;-\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t)\cos(2\omega_c t)$$ ### Step 5: Apply low-pass filter A LPF with cutoff $f_L \ll 2f_c$ removes the high-frequency term, leaving the recovered baseband signal: $$\boxed{\,v_{out}(t) \;=\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}\, v_s(t)\,}$$ *Result:* The carrier amplifier behaves as an ideal linear amplifier with effective gain: $$G_{CA} \;=\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Observation:** The effective gain scales with $V_c^2$. Therefore, **carrier amplitude stability** directly determines the **gain accuracy** of the entire amplifier — a critical design consideration. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Chopper-Stabilized Amplifier Transfer Function A chopper-stabilized amplifier uses a square-wave chopper at frequency $f_{ch}$ to alternately invert the input signal. The Fourier series of a symmetric square wave $c(t)$ of amplitude $\pm V_c$ is: $$c(t) \;=\; \frac{4V_c}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega_{ch}t)$$ ### Step 1: Modulated signal after chopper $$v_m(t) \;=\; v_s(t)\, c(t) \;=\; \frac{4V_c}{\pi}\, v_s(t) \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(n\omega_{ch}t)}{n}$$ ### Step 2: Cross-product expansion For a single-tone input $v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega_s t)$ and using the product-to-sum identity: $$\sin(\omega_s t)\sin(n\omega_{ch}t) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\cos((\omega_s - n\omega_{ch})t) - \cos((\omega_s + n\omega_{ch})t)\right]$$ we obtain: $$v_m(t) \;=\; \frac{2V_c V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\!\left[\cos((\omega_s - n\omega_{ch})t) - \cos((\omega_s + n\omega_{ch})t)\right]$$ ### Step 3: AC amplification and re-chopping The AC amplifier centered at $\omega_{ch}$ passes only the $n = 1$ component. After re-chopping at the same $f_{ch}$ and low-pass filtering, the output is: $$v_{out}(t) \;=\; \frac{8 V_c^2}{\pi^2}\, A_v \, v_s(t)$$ Hence: $$\boxed{\,G_{CS} \;=\; \frac{8 A_v V_c^2}{\pi^2} \;\approx\; 0.811\, A_v V_c^2\,}$$ *Explanation:* The factor $8/\pi^2 \approx 0.811$ arises from the Fourier coefficients of the square wave. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Modulation Index and Linearity Constraint For amplitude modulation with carrier $V_c$ and modulating signal of peak $\vert v_s \vert_{\max}$: $$m \;=\; \frac{\vert v_s \vert_{\max}}{V_c}$$ The total instantaneous envelope becomes: $$v_{env}(t) \;=\; V_c \bigl(1 + m \cdot v_n(t)\bigr) \ge 0$$ where $v_n(t)$ is the normalized modulating signal with $\vert v_n(t) \vert \le 1$. **Linearity condition:** To avoid envelope distortion (over-modulation clipping): $$m \;\le\; 1$$ For $m = 1$, the modulated envelope reaches zero — a condition called **100 % modulation**. Beyond this, **phase reversal** and harmonic distortion occur. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Carrier Amplifier Topologies | Parameter | AC Sine Carrier | Chopper (Square) | PWM Carrier | |-----------|----------------|------------------|-------------| | Carrier frequency $f_c$ | 400 Hz – 50 kHz | 50 Hz – 10 kHz | 1 kHz – 100 kHz | | Spectral purity | Excellent | Contains odd harmonics | Pulse spectrum | | Demodulator complexity | Phase-sensitive | Synchronous switch | LPF only | | Power efficiency | Low (linear) | High (switched) | Very high | | Drift rejection | Very high | Highest | High | | Common biomedical use | Strain gauges, LVDT | ECG / EEG pre-amp | Isolated telemetry | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Carrier Amplifier The figure below captures the complete signal-flow chain of a typical biomedical carrier amplifier. All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (as required for safe Mermaid rendering). ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Bioelectric Transducer
Strain Gauge or LVDT] --> A2[Pre-Amplifier
High Zin Buffer] A2 --> A3[Modulator Stage
Multiplier with Carrier] A4[Carrier Oscillator
Sine Wave f_c] --> A3 A3 --> A5[AC Coupled Amplifier
Tuned to f_c] A5 --> A6[Phase Sensitive
Demodulator PSD] A4 --> A6 A6 --> A7[Low Pass Filter
Removes 2f_c Component] A7 --> A8[Post Amplifier
and Level Shifter] A8 --> A9[Output Stage
Display or ADC] classDef signal fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366 classDef carrier fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#663300 classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,color:#003300 class A1,A2,A5,A7,A8 signal class A4,A3,A6 carrier class A9 output ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Blue blocks represent the **baseband / signal path**, orange blocks represent the **carrier / modulation path**, and the green block is the **final output**. The orange carrier path feeds both modulator and demodulator, ensuring **synchronization** essential for coherent detection. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Chopper-Stabilized Topology ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[DC Input Signal
v_s low frequency] --> B2[Input Chopper
Square Wave Switch] B3[Chopper Driver
50 Hz to 10 kHz] --> B2 B2 --> B4[AC Amplifier
High Gain Band Pass] B4 --> B5[Output Chopper
Synchronous Demodulator] B3 --> B5 B5 --> B6[Low Pass Filter
Recovers Baseband] B6 --> B7[Final Output
v_out DC Restored] B4 --> B8[Drift Correction
Nulling Loop] B8 --> B4 classDef dc fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#003366 classDef chop fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#663300 classDef fb fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#330066 class B1,B4,B6,B7 dc class B2,B3,B5 chop class B8 fb ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Drift-Correction Loop (purple block):** The chopper-stabilized topology employs a **servo nulling loop** that senses any residual DC drift at the AC amplifier output and applies a corrective signal back to its input, achieving effective offset drift below **0.01 µV/°C**. --- ## 4.3 Sub-System Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[Modulator] --> C2[Spectral Shifter
DC to f_c] C2 --> C3[AC Amplifier] C3 --> C4[Demodulator] C4 --> C5[Spectral Shifter
f_c to DC] C1 -. Phase Lock .-> C4 C3 -. Bandwidth Limited .-> C4 C5 --> C6[Baseband Recovery
LPF Output] classDef process fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc classDef lock fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600 class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 process class C4 lock ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a carrier amplifier. Why is it preferred over a direct-coupled DC amplifier in biomedical instrumentation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **carrier amplifier** is an amplifier in which a low-frequency or DC input signal is first **modulated** onto a high-frequency AC carrier wave, amplified by an AC-coupled stage, and then **demodulated** back to its original form. *(1 mark)* It is preferred over a DC amplifier because the modulation–demodulation process eliminates the **DC drift**, **thermal offset**, and **1/f flicker noise** that plague direct-coupled amplifiers, enabling accurate amplification of microvolt-level bioelectric signals such as ECG, EMG, and strain-gauge outputs. *(2 marks)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the function of a phase-sensitive demodulator in a carrier amplifier.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD) **multiplies** the amplified modulated signal with the original carrier waveform and recovers the baseband signal through a low-pass filter. *(1 mark)* It performs **synchronous detection**, which preserves the **phase** and **polarity** of the original signal while rejecting **out-of-phase noise** and quadrature components. *(2 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of an AC carrier amplifier**. Identify each block and state its function. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the **overall voltage gain** of the carrier amplifier, showing how the modulated, amplified, and demodulated signals relate to the input. Discuss how carrier amplitude affects gain accuracy. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)** The AC carrier amplifier consists of the following blocks — refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1**: 1. **Bioelectric Transducer / Signal Source** — produces low-level DC or LF signal $v_s(t)$ (e.g., strain gauge bridge). *(1 mark)* 2. **Pre-amplifier** — high input-impedance buffer using FET-input op-amp to avoid loading the transducer. *(1 mark)* 3. **Modulator** — an analog multiplier (e.g., AD633) that multiplies $v_s(t)$ with carrier $c(t) = V_c\sin(\omega_c t)$ to produce $v_m(t)$. *(1 mark)* 4. **Carrier Oscillator** — Wien-bridge or crystal oscillator providing a stable, low-distortion sine wave at $f_c$. *(1 mark)* 5. **AC Amplifier** — band-pass or tuned amplifier centered at $f_c$, providing the bulk of voltage gain. *(1 mark)* 6. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator** — multiplier that re-multiplies the signal by the same carrier. *(1 mark)* 7. **Low-Pass Filter** — removes the $2f_c$ component, leaving only the recovered baseband. *(0.5 mark)* 8. **Output Stage** — post-amplification and level shifting for display or ADC. *(0.5 mark)* **(b) Gain Derivation (7 marks)** Following the derivation in **Section 3.1**: $$\begin{aligned} v_m(t) &= v_s(t) \cdot V_c \sin(\omega_c t) \quad &\text{(after modulator)}\\ v_a(t) &= A_v \cdot v_m(t) = A_v V_c v_s(t)\sin(\omega_c t) \quad &\text{(after AC amplifier)}\\ v_d(t) &= v_a(t) \cdot V_c\sin(\omega_c t) = A_v V_c^2 v_s(t)\sin^2(\omega_c t) \quad &\text{(after demodulator)}\\ v_d(t) &= \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}v_s(t) - \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}v_s(t)\cos(2\omega_c t) \quad &\text{(using trig identity)}\\ v_{out}(t) &= \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2} v_s(t) \quad &\text{(after LPF)} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$G_{CA} \;=\; \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \;=\; \frac{A_v V_c^2}{2}$$ **Discussion on Gain Accuracy:** Since the gain depends on $V_c^2$, any drift in carrier amplitude directly translates into gain error. A 1 % drift in $V_c$ produces a 2 % drift in overall gain. Hence, **carrier amplitude regulation** (using AGC or amplitude-stabilized oscillators) is essential in precision biomedical instruments. *(2 marks)* --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Explain the **chopper-stabilized DC amplifier** with a block diagram. How does it achieve ultra-low offset drift? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A chopper amplifier uses a 1 kHz square-wave chopper with carrier amplitude $V_c = 5$ V and AC amplifier gain $A_v = 1000$. Calculate the overall voltage gain and the output voltage for an input of $V_s = 100\ \mu V$. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Working of Chopper-Stabilized Amplifier (7 marks)** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.2**. 1. The DC input signal $v_s(t)$ is **chopped** (multiplied) by a square wave of frequency $f_{ch}$, converting DC into an AC square-modulated signal at $f_{ch}$. *(1 mark)* 2. This AC signal is amplified by a high-gain band-pass AC amplifier centered at $f_{ch}$. *(1 mark)* 3. The amplified signal is **re-chopped** synchronously by the same square wave (synchronous demodulation), translating the spectrum back to baseband. *(1 mark)* 4. A low-pass filter recovers the amplified DC signal. *(1 mark)* 5. **Drift Cancellation Principle:** Any DC offset or 1/f noise generated within the AC amplifier is also chopped at $f_{ch}$. When re-chopped, this offset is moved up to $f_{ch}$ and filtered out by the LPF, leaving only the true signal. *(2 marks)* 6. A **nulling servo loop** senses residual drift and injects a correcting voltage back to the AC amplifier input, achieving drift figures below **0.01 µV/°C**. *(1 mark)* **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks)** Given: $f_{ch} = 1$ kHz, $V_c = 5$ V, $A_v = 1000$, $V_s = 100\ \mu V$. Using the chopper-amplifier gain formula derived in **Section 3.2**: $$G_{CS} \;=\; \frac{8 A_v V_c^2}{\pi^2}$$ Substituting values: $$\begin{aligned} G_{CS} &= \frac{8 \times 1000 \times (5)^2}{\pi^2}\\ G_{CS} &= \frac{8 \times 1000 \times 25}{9.8696}\\ G_{CS} &= \frac{200{,}000}{9.8696}\\ G_{CS} &\approx 20{,}264 \end{aligned}$$ **Overall voltage gain:** $G_{CS} \approx 2.03 \times 10^4$ *(3 marks)* **Output voltage:** $$V_{out} = G_{CS} \times V_s = 20{,}264 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 2.0264\ \text{V}$$ $$V_{out} \approx 2.03\ \text{V} \quad \text{*(4 marks)*}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the factor $8/\pi^2$** in chopper-amplifier gain — the square-wave Fourier coefficient is mandatory, not optional. > 2. **Confusing demodulation with rectification** — students often write about simple diode detection instead of *synchronous (multiplicative) demodulation*, losing full process marks. > 3. **Missing the low-pass filter step** — without LPF, the $2\omega_c$ ripple appears in the output, costing 1–2 marks. > 4. **Omitting carrier amplitude discussion** in gain derivation — examiners specifically test understanding of *why* $V_c$ stability matters. > 5. **Using DC amplifier language** (e.g., "input bias current") in carrier amplifier answers — terminology mismatch signals conceptual confusion. > 6. **Wrong unit notation** — gain is dimensionless, but writing "volts" or "V/V" inconsistently invites deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Carrier Amplifiers** - **Definition:** Carrier amplifier = Modulator + AC Amplifier + Synchronous Demodulator + LPF. *(Memorize the block sequence.)* - **Purpose:** Eliminates DC drift, thermal EMF, and 1/f noise in low-level bioelectric signal amplification. - **Carrier frequency:** Typically **400 Hz – 50 kHz**, chosen above the **1/f corner** of the op-amp. - **Carrier types:** Sine-wave (preferred for low distortion) and square-wave (preferred for chopper designs). - **Chopper-stabilized:** Uses square wave + drift-correction loop → achieves drift **< 0.01 µV/°C**. - **Key formulas:** - Modulation: $v_m(t) = V_c v_s(t)\sin(\omega_c t)$ - Sine carrier gain: $G_{CA} = A_v V_c^2 / 2$ - Square-wave chopper gain: $G_{CS} = 8 A_v V_c^2 / \pi^2$ - Modulation index: $m = \vert v_s \vert_{\max} / V_c \le 1$ - **Demodulation must be synchronous** to preserve polarity and phase — distinguishes carrier amplifier from simple AM envelope detector. - **Gain depends on $V_c^2$** — therefore carrier amplitude regulation is critical. - **Applications:** Strain-gauge bridges, LVDT position sensors, ECG/EEG pre-amplifiers, isolated patient monitors (IEC 60601-1 compliance). - **Advantage over DC amp:** Drift-free, low 1/f noise, supports **galvanic isolation** via carrier transformers. - **Disadvantages:** More complex circuitry, limited bandwidth, sensitivity to carrier phase drift. - **Examiner tip:** Always show the complete derivation chain — modulation → amplification → demodulation → LPF — never skip the LPF step.

topics.title: Isolation Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Isolation Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Isolation Amplifier** is a specialized differential amplifier whose input section, output section, and power supply section are electrically isolated from one another through a physical isolation barrier. It is engineered to transmit an analog signal from a high-voltage, noisy, or hazardous source (such as the human body in a clinical environment) to a measurement/recording instrument while **completely breaking the galvanic (ohmic/electrical) path** between the two sides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Exact):** "An isolation amplifier is an amplifier that provides complete electrical isolation between its input and output, while accurately transferring the signal from the source to the load. The galvanic isolation is essential in biomedical applications to protect the patient from leakage currents, microshock hazards, and to suppress line-frequency common-mode voltages up to hundreds of volts."

**Key Performance Metrics (must be memorised verbatim):**
- **Isolation Voltage Rating:** Typically **1500 V RMS to 5000 V RMS** (sometimes up to 7500 V peak).
- **Leakage Current:** Must be **less than 10 µA** (per IEC 60601-1 medical safety standard).
- **Isolation Mode Rejection Ratio (IMRR):** **> 100 dB at 50/60 Hz**.
- **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** **> 100 dB at 50/60 Hz**.
- **Bandwidth:** Typically **DC to 20 kHz** (sufficient for ECG/EEG/EMG).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition — The "Lifeguard on a Rope" Analogy

Imagine a person drowning in a stormy, high-voltage river (the **patient's body** with mains-frequency interference and defibrillator-induced high voltages). A lifeguard standing safely on the shore (the **measuring instrument / recording device**) wants to measure the water current around the swimmer without ever stepping into the river or making any physical (electrical) contact.

The lifeguard uses a **strong, insulated rope** (the **isolation barrier**). The rope transmits the exact motion/force of the water (the **biomedical signal** like ECG) without any electricity flowing through it. If the river is struck by lightning, only the rope and the swimmer get shocked — the lifeguard is **completely safe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Ports of an Isolation Amplifier (KEEP IN MIND):**
> 1. **Input Port** — connected to the patient/sensor.
> 2. **Output Port** — connected to the display/recorder/ADC.
> 3. **Power Port** — supplies DC to the input stage without any galvanic connection to the system power.
>
> The isolation barrier exists between **Input↔Output**, **Input↔Power**, and **Power↔Output**.

### Why are Isolation Amplifiers *Essential* in Biomedical Engineering?

| Hazard | Cause | How Isolation Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| **Macroshock** (>100 mA) | Direct contact with mains line | Breaks ground loop; leakage current < 10 µA |
| **Microshock** (<10 µA direct to heart) | Catheter-borne leakage | Full galvanic isolation, capacitive/transformer barrier |
| **Common-Mode 50/60 Hz interference** | Patient coupled to earth/ground | High CMRR + isolation barrier |
| **Defibrillator Overload** | 5000 V pulse from defibrillator | Isolation voltage rating > 5000 V |
| **Ground-Loop Currents** | Multiple earth connections | Eliminates ground loops completely |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture — The Three-Stage Block View

A complete isolation amplifier is a **closed-loop, three-port system**. The signal path and power path are isolated through one of three physical phenomena:

**(A) Transformer Coupling (Magnetic Isolation)** — Signal modulates an AC carrier, coupled across a transformer.
**(B) Optical Coupling (Opto-Isolation)** — Signal drives an LED; a photodiode/phototransistor on the other side detects light.
**(C) Capacitive Coupling (Electric-Field Isolation)** — Signal modulates a high-frequency carrier that is coupled across a tiny, high-voltage capacitor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Most-Emphasised Type:** The **transformer-coupled isolation amplifier** using the **duty-cycle modulation–demodulation** technique (e.g., the classic Burr-Brown ISO100 / ISO102 / ISO122 family). It is the canonical exam answer.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Duty-Cycle Modulation Isolation Amplifier

The input signal $v_{in}$ is first conditioned by a **pre-amplifier (A1)**. A **voltage-to-current converter (V/I)** then drives the primary of an **isolation transformer** with a current proportional to $v_{in}$. An internal **oscillator** (~50 kHz) chops this current so that a current pulses through the primary. The secondary of the transformer receives these pulses and is synchronously demodulated on the output side. The demodulated current is then converted back to a voltage by an **output amplifier (A2)**.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Functional Block

- **Pre-amp A1:** Provides high input impedance (>10¹² Ω) — critical because the patient/skin-electrode interface is a high-impedance source.
- **V/I Converter:** Converts voltage to current so the signal can be *magnitude-encoded* (i.e., immune to transformer noise pickup).
- **Oscillator (50 kHz):** Frequency must be **> 20 kHz** to be above mains and its harmonics, but **< 200 kHz** to keep transformer core loss low.
- **Transformer:** Provides **galvanic isolation** of 1500–5000 V. Two windings are physically separated but magnetically coupled.
- **Synchronous Demodulator:** Uses the same oscillator signal (carried on a separate tiny channel) to **recover the polarity** of the original signal — this is what gives the amplifier its bipolar, DC-coupled capability.
- **Output Amplifier A2:** Provides low output impedance and the **externally settable gain** via a single resistor $R_G$ between pins.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet) — Isolation Amplifier

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Gain of Isolation Amplifier | $A_{T} = A_{1} \cdot A_{2}$ | $A_1$ = pre-amp gain, $A_2$ = post-amp gain |
| 2 | Differential Gain of Front-End (Pre-amp) | $A_{1} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_{1}}{R_{G}}$ | $R_G$ = gain-setting resistor |
| 3 | Output Stage Gain | $A_{2} = \dfrac{R_{F}}{R_{S}}$ | Set internally or by user network |
| 4 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Expressed in **dB** |
| 5 | Isolation Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{IMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{iso}}{V_{out}}\right)$ | Tested at line frequency 50/60 Hz |
| 6 | Leakage Current (medical limit) | $I_{L} \le 10\,\mu\text{A}$ | Per **IEC 60601-1** |
| 7 | Isolation Voltage Rating | $V_{iso} = 1500\text{–}5000\,\text{V}_{rms}$ | Continuous withstand voltage |
| 8 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{os} = 0.5\text{–}5\,\text{mV}$ | Adjustable to zero with trim pot |
| 9 | Input Bias Current | $I_{b} \le 50\,\text{pA}$ (FET input) | Critical for high-Z sources |
| 10 | Bandwidth | $BW = \text{DC to 20 kHz}$ | Sufficient for ECG (0.05–150 Hz), EEG (0.5–100 Hz) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for Markdown:** In the table above, all absolute values, fractions, and ratios have been written using `\dfrac` or parentheses — **never use the vertical pipe `|` symbol** within a row, as it breaks the markdown table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Patient Monitoring Systems (ECG, EEG, EMG, ICU monitors):** Required by **IEC 60601-1** for any patient-connected circuit during surgery, ICU, or catheterization.
- **Defibrillation Protection:** The 5000 V isolation barrier prevents the defibrillator pulse from destroying the monitor.
- **Cardiac Catheterization Labs:** Microshock hazard demands < 10 µA leakage — only isolation amplifiers guarantee this.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Breaking ground loops between high-voltage motors/transducers and 4–20 mA control loops.
- **Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ):** Multichannel DAQs need channel-to-channel + channel-to-bus isolation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain of an Isolation Amplifier (Transformer-Coupled, e.g., ISO122)

The classical isolation amplifier consists of a **front-end instrumentation-amplifier-like stage** (A1), an **isolation barrier**, and a **summing output stage** (A2). We derive the gain rigorously.

**Step 1 — Identify the Front-End Topology**
The pre-amplifier A1 is a standard three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Let the gain-setting resistor be $R_G$ (between the two op-amp inverting inputs of the input stage). The fixed resistors in the input buffers are each $R_1$. The fixed resistors in the difference-amplifier stage are $R_2$ and $R_3$.

**Step 2 — Express the Differential Gain of A1**
For a balanced differential input $v_{in} = v_{1} - v_{2}$, the differential gain of the input stage is given by the well-known instrumentation-amp equation. Each input op-amp produces a gain of $1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_G/2} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$ on the difference, and the difference amplifier that follows has unity gain if $R_2 = R_3$.

$$
A_1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}
$$

**Step 3 — Modulation-Coupling-Demodulation Block (Unity Pass-Band Transfer)**
Assuming the transformer, modulator, and demodulator are designed for unity pass-band gain (the carrier is just a transport mechanism), the magnitude of the signal at the secondary equals the magnitude at the primary, with no DC path between them. Hence the barrier stage gain $A_{barrier} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Output Stage Gain (A2)**
The output op-amp is configured as a difference amplifier or summing amplifier. For the standard ISO122, $A_2$ is internally set to unity (the user only sets $R_G$).

$$
A_2 = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Total Closed-Loop Gain**
The total gain from input to output is the cascade product of the three stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_T &= A_1 \cdot A_{barrier} \cdot A_2 \\[6pt]
A_T &= \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot (1) \cdot (1) \\[6pt]
A_T &= 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Output Voltage Equation**
For a differential input signal $v_{in}$ (e.g., ECG lead-II voltage) the output is:

$$
v_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) v_{in}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $R_G$ outside the isolation barrier?** Because $R_G$ is on the **input side**. Adjusting gain does **not** require any connection to the output side — this preserves isolation. This is a frequent KTU short-answer / viva question.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** A transformer-coupled isolation amplifier (e.g., ISO100 family) has $R_1 = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$ in each input buffer. The output stage has unity gain. The barrier stage has unity gain. The ECG signal to be measured has peak amplitude $5\,\text{mV}$ and the system must produce $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ swing. Find (a) the required value of $R_G$, and (b) the CMRR (in dB) if the differential gain is 1000 and the common-mode gain is 0.001.

**(a) Required Gain and $R_G$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_T &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{5\,\text{V}}{5\,\text{mV}} = 1000 \\[6pt]
A_T &= 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G} \\[6pt]
1000 &= 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 25\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G} \\[6pt]
999 &= \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G} \\[6pt]
R_G &= \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{999} \approx 50.05\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) CMRR in dB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1000}{0.001}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(10^{6}) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \times 6 = 120\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Leakage-Current Bound (Patient Safety)

The patient-leakage current flows through the patient to ground. It is set by the **isolation impedance** $Z_{iso}$ and the common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ (e.g., 220 V mains).

$$
I_{leak} = \frac{V_{cm}}{Z_{iso}}
$$

For microshock safety, the international standard requires $I_{leak} < 10\,\mu\text{A}$. Solving for the required isolation impedance:

$$
Z_{iso} \ge \frac{220\,\text{V}}{10\,\mu\text{A}} = 22\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

In practice, isolation barrier impedance must exceed **100 MΩ–10 GΩ** to provide a wide safety margin — and this is exactly what transformer/optical/capacitive isolation achieves.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Transformer-Coupled Isolation Amplifier (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_SIDE["INPUT PORT (Patient / Sensor Side)"]
        nodeP["Patient Biopotential Source ECG EEG EMG"]
        nodeA1["Pre Amplifier A1 Instrumentation Amp Topology High Z input"]
        nodeVI["Voltage to Current Converter V to I Stage"]
        nodeOSC["Oscillator 50 kHz Carrier Generator"]
    end

    subgraph ISOLATION_BARRIER["GALVANIC ISOLATION BARRIER 1500 to 5000 Vrms"]
        nodeTX["Isolation Transformer Two Windings Physically Separated"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_SIDE["OUTPUT PORT (Instrument / Recorder Side)"]
        nodeDEM["Synchronous Demodulator Recovers Polarity"]
        nodeA2["Output Amplifier A2 Low Impedance Buffer"]
        nodeOUT["Recorder Display ADC or Data Acquisition System"]
    end

    subgraph POWER_SIDE["DC POWER PORT (Isolated DC DC Converter)"]
        nodePWR["Isolated DC DC Converter Provides Power to Input Stage"]
    end

    nodeP --> nodeA1
    nodeA1 --> nodeVI
    nodeOSC --> nodeVI
    nodeVI --> nodeTX
    nodeTX --> nodeDEM
    nodeDEM --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeOUT
    nodePWR -.powers.-> nodeA1
    nodePWR -.powers.-> nodeVI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The thick horizontal line in the middle (`ISOLATION_BARRIER`) represents the physical gap — *no wire crosses it*. Signal crosses magnetically (transformer), light (opto), or capacitively (capacitor). The dashed arrows from `Isolated DC-DC Converter` show that power is also delivered across an isolation barrier (often a small high-frequency transformer) to the input side, so even the supply rails are floating.

### 4.2 Comparison Diagram — Three Isolation Techniques (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["METHOD A TRANSFORMER ISOLATION"]
        A1["Modulate input signal to AC carrier"]
        A2["Couple via magnetic flux through transformer"]
        A3["Demodulate on secondary side"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph B["METHOD B OPTICAL ISOLATION"]
        B1["Drive an LED with input current"]
        B2["Light crosses an air gap or transparent dielectric"]
        B3["Photodiode or Phototransistor detects on other side"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph C["METHOD C CAPACITIVE ISOLATION"]
        C1["Modulate signal to high frequency"]
        C2["Couple via electric field across small capacitor"]
        C3["Differential receiver on other side"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end
```

### 4.3 Complete Application Diagram — ECG Front-End with Isolation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeHUMAN["Human Patient Connected Electrodes RA LA LL"]
    nodePRE["Pre Amplifier A1 Gain 10 CMRR 100 dB"]
    nodeBAR["Isolation Barrier 4000 Vrms"]
    nodePOST["Output Amplifier A2 Gain 100 to 1000"]
    nodeMON["ECG Monitor or PC Based DAQ System"]
    nodeHUMAN --> nodePRE --> nodeBAR --> nodePOST --> nodeMON
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an isolation amplifier. List any three of its key specifications that make it suitable for biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

An isolation amplifier is a device that provides complete electrical (galvanic) isolation between its input, output, and power supply sections while accurately transmitting the analog signal from source to load.

Three key specifications:

1. **High Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > 100 dB)** — rejects 50/60 Hz interference coupled to the patient.
2. **High Isolation Voltage (1500 V RMS to 5000 V RMS)** — protects the patient and instrument from defibrillator and mains surges.
3. **Very Low Leakage Current (< 10 µA)** — meets **IEC 60601-1** medical safety standards, preventing macroshock/microshock.

*(Any three of: high input impedance, IMRR > 100 dB, bandwidth DC–20 kHz, low input bias current, isolated DC-DC power port.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of an isolation barrier? Name the three physical mechanisms by which isolation can be achieved.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the Isolation Barrier:** It provides complete galvanic (electrical) separation between the input port (connected to the patient) and the output port (connected to the instrument), thereby breaking any DC path for leakage current and blocking high common-mode voltages. It transmits the signal by a non-galvanic means.

**Three Physical Mechanisms:**

1. **Magnetic Coupling** — using an **isolation transformer** (signal modulates an AC carrier).
2. **Optical Coupling** — using an **optocoupler** (LED + photodiode/phototransistor).
3. **Capacitive (Electric-Field) Coupling** — using a **high-voltage capacitor** to couple a high-frequency modulated signal.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **transformer-coupled isolation amplifier**. Discuss the role of the modulator, isolation transformer, and demodulator.

**(b) [7 Marks)** An isolation amplifier uses an input instrumentation amplifier with $R_1 = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ and gain-setting resistor $R_G$. The output stage and barrier stage each have unity gain. If the desired overall gain is 100, calculate the required value of $R_G$. If a common-mode voltage of 5 V produces an output of 50 mV while the differential input of 5 mV produces 500 mV output, find the **CMRR in dB**.

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Working of Transformer-Coupled Isolation Amplifier [7 Marks]**

The transformer-coupled isolation amplifier consists of the following functional blocks (also shown in the diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Pre-Amplifier (A1):** Built as a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. It accepts the **differential biopotential signal** from the patient electrodes (e.g., ECG). It has very high input impedance (FET input, > 10¹² Ω) and provides initial gain. The gain is set by a single external resistor $R_G$ on the input side.

2. **Voltage-to-Current (V/I) Converter:** Converts the amplified voltage into a proportional current. This is essential because a current signal is **immune to the magnetically-induced noise** picked up by the transformer windings.

3. **Oscillator / Carrier Generator:** Generates a high-frequency square wave carrier (typically **50 kHz**). This frequency is chosen **above 20 kHz** (above mains and its audible harmonics) and **below 200 kHz** (to keep transformer core loss low). The oscillator is used in two places: to chop the input current, and synchronously to demodulate the output.

4. **Isolation Transformer:** Has a primary winding on the input side and a secondary winding on the output side. The two windings are **physically separated** by a high-grade dielectric (withstanding > 4000 V). Magnetic flux couples the chopped signal across. The transformer has **unity pass-band gain** for the modulating signal.

5. **Synchronous Demodulator:** Uses the same oscillator signal (delivered across a *small* isolation barrier or via a separate channel) to rectify the secondary current in synchronism with the input chopper. This recovers the **polarity** of the original signal — providing full DC-coupled, bipolar operation.

6. **Output Amplifier (A2):** A low-impedance buffer / difference amplifier. Provides the final gain (often user-settable) and drives the recorder/ADC.

7. **Isolated DC-DC Converter:** Generates the floating DC supply rails for the input-side circuitry without any galvanic connection to the system power — completing the **three-port isolation**.

**Signal Flow:** $v_{in} \rightarrow A_1 \rightarrow V/I \rightarrow \text{Chopper} \rightarrow \text{Transformer} \rightarrow \text{Demodulator} \rightarrow A_2 \rightarrow v_{out}$

The **modulator** (chopper) acts as a "DC-to-AC converter" so the signal can pass through the transformer. The **demodulator** is a synchronous rectifier that recovers the original DC waveform with the correct polarity.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Finding $R_G$**

Given: $A_T = 100$, $A_2 = 1$, $A_{barrier} = 1$, $R_1 = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$.

The total gain is the product of all stages:

$$
A_T = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
100 &= 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 50\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G} \\[4pt]
99 &= \frac{100\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G} \\[4pt]
R_G &= \frac{100\,\text{k}\Omega}{99} \approx 1.0101\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 1.01\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Finding Differential and Common-Mode Gains**

Differential input $v_{d,in} = 5\,\text{mV}$ produces $v_{out} = 500\,\text{mV}$.

$$
A_d = \frac{500\,\text{mV}}{5\,\text{mV}} = 100
$$

Common-mode input $v_{cm,in} = 5\,\text{V}$ produces $v_{out,cm} = 50\,\text{mV}$.

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{50\,\text{mV}}{5\,\text{V}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Computing CMRR in dB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{0.01}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(10^4) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \times 4 = 80\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $R_G \approx 1.01\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $\text{CMRR} = 80\,\text{dB}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **three methods of isolation** (transformer, optical, capacitive) used in isolation amplifiers. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **CMRR** and **IMRR**. An isolation amplifier has CMRR = 110 dB and IMRR = 100 dB. If the differential input signal is 1 mV and a common-mode voltage of 10 V is present, calculate the expected output signal (in mV) and the isolation-mode error at the output.

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Comparison of Three Isolation Methods [7 Marks]**

| Method | Principle | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transformer (Magnetic)** | Signal modulates AC carrier; coupled via magnetic flux | High isolation voltage (up to 5000 V); excellent linearity; wide bandwidth | Bulky; sensitive to external magnetic fields; requires carrier oscillator |
| **Optical (Opto-coupler)** | LED emits light; photodiode/transistor receives | Very small size; complete electrical isolation; no magnetic interference | Non-linear LED transfer curve; limited bandwidth; aging/temperature drift |
| **Capacitive** | High-frequency signal couples across a small HV capacitor | High speed; integrated in CMOS; low cost | Lower isolation voltage rating; sensitive to EMI; needs modulator/demodulator |

**Best for Biomedical:** Transformer-coupled (most robust, highest safety rating).

**(b) Definition and Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):** It is the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in dB. It quantifies the amplifier's ability to reject signals that appear simultaneously on both inputs.

$$
\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)\,\text{dB}
$$

**IMRR (Isolation-Mode Rejection Ratio):** It is the ratio of the isolation-mode voltage (V_iso) appearing across the isolation barrier to the resulting output voltage $V_{out}$, expressed in dB. It quantifies the suppression of voltages appearing *across the barrier itself*.

$$
\text{IMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{iso}}{V_{out}}\right)\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 1 — Output Signal due to Differential Input**

Assume a nominal differential gain $A_d = 100$ (typical for the front-end of an isolation amplifier). The differential output is:

$$
V_{out,d} = A_d \cdot v_d = 100 \times 1\,\text{mV} = 100\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Output Signal due to Common-Mode Voltage**

CMRR = 110 dB implies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} &= 10^{110/20} = 10^{5.5} = 3.16 \times 10^5 \\[4pt]
A_{cm} &= \frac{A_d}{3.16 \times 10^5} = \frac{100}{3.16 \times 10^5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
V_{out,cm} &= A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} = 3.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 10\,\text{V} = 3.16\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Output (assuming $V_{out,cm}$ appears as unwanted noise)**

$$
V_{out,total} \approx V_{out,d} + V_{out,cm} = 100 + 3.16 \approx 103.16\,\text{mV}
$$

The desired signal is 100 mV; the common-mode leakage is only 3.16 mV — showing the effectiveness of the high CMRR.

**Step 4 — Isolation-Mode Error**

IMRR = 100 dB implies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{iso}}{V_{out,iso}} &= 10^{100/20} = 10^5 = 100{,}000 \\[4pt]
V_{out,iso} &= \frac{V_{iso}}{100{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

If a 1000 V isolation-mode voltage is present (e.g., mains surge):

$$
V_{out,iso} = \frac{1000\,\text{V}}{100{,}000} = 10\,\text{mV}
$$

This is the maximum spurious output due to the isolation voltage.

**Final Answer:** Differential output ≈ 100 mV; common-mode leakage ≈ 3.16 mV; isolation-mode error (for 1000 V across barrier) = 10 mV.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "$2R_1/R_G$" factor:** Many students write $A_1 = 1 + R_1/R_G$ (single-op-amp non-inverting formula). The instrumentation-amp front-end has a $2R_1/R_G$ — losing **1 mark** if wrong.
> 2. **CMRR units confusion:** CMRR and IMRR are dimensionless ratios expressed in **dB**. Use $20 \log_{10}$, NOT $10 \log_{10}$ (that's for power). Mixed units cost **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing IMRR with CMRR:** IMRR is for *isolation-mode* (across barrier); CMRR is for *common-mode* (between inputs). Examiners **always** test this distinction.
> 4. **Block diagram completeness:** A block diagram *without* the isolated DC-DC power port is incomplete — you will lose **2 marks** for omitting the three-port architecture.
> 5. **Mixing up leakage current limits:** Macroshock = 1 mA threshold; Microshock = 10 µA. Use **10 µA** for isolation amplifier specifications.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **isolation amplifier** provides **complete galvanic isolation** between input, output, and power ports.
- The **isolation barrier** is a physical gap — **no wire crosses it**. Signal crosses by **magnetic, optical, or capacitive** means.
- The **three most common methods** are: **transformer**, **optical (opto-coupler)**, and **capacitive** coupling.
- The **transformer-coupled type** uses **duty-cycle modulation** (or amplitude modulation) of a **~50 kHz carrier**.
- **Gain equation:** $A_T = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$ for the standard transformer-coupled ISO-series ICs.
- **CMRR (dB)** = $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$; **IMRR (dB)** = $20 \log_{10}(V_{iso} / V_{out})$.
- **Critical medical specs:** Isolation voltage **≥ 1500 V RMS**, leakage current **< 10 µA**, CMRR/IMRR **> 100 dB**.
- The **DC-DC isolated converter** is *part of the isolation system* — it powers the input side from the system supply without breaking isolation.
- **Applications:** ECG, EEG, EMG, ICU monitors, defibrillator-protected circuits, cardiac catheterization labs, electrosurgery, and any patient-connected equipment.
- **Why mandatory in KTU biomedical syllabus:** Because it is the **single most important safety component** between a patient and an instrument, mandated by **IEC 60601-1**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: CHOPPER AMPLIFIERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *Chopper Amplifiers are a class of indirect-coupled (AC) amplifiers used to amplify extremely small DC or very low-frequency signals (in the microvolt to millivolt range) by first converting (chopping/modulating) them into an AC signal, amplifying the AC signal using a drift-free AC amplifier, and then demodulating (synchronous detection) and low-pass filtering the amplified signal back to its original baseband form.*

### 1.1 Why Chopper Amplifiers? — The Real Engineering Problem

In **Biomedical Instrumentation**, the signals we routinely deal with (ECG ≈ 1 mV, EEG ≈ 50 µV, EMG ≈ 100 µV, EOG ≈ even smaller) are **very low-frequency (0.05 Hz – 150 Hz) signals** sitting dangerously close to **DC**. 

If we try to amplify them with a plain **direct-coupled (DC) amplifier**, we hit two killer enemies:

1. **Drift:** Slow, unwanted variation of the output with temperature, time, or supply voltage that gets amplified along with the signal — it can completely swamp a 50 µV EEG signal.
2. **1/f Noise (Flicker Noise):** Semiconductor noise whose power density is **inversely proportional to frequency** — maximum at DC, exactly where our biosignals live!

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering into a Noisy Room"**
> Imagine you have a very soft whisper (your 50 µV EEG signal) and you're in a noisy room. The room has a low-frequency rumble (drift + 1/f noise) that drowns your whisper. Now, if you and your listener both put on a special **pulsing flashlight** that turns ON and OFF at a known fast rate (say, 1000 Hz), and you whisper **only when the light is ON**, your listener can now:
> 1. **Synchronize** their hearing to the flashing light.
> 2. **Ignore** all the low-frequency rumble because they only listen during the bright moments.
> 3. **Convert** the pulsed whisper back into a continuous one.
>
> The flashlight-pulsing is exactly what a **chopper** does to the signal — it converts the DC/low-frequency signal into a higher-frequency AC signal (the chopper frequency $f_c$), where we can amplify it cheaply and cleanly with AC-coupled (drift-free) amplifiers, before turning it back into DC. 🎯

### 1.2 Classification of Choppers Used in Biomedical Front-Ends

| Chopper Type | Operating Principle | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Chopper** | Electromagnetic vibrating reed (50/60 Hz mains-driven SPDT switch) | Excellent stability, ultra-low noise, decades-proven | Bulky, contact bounce, limited life, EMI from coil |
| **Transistor Chopper (BJT / FET)** | Electronic switching of transistor between saturation and cutoff using a square wave | Compact, no moving parts, long life, fast switching | Tiny offset voltage, charge injection, switching transients |
| **Photo-Chopper (Optical)** | LED + photoconductive cell (CdS/LDR) switched by light pulse | Total galvanic isolation, very low drift | Bulky, slow, temperature-sensitive cell |
| **MOSFET Chopper** | Modern CMOS switch — gate driven by chopper clock | Near-zero offset, IC-friendly, low power | Charge injection, clock feedthrough |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** In **CMOS chopper-stabilized op-amps** (e.g., TI OPA2188, ADA4522), the chopping frequency $f_c$ is typically 5 kHz – 20 kHz, well above the 1/f corner of the input transistors, but well below the biosignal bandwidth.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Functional Blocks of a Chopper Amplifier

A classical chopper amplifier is a **cascade of three signal-processing stages** plus a clock:

$$\boxed{\; v_{in}(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Chopper} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{AC Amplifier} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Demodulator} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{LPF} \;\longrightarrow\; v_{out}(t)\;}$$

#### Stage 1 — The Chopper (Modulator)
A chopper is essentially a **single-pole double-throw (SPDT) switch** that is flipped at a fixed **chopper frequency $f_c$** by a square-wave clock $c(t)$. The switch alternately connects the input signal to the amplifier input, with the polarity reversed on alternate half-cycles.

Mathematically, the input is **multiplied** by a square wave $m(t)$ of frequency $f_c$:

$$v_1(t) = v_{in}(t) \cdot m(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Why Multiplication?):** When you multiply a time-domain signal by a square wave, you are performing **amplitude modulation**. The baseband spectrum of $v_{in}(t)$ is shifted up to the chopper frequency $f_c$ and its odd harmonics ($3f_c, 5f_c, \ldots$). The original DC/low-frequency content is now carried by an AC waveform — **drift-free territory!**

#### Stage 2 — The AC Amplifier
A **capacitor-coupled (AC) amplifier** with high gain $A_v$ amplifies the chopped signal. Because the signal is now AC at $f_c$, the AC amplifier exhibits:
- **No DC drift** (capacitor blocks DC offsets)
- **Minimal 1/f noise** (signal is well above the 1/f corner)
- **Stable gain** (no thermal runaway)

$$v_2(t) = A_v \cdot v_1(t) = A_v \cdot v_{in}(t) \cdot m(t)$$

#### Stage 3 — The Synchronous Demodulator
Another SPDT switch, **synchronized in phase** with the chopper, multiplies $v_2(t)$ by the *same* square wave $m(t)$:

$$v_3(t) = v_2(t) \cdot m(t) = A_v \cdot v_{in}(t) \cdot [m(t)]^2$$

Because $[m(t)]^2 = 1$ for a perfect $\pm 1$ square wave, we have:

$$v_3(t) = A_v \cdot v_{in}(t)$$

The original signal is **fully recovered**, amplified, but now sitting on a baseband pedestal (low frequency recovered perfectly) along with **high-frequency ripple at $2f_c, 4f_c, \ldots$**.

#### Stage 4 — The Low-Pass Filter (LPF)
A simple **RC low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_{LPF}$ removes the high-frequency ripple components, leaving a clean amplified replica of $v_{in}(t)$:

$$v_{out}(t) \;\approx\; A_v \cdot v_{in}(t), \quad \text{for} \; f_{signal} \ll f_{LPF} \ll 2f_c$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every equation** you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic. Pay close attention to units and frequency-domain relations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulation step (chopper) | $v_1(t) = v_{in}(t)\cdot m(t)$ | Shifts spectrum of $v_{in}$ to $f_c$ |
| 2 | Square wave Fourier series (odd harmonics only) | $m(t) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{n}\sin(2\pi n f_c t)$ | Carrier waveform is a $\pm 1$ square wave |
| 3 | Single-tone input $v_{in}(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_s t)$ → output of chopper | $v_1(t) = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c-f_s)t\big) + \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c+f_s)t\big) + \ldots$ | Frequency translation to $\pm f_c$ (DSB-SC AM) |
| 4 | AC amplifier gain | $v_2(t) = A_v \cdot v_1(t)$ | Drift-free, low 1/f noise region |
| 5 | Demodulation step | $v_3(t) = v_2(t)\cdot m(t)$ | Restores baseband + ripple at $2f_c$ |
| 6 | Property of square wave | $m^2(t) = 1$ | Means perfect synchronous detection recovers the input |
| 7 | Final LPF cutoff condition | $f_{signal,\max} \ll f_{LPF} \ll 2f_c$ | Avoids aliasing & removes chopper ripple |
| 8 | RC LPF time constant | $\tau = RC \Rightarrow f_{LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Ripple rejection at $2f_c$: attenuation $\geq 40$ dB |
| 9 | Chopper-stabilized effective offset | $V_{os,eff} \approx \dfrac{V_{os,DC}}{A_1 \cdot A_2}$ | Where $A_1, A_2$ are gain of main + aux paths |
| 10 | Required $f_c$ for biomedical apps | $f_c \gg f_{1/f,corner}$, $f_c < f_{unity,opamp}$ | Typical: $f_c \in [5\text{ kHz},\; 20\text{ kHz}]$ |
| 11 | CMRR improvement factor | $CMRR_{chop} \approx CMRR_{opamp} \cdot 2f_c / f_s$ | Chopping boosts CMRR for low $f_s$ |
| 12 | Noise density at low freq (chopped) | $S_{n,out}(f) \approx S_{n,opamp}(f) + \dfrac{S_{n,chopper}}{f_c^2}$ | 1/f noise up-converted and filtered out |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often write $m(t) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\left[\sin(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{1}{3}\sin(2\pi 3f_c t) + \ldots\right]$ with **even** harmonics. The square wave contains **ONLY ODD HARMONICS** — $n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \ldots$. Loss of 1 mark guaranteed if you include $n=2, 4, 6, \ldots$

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

Chopper amplifiers are the **invisible workhorses** behind:
- **ECG/EEG/EMG front-ends** (e.g., AD620, INA128 instrument amp stages)
- **Strain-gauge bridge amplifiers** for blood-pressure transducers
- **Thermocouple amplifiers** for patient temperature monitoring
- **pH meter amplifiers** in blood-gas analyzers
- **CMOS chopper op-amps** (LMP7701, AD8628) in wearable/patch biosensors
