While Einthoven's original model used only the **bipolar** leads, the triangle is later extended by **Emanuel Goldberger (1942)** to add three **augmented unipolar leads**:

- **aVR** (Augmented Vector Right) — explores the right shoulder region
- **aVL** (Augmented Vector Left) — explores the left shoulder region
- **aVF** (Augmented Vector Foot) — explores the inferior (foot/diaphragm) region

These are derived from the bipolar leads using the formulas in the cheat sheet above and together with the original three leads form the complete **hexaxial reference system** of modern ECG.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

**Starting Point:** The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) and the body tissue form a closed electrical loop, since the human body is a continuous volume conductor.

**Step 1 — Define the bipolar lead potentials** as voltage differences:

$$V_{I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the closed loop RA → LA → LL → RA. The algebraic sum of potential differences around any closed loop in a conservative field must equal zero:

$$(V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) + (V_{RA} - V_{LL}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the lead definitions into the KVL equation.** The first parenthesis is $V_I$, the second is $V_{III}$, and the third is the negative of $V_{II}$:

$$V_{I} + V_{III} + (V_{RA} - V_{LL}) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Note that $(V_{RA} - V_{LL}) = -(V_{LL} - V_{RA}) = -V_{II}$.** Substituting:

$$V_{I} + V_{III} - V_{II} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain Einthoven's Law:**

$$\boxed{V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}}$$

**Logical Interpretation:** This equation is not a coincidence — it is a **topological necessity** arising from the closed-loop nature of the body conductor. Any two leads can be used to *calculate* the third, which is the basis of lead redundancy in clinical ECG machines.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Augmented Lead Equations (Goldberger's Equations)

**Background:** In a unipolar lead configuration, the *exploring* electrode measures the potential at one vertex, while the *reference* is the average of the other two vertices.

**Step 1 — Compute the unipolar potential at the Right Arm (without augmentation):**

$$V_{R} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $V_{RA}$ in terms of bipolar leads.** From the definitions:
- $V_{I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA} \Rightarrow V_{LA} = V_{I} + V_{RA}$
- $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} \Rightarrow V_{LL} = V_{II} + V_{RA}$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the unipolar expression:**

$$V_{R} = V_{RA} - \frac{(V_{I} + V_{RA}) + (V_{II} + V_{RA})}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the numerator and simplify:**

$$V_{R} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{I} + V_{II} + 2V_{RA}}{2}$$

$$V_{R} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{I}}{2} - \frac{V_{II}}{2} - V_{RA}$$

$$V_{R} = -\frac{V_{I} + V_{II}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Goldberger's augmentation factor** (the signal is amplified by 1.5× to compensate for the reduced amplitude caused by using two reference electrodes). The augmented unipolar lead is therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{aVR} = -\frac{V_{I} + V_{II}}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply identical reasoning to the other two vertices.** By symmetry of construction:

$$\boxed{V_{aVL} = \frac{V_{I} - V_{III}}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{aVF} = \frac{V_{II} + V_{III}}{2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Mean Electrical Axis from the Einthoven Triangle

**Step 1 — Resolve the cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ into rectangular components** using Leads I and III (which are orthogonal to aVF but conventionally used in the simple method):

$$M_{x} = V_{I}$$

$$M_{y} = \frac{V_{II} - V_{I} \cdot \cos(60°)}{\sin(60°)} = \frac{V_{II} - 0.5 V_{I}}{0.866}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean QRS axis angle** in the frontal plane:

$$\boxed{\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{M_{y}}{M_{x}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2V_{II} - V_{I}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{I}}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Classify the result** clinically:

| Axis Range | Clinical Term |
|:----------:|:-------------:|
| $-30°$ to $+100°$ | **Normal axis** |
| $+100°$ to $+180°$ | **Right axis deviation (RAD)** |
| $-30°$ to $-90°$ | **Left axis deviation (LAD)** |
| $-90°$ to $+180°$ (indeterminate) | **Extreme axis deviation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip for Derivation Questions:** In KTU exam answers, always state KVL explicitly, then identify which terms correspond to which leads, and only then perform the algebraic simplification. Examiners award method marks generously for showing the **reasoning chain**, not just the final boxed result.

## 3.4 Analytical Verification of the Equilateral Geometry

**Proof that inter-lead angle = 60°:** Consider the cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ at the centroid. The lead vectors $\vec{l}_I$, $\vec{l}_{II}$, $\vec{l}_{III}$ are directed along the sides of the triangle (from the negative to the positive electrode). Since the triangle is equilateral and the centroid is the center, the angle subtended at the centroid between any two adjacent vertices is $360°/3 = 120°$. The lead vectors are perpendicular to the lines joining the centroid to the vertices (projection geometry), so the angle between adjacent lead vectors is $180° - 120° = 60°$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Einthoven Triangle — Mermaid Block Diagram

Below is a block-level functional architecture diagram representing the Einthoven Triangle, its three vertices, the bipolar leads as connecting edges, and the central cardiac dipole.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[RA RIGHT ARM VERTEX] -->|LEAD I POSITIVE END| nodeB[LA LEFT ARM VERTEX]
    nodeB -->|LEAD III POSITIVE END| nodeC[LL LEFT LEG VERTEX]
    nodeC -->|LEAD II POSITIVE END| nodeA
    nodeD((CARDIAC DIPOLE M)) --- nodeA
    nodeD --- nodeB
    nodeD --- nodeC
    nodeA -->|V EQUALS VLA MINUS VRA| nodeE[LEAD I AXIS ZERO DEGREES]
    nodeA -->|V EQUALS VLL MINUS VRA| nodeF[LEAD II AXIS PLUS SIXTY DEGREES]
    nodeB -->|V EQUALS VLL MINUS VLA| nodeG[LEAD III AXIS PLUS ONE TWENTY DEGREES]
    nodeH[RL RIGHT LEG GROUND REFERENCE] -.->|CMRR ENHANCEMENT| nodeD
    nodeI[EINTHOVEN LAW V II EQUALS V I PLUS V III] -.-> nodeE
    nodeI -.-> nodeF
    nodeI -.-> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Hexaxial Reference System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisI[LEAD I ZERO DEG] --- axisII[LEAD II PLUS SIXTY DEG]
    axisII --- axisIII[LEAD III PLUS ONE TWENTY DEG]
    axisIII --- axisAVR[aVR MINUS ONE FIFTY DEG]
    axisAVR --- axisAVL[aVL MINUS THIRTY DEG]
    axisAVL --- axisAVF[aVF PLUS NINETY DEG]
    axisAVF --- axisI
    axisN((NORMAL RANGE MINUS THIRTY TO PLUS ONE HUNDRED)) --- axisI
    axisRAD((RIGHT AXIS DEV PLUS ONE HUNDRED TO PLUS ONE EIGHTY)) --- axisII
    axisLAD((LEFT AXIS DEV MINUS THIRTY TO MINUS NINETY)) --- axisAVL
```

## 4.3 ECG Lead Signal-Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    src1[RA ELECTRODE] --> diffAmp1[Differential Amplifier 1]
    src2[LA ELECTRODE] --> diffAmp1
    src3[LL ELECTRODE] --> diffAmp2[Differential Amplifier 2]
    src1 --> diffAmp2
    src3 --> diffAmp3[Differential Amplifier 3]
    src2 --> diffAmp3
    src4[RL GROUND] --> cmfb[Common Mode Feedback Network]
    diffAmp1 --> leadI[LEAD I OUTPUT]
    diffAmp2 --> leadII[LEAD II OUTPUT]
    diffAmp3 --> leadIII[LEAD III OUTPUT]
    leadI --> adder[Digital Summation Check]
    leadIII --> adder
    adder -->|VERIFICATION| ecgLaw{EINTHOVEN LAW SATISFIED}
    ecgLaw -->|YES| display[ECG DISPLAY]
    ecgLaw -->|NO| alarm[Electrode Fault Alarm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The signal-flow architecture above mirrors the actual hardware topology inside a clinical ECG machine. The Einthoven Triangle is not merely a theoretical abstraction — it is physically implemented in the analog front-end using **instrumentation amplifiers** that compute $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$, $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$, and $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ in real time. The consistency check on $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ is performed digitally in modern devices.

## 4.4 Cardiac Vector Projection onto Lead Axes

```mermaid
graph TD
    heartVec[CARDIAC VECTOR M] --> projI[Projection onto Lead I Axis]
    heartVec --> projII[Projection onto Lead II Axis]
    heartVec --> projIII[Projection onto Lead III Axis]
    projI --> vIcalc[V I EQUALS M COS ALPHA]
    projII --> vIIcalc[V II EQUALS M COS ALPHA MINUS SIXTY]
    projIII --> vIIIcalc[V III EQUALS M COS ALPHA PLUS SIXTY]
    vIcalc --> einthoven[EINTHOVEN TRIANGLE]
    vIIcalc --> einthoven
    vIIIcalc --> einthoven
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** Define the Einthoven Triangle. What are the assumptions made in Einthoven's model of ECG?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The Einthoven Triangle is an imaginary equilateral triangle formed by placing the three limb electrodes — Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), and Left Leg (LL) — at its vertices, with the heart assumed to be located at the geometric centroid. Each side of the triangle represents the axis of one of the three standard bipolar limb leads (Lead I, Lead II, Lead III).

**Assumptions:**
1. The human body acts as a **homogeneous** volume conductor (uniform electrical properties).
2. The body is **isotropic** (conductivity is the same in all directions).
3. The heart's electrical activity can be represented as a **single equivalent current dipole** at the centroid.
4. The triangle is perfectly **equilateral**, with the electrodes equidistant from the heart.

### Question 2
**Q:** State and prove Einthoven's Law.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Einthoven's Law:** $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$

**Proof:** By definition, $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$, $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$, $V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$. Adding $V_I$ and $V_{III}$:

$$V_I + V_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}$$

Therefore $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$, which is Einthoven's Law. It is a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the closed electrical loop of the body.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the Einthoven Triangle and label all three leads, their polarities, and the standard axis angles. Explain the relationship between the cardiac vector and the recorded lead potentials. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for the augmented unipolar limb leads $V_{aVR}$, $V_{aVL}$, and $V_{aVF}$ in terms of the bipolar lead potentials $V_I$, $V_{II}$, and $V_{III}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
                    LA (Lead I +)
                     /\
                    /  \
                   /    \
       Lead III  /  ♥    \  Lead II
      (LA→LL)   /  Heart   \  (RA→LL)
               /   Centroid \
              /              \
             /________________\
            RA (–)         LL (+)
        (Lead I & II –)   (Lead II & III +)
```

**Axis Angles:**
- Lead I: **0°** (directed from RA to LA, i.e., leftward)
- Lead II: **+60°** (directed from RA to LL, i.e., leftward and downward)
- Lead III: **+120°** (directed from LA to LL, i.e., rightward and downward)

**Relationship to Cardiac Vector:** The recorded lead potential equals the dot product of the cardiac dipole vector $\vec{M}$ with the unit lead vector $\vec{l}_k$:

$$V_k = \vec{M} \cdot \vec{l}_k = \vert \vec{M} \vert \cos(\theta_k)$$

where $\theta_k$ is the angle between the cardiac vector and the lead axis. Thus each lead records the **projection** of the heart's electrical activity onto its own measurement axis.

#### Model Solution to (b):

From Goldberger's theory, the unipolar potential at any limb electrode $X$ is the difference between the potential at $X$ and the average of the other two electrodes:

$$V_X = V_X - \frac{V_Y + V_Z}{2}$$

**Derivation for $V_{aVR}$** (at Right Arm, with $X = RA$, $Y = LA$, $Z = LL$):

$$V_{R} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

Using $V_{LA} = V_{I} + V_{RA}$ and $V_{LL} = V_{II} + V_{RA}$:

$$V_{R} = V_{RA} - \frac{(V_{I} + V_{RA}) + (V_{II} + V_{RA})}{2} = -\frac{V_{I} + V_{II}}{2}$$

Applying the 1.5× augmentation: $V_{aVR} = -\frac{V_{I} + V_{II}}{2}$

**By symmetry:**

$$V_{aVL} = \frac{V_{I} - V_{III}}{2}, \quad V_{aVF} = \frac{V_{II} + V_{III}}{2}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the mean electrical axis. Using the Einthoven Triangle, derive the formula to compute the mean QRS axis from Leads I and III. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient ECG records the following QRS amplitudes: Lead I = +0.8 mV, Lead III = +0.4 mV. Calculate the mean QRS axis angle. Classify it as normal, right-deviated, or left-deviated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Mean Electrical Axis:** It is the average direction of the cardiac depolarization wavefront in the **frontal plane**, derived from the area under the QRS complex. The Einthoven Triangle provides the geometric framework to determine this axis.

**Derivation:** Place the cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ at the centroid of the triangle. The projection of $\vec{M}$ onto the Lead I axis (0°) gives the x-component:

$$M_x = V_{I} = \vert \vec{M} \vert \cos\alpha$$

The Lead III axis is at +120° from Lead I, so the projection onto the vertical (y-axis) involves the y-component:

$$V_{III} = M_x \cos(120°) + M_y \sin(120°)$$

Solving for $M_y$:

$$M_y = \frac{V_{III} - V_{I} \cos(120°)}{\sin(120°)} = \frac{V_{III} + 0.5 V_{I}}{0.866}$$

The mean axis angle is:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{M_y}{M_x}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2V_{III} + V_{I}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{I}}\right)}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $V_{I} = +0.8$ mV, $V_{III} = +0.4$ mV

**Step 1 — Compute the x-component:** $M_x = V_I = +0.8$ mV

**Step 2 — Compute the y-component:**

$$M_y = \frac{V_{III} + 0.5 V_I}{0.866} = \frac{0.4 + 0.5 \times 0.8}{0.866} = \frac{0.8}{0.866} = 0.924 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the axis angle:**

$$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{0.924}{0.8}\right) = \arctan(1.155) = +49.1°$$

**Step 4 — Classification:** Since $+49.1°$ lies within $-30°$ to $+100°$, the axis is **NORMAL**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often forget that Lead III is the projection on the +120° axis and mistakenly use the wrong trigonometric identity. Always re-derive carefully.
> 2. **Units Confusion:** Lead potentials in the formula are in **mV**, but clinical convention reports ECG amplitudes in **mm** (using the 10 mm/mV calibration). Always state your unit conversion explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the Augmentation Factor:** In derivation questions, students derive $V_{aVR} = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2}$ but forget that the Goldberger augmentation multiplies this by 1.5×. Either include the factor or state the *unaugmented* potential explicitly to avoid losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Mis-drawing Polarity Arrows:** The Einthoven Triangle's arrows must point *toward the positive electrode*. Reversed arrows will lose 1 full mark in diagram-based questions.
> 5. **Skipping the Assumptions:** Whenever the Einthoven Triangle is asked, examiners expect the **four assumptions** to be listed. Skipping them costs a full mark even if the rest of the answer is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Einthoven Triangle**

- ✅ The **Einthoven Triangle** is an **equilateral triangle** with vertices at **RA, LA, and LL**, and the **heart at the centroid**.
- ✅ Each side of the triangle represents one **bipolar limb lead** (I, II, or III).
- ✅ The triangle is based on **four idealizing assumptions**: homogeneous, isotropic, point-dipole, equilateral.
- ✅ **Einthoven's Law:** $\mathbf{V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}}$ — the single most important formula, derived from **KVL**.
- ✅ **Lead directions:** Lead I → 0°, Lead II → +60°, Lead III → +120°.
- ✅ **Augmented (unipolar) leads** extend the model: aVR, aVL, aVF (Goldberger, 1942).
- ✅ **Goldberger's equations:** $V_{aVR} = -\frac{V_{I} + V_{II}}{2}$, $V_{aVL} = \frac{V_{I} - V_{III}}{2}$, $V_{aVF} = \frac{V_{II} + V_{III}}{2}$.
- ✅ **Normal QRS axis range:** **−30° to +100°** in the frontal plane.
- ✅ **Axis deviation classification:** Right axis deviation (+100° to +180°), Left axis deviation (−30° to −90°), Extreme axis deviation (rest).
- ✅ Each lead measures the **scalar projection** of the cardiac vector onto its axis: $V_k = \vec{M} \cdot \vec{l}_k$.
- ✅ **Inter-lead angle** is always **60°** due to equilateral geometry (180° − 120° at centroid).
- ✅ **Engineering application:** Lead redundancy check $V_{II} \approx V_I + V_{III}$ is used to detect electrode faults in modern ECG machines.
- ✅ The **Right Leg (RL) electrode** is the **ground reference** for common-mode rejection of mains interference (50/60 Hz).
- ✅ **Standard ECG calibration:** 10 mm/mV vertical, 25 mm/s horizontal.
- ✅ Willem Einthoven received the **1924 Nobel Prize** for this foundational work on ECG.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrocardiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrocardiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiography (ECG / EKG)** is the process of graphically recording the **bioelectric potentials** generated by the electrical activity of the cardiac muscle (myocardium) over a period of time, using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest wall. The instrument used to perform this recording is called an **Electrocardiograph**, and the resulting tracing is the **Electrocardiogram**.

The technique was pioneered by **Willem Einthoven** (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, **1924**), who introduced the string galvanometer and formulated the foundational laws governing lead relationships. Modern ECG machines digitize these bioelectric signals, which originate from the depolarization and repolarization cycles of cardiac cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ECG Frequency Range:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**
> **Standard Sensitivity:** **10 mm/mV**
> **Standard Paper Speed:** **25 mm/s** (sometimes 50 mm/s)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Heart as a Battery

Imagine your heart as a **tiny battery** floating inside a saltwater tank (your body, which is filled with conductive ionic fluid). Every single heartbeat is a brief, coordinated "discharge" of this battery as millions of cardiac cells fire in sequence.

- The **SA Node** acts like the **switch** that initiates the discharge.
- The **electrical impulse** spreads outward through the muscle like ripples in a pond.
- The **electrodes** placed on your skin are like **voltmeter probes** dipped into the tank at specific points.
- The **ECG machine** is simply a sensitive voltmeter that records how this voltage changes with time.

When the heart's "battery polarity" is parallel to the electrode pair, a large deflection is seen; when perpendicular, the deflection is zero. The resulting graph is the **Electrocardiogram** — a literal signature of the heart's electrical life.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not measure the heart's mechanical activity instead?** Because electrical activity *precedes* mechanical contraction by a fraction of a second. Thus, ECG is a leading indicator and can diagnose conditions like ischemia, arrhythmia, and infarction long before structural damage becomes visible in imaging.

## 1.3 Origin of the ECG Signal: Cardiac Dipole

The heart, at any instant, behaves as an **equivalent electric dipole** located near its center, with a magnitude and orientation (vector) that changes throughout the cardiac cycle. The ECG is a projection of this time-varying dipole vector onto the **lead axis** defined by a pair of measurement electrodes.

$$\vec{M}(t) = \text{Instantaneous cardiac dipole moment}$$

Each ECG lead "sees" only one component of this vector — namely, the component along its lead axis. This is why **12 different leads** are required to reconstruct the full 3-D picture of the heart's electrical activity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heart's Electrical Conduction System

The cardiac cycle is orchestrated by a precisely timed sequence of electrical events. Understanding this is essential because each ECG wave corresponds to a specific physiological event.

| Structure | Location | Function | ECG Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SA Node** (Sinoatrial Node) | Right atrium, near SVC | Primary pacemaker (~70–80 bpm); initiates impulse | Origin of P wave |
| **AV Node** (Atrioventricular Node) | Interatrial septum | Delays impulse (~0.1 s) for atrial emptying | PR interval |
| **Bundle of His** | Interventricular septum | Conducts impulse to ventricles | Hidden within PR |
| **Right & Left Bundle Branches** | Along interventricular septum | Distribute impulse to each ventricle | Beginning of QRS |
| **Purkinje Fibers** | Subendocardium of ventricles | Rapid ventricular activation | Completion of QRS |
| **Ventricular Myocardium** | Bulk of ventricles | Mechanical contraction | ST segment / T wave |

## 2.2 The ECG Waveform — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A normal ECG cycle (one heartbeat) consists of the following deflections, intervals, and segments:

- **P Wave:** Small, rounded, upright deflection caused by **atrial depolarization**. Duration: **< 0.12 s**, Amplitude: **< 2.5 mm**.
- **PR Interval:** Time from onset of P wave to onset of QRS complex. Represents the conduction delay through the AV node. Normal: **0.12 s to 0.20 s**.
- **QRS Complex:** Sharp, tall deflection representing **ventricular depolarization**. Duration: **0.06 s to 0.10 s**.
  - **Q Wave:** Initial downward deflection (small).
  - **R Wave:** First upward deflection (large).
  - **S Wave:** Downward deflection following R.
- **ST Segment:** Flat (isoelectric) region between QRS end and T wave start. Represents the **plateau phase** of ventricular action potential. Normal duration: **0.08 s to 0.12 s**.
- **T Wave:** Smooth, asymmetric upward deflection due to **ventricular repolarization**.
- **QT Interval:** Total time of ventricular depolarization + repolarization. Normal: **0.36 s to 0.44 s** (heart-rate dependent).
- **U Wave:** Small, rounded deflection sometimes seen after T wave; origin is debated (possibly Purkinje fiber repolarization).

## 2.3 The 12-Lead ECG System

Modern ECG uses **12 leads** — these are not 12 separate cables, but **12 different "viewpoints"** of the same 10 electrodes.

### A. Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Leads)
- **Lead I:** Left Arm (LA) is positive, Right Arm (RA) is negative. Records potential difference: $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$
- **Lead II:** Left Leg (LL) is positive, Right Arm (RA) is negative. $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$
- **Lead III:** Left Leg (LL) is positive, Left Arm (LA) is negative. $V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$

### B. Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger's Leads)
- **aVR:** Augmented Vector Right — explores the right shoulder.
- **aVL:** Augmented Vector Left — explores the left shoulder.
- **aVF:** Augmented Vector Foot — explores the inferior wall (left leg).

### C. Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson's Leads)
- **V1:** 4th intercostal space, right sternal border.
- **V2:** 4th intercostal space, left sternal border.
- **V3:** Midway between V2 and V4.
- **V4:** 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line.
- **V5:** Anterior axillary line, same level as V4.
- **V6:** Mid-axillary line, same level as V4.

## 2.4 Einthoven's Triangle & Einthoven's Law

The three bipolar limb leads form a closed triangle (Einthoven's Triangle) at the heart's center. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$$

This is **Einthoven's Law** — a cornerstone relationship in vector cardiography.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ | Einthoven's Law (vector sum) | mV |
| $V_I + V_{III} - V_{II} = 0$ | Einthoven's Law rearranged | mV |
| $\text{Time (s)} = \dfrac{\text{Distance (mm)}}{25}$ | Time duration (at 25 mm/s) | seconds |
| $\text{Time (s)} = \dfrac{\text{Distance (mm)}}{50}$ | Time duration (at 50 mm/s) | seconds |
| $\text{Voltage (mV)} = \dfrac{\text{Amplitude (mm)}}{10}$ | Voltage (at 10 mm/mV) | mV |
| $\text{Heart Rate (BPM)} = \dfrac{60}{R\text{-}R\ \text{interval (s)}}$ | Heart rate from R-R | bpm |
| $\text{Heart Rate (BPM)} = \dfrac{1500}{R\text{-}R\ \text{ (small boxes)}}$ | Heart rate (small box method) | bpm |
| $\tan\theta = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}\,(V_I + V_{III})}$ | Mean electrical axis (in frontal plane) | degrees |
| $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Cutoff frequency of filter | Hz |
| $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common Mode Rejection Ratio | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - **PR interval:** 0.12 to 0.20 s (4 to 5 small boxes)
> - **QRS duration:** 0.06 to 0.10 s (1.5 to 2.5 small boxes)
> - **QT interval:** 0.36 to 0.44 s (corrected: QTc < 0.44 s in men, < 0.46 s in women)
> - **Normal heart rate:** 60 to 100 bpm
> - **Normal mean electrical axis:** $-30°$ to $+100°$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

ECG technology is foundational to:
- **Cardiac diagnostics** in hospitals and clinics.
- **Ambulatory monitoring** (Holter monitors for 24–48 hr recordings).
- **Implantable devices** (pacemakers, ICDs sense ECG internally).
- **Wearable fitness trackers** (Apple Watch, Fitbit).
- **Telemetry and ICU bedside monitors** with arrhythmia detection.
- **Stress test systems** and **fetal ECG monitors**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The ECG's tiny amplitude (**0.5 mV to 5 mV**) and low frequency make it highly susceptible to **50/60 Hz power-line interference**, **EMG artifacts**, and **baseline wander**. ECG amplifier design demands **high input impedance**, **high CMRR (> 100 dB)**, **low noise**, and **galvanic patient isolation** for safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

**Statement to prove:** $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$

**Given:** Three electrodes placed on the right arm (RA), left arm (LA), and left leg (LL). Let their potentials with respect to ground be $V_{RA}$, $V_{LA}$, and $V_{LL}$ respectively.

**Step 1:** Write expressions for the three bipolar leads by definition of potential difference.

By the standard definitions of the bipolar limb leads (Einthoven, 1903):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_I   &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} \quad &\text{(Lead I: LA positive, RA negative)} \\
V_{II}  &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \quad &\text{(Lead II: LL positive, RA negative)} \\
V_{III} &= V_{LL} - V_{LA} \quad &\text{(Lead III: LL positive, LA negative)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Add Lead I and Lead III potentials algebraically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_I + V_{III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\
              &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\
              &= -V_{RA} + V_{LL} \\
              &= V_{LL} - V_{RA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the expression for Lead II.

$$
V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}
$$

**Step 4:** Conclude the identity.

$$
\boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Einthoven's Law** — a direct consequence of the principle of superposition. It also implies that the three lead vectors $\vec{I}$, $\vec{II}$, and $\vec{III}$ close upon themselves, forming Einthoven's equilateral triangle. The cardiac dipole vector projects onto each side of this triangle to give the recorded voltage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heart Rate from R–R Interval

**Given:** The R–R interval (time between two consecutive R-wave peaks) measured on the ECG paper is $T_R$ seconds.

**Step 1:** Recognize that one R–R interval corresponds to one complete cardiac cycle (one heartbeat).

**Step 2:** The number of cardiac cycles per second is the reciprocal:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T_R} \;\; \text{[beats per second]}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to beats per minute by multiplying by 60:

$$
\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{60}{T_R} \;\; \text{[BPM]}
$$

**Step 4:** Equivalently, in terms of small boxes (each 1 mm = 0.04 s at 25 mm/s):

$$
\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{60}{0.04 \cdot N} = \frac{1500}{N}
$$

where $N$ is the number of small boxes between consecutive R waves.

**Step 5:** For the "large box" method (each large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s):

$$
\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{300}{M}
$$

where $M$ is the number of large boxes.

**Worked Example:** An ECG strip has an R–R interval of 5 large boxes.

$$
\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{300}{5} = 60 \text{ BPM}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Electrical Axis (Frontal Plane)

**Given:** The mean electrical axis $\theta$ of the heart's dipole in the frontal plane can be calculated from Lead I and Lead III.

**Step 1:** Set up the vector triangle. The lead vectors I, II, and III are 60° apart, with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{I}   &\rightarrow 0° \text{ (horizontal, LA positive)} \\
\vec{II}  &\rightarrow +60° \\
\vec{III} &\rightarrow +120°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The heart's mean vector $\vec{M}$ has components $M_x$ (horizontal) and $M_y$ (vertical):

$$
M_x = M \cos\theta, \quad M_y = M \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Project $\vec{M}$ onto Lead I (horizontal axis):

$$
V_I = M \cos\theta
$$

**Step 4:** Project $\vec{M}$ onto Lead III (which is at 120° from horizontal). The angle between $\vec{M}$ and Lead III is $(120° - \theta)$, so:

$$
V_{III} = M \cos(120° - \theta)
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the cosine angle difference identity:

$$
V_{III} = M [\cos 120° \cos\theta + \sin 120° \sin\theta] = M \left[-\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\theta + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\theta\right]
$$

**Step 6:** Combine the two equations. Solve for $\tan\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_I + V_{III} &= M\cos\theta + M\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\theta + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\theta\right] \\
              &= \tfrac{1}{2}M\cos\theta + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}M\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

For Lead III alone:

$$
V_{III} - \tfrac{1}{2}V_I = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}M\sin\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}M\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}M\cos\theta = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}M\sin\theta
$$

But also $V_I = M\cos\theta$, hence:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{2(V_{III} - \tfrac{1}{2}V_I)}{\sqrt{3}\,V_I} = \frac{2V_{III} - V_I}{\sqrt{3}\,V_I}
$$

Equivalently (in terms of $V_I$ and $V_{II}$):

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{2 V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}\,(V_I + V_{III})}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = \arctan\!\left[\frac{2 V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}\,(V_I + V_{III})}\right]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick clinical shortcut:** The axis is normal if Lead I is positive **and** aVF is positive. If Lead I is positive but aVF is negative, the axis is **left-deviated**. If Lead I is negative but aVF is positive, the axis is **right-deviated**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ECG Amplifier Cutoff Frequency

**Given:** A first-order RC high-pass filter with $R = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$ is used to remove baseline wander.

**Step 1:** Recall the transfer function of an RC high-pass filter:

$$
H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 2:** The cutoff (-3 dB) frequency is where $\vert H(j\omega_c)\vert = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\omega_c RC = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to Hz:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^6 \cdot 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 0.1} \approx 1.59 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: Frequencies below 1.59 Hz (including DC and slow baseline drift) are attenuated, while higher ECG signal frequencies pass through. For ECG, the typical low-frequency cutoff is set near **0.05 Hz** to preserve the ST segment.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ECG Lead Types

| Feature | Bipolar Limb | Augmented Limb | Precordial (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventor** | Einthoven | Goldberger | Wilson |
| **No. of leads** | 3 | 3 | 6 |
| **Electrode configuration** | 2 active, 1 reference | 1 active, modified reference | 1 active, Wilson central terminal |
| **Lead axis orientation** | Frontal plane, 60° apart | Frontal plane, 30° offset | Horizontal/transverse plane |
| **Typical amplitude** | 0.5–2 mV | 0.5–2 mV | 0.5–3 mV |
| **Diagnostic strength** | Axis, rate, rhythm | Specific wall analysis | Anterior / lateral walls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: Normal ECG Waveform — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the time-aligned sequence of physiological events that produce the ECG waveform. Each "node" represents a cardiac event, and the edges indicate the chronological progression of electrical activity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SA Node Fires - Pacemaker Initiation - 70 to 80 BPM"]
    nodeB["Atrial Depolarization - Duration under 0.12 sec"]
    nodeC["AV Node Conduction Delay - 0.10 sec pause"]
    nodeD["Bundle of His and Bundle Branches - Rapid conduction"]
    nodeE["Purkinje Fiber Activation - Ventricular endocardium"]
    nodeF["Ventricular Myocardial Depolarization - QRS complex 0.06 to 0.10 sec"]
    nodeG["Ventricular Plateau Phase - ST segment isoelectric"]
    nodeH["Ventricular Repolarization - T wave asymmetric"]
    nodeI["Diastolic Recovery - Baseline to next cycle"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Figure 2: Block Diagram of a Modern ECG Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Patient Electrodes - 10 placed on limbs and chest"]
    nodeL["Lead Selector Switch - 12 lead configurations"]
    nodeC["Calibration Circuit - 1 mV reference signal"]
    nodePre["Preamplifier - High input impedance, low noise"]
    nodeIso["Isolation Amplifier - Patient safety barrier"]
    nodeMain["Main Amplifier - Gain of 1000 typical"]
    nodeF["Filter Bank - HPF, LPF, Notch at 50 Hz"]
    nodeDrive["Driver Amplifier - Power to display"]
    nodeDisp["Display or Stylus - Real time trace"]
    nodeRec["Recorder and Memory - Digital storage"]
    nodeMon["Monitor with Heart Rate and Alarm"]

    nodeP --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodePre
    nodeC --> nodePre
    nodePre --> nodeIso
    nodeIso --> nodeMain
    nodeMain --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeDrive
    nodeDrive --> nodeDisp
    nodeDisp --> nodeRec
    nodeRec --> nodeMon
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The **isolation amplifier** (often an optocoupler or transformer-based barrier) is critical. It provides **galvanic isolation** of > 5000 V between the patient and the mains-powered circuitry, protecting the patient from microshock and macroshock hazards, especially when used alongside defibrillators or during surgery.

## 4.3 Figure 3: Einthoven's Triangle — Frontal Plane Lead Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["Right Arm RA - Negative pole for Lead I and II"]
    nodeL["Left Arm LA - Positive pole for Lead I, Negative for Lead III"]
    nodeF["Left Leg LL - Positive pole for Lead II and Lead III"]
    nodeC["Cardiac Center - Equivalent dipole source"]
    nodeI["Lead I axis 0 degrees - Horizontal"]
    nodeII["Lead II axis plus 60 degrees"]
    nodeIII["Lead III axis plus 120 degrees"]

    nodeR --- nodeC
    nodeL --- nodeC
    nodeF --- nodeC
    nodeI --- nodeL
    nodeI --- nodeR
    nodeII --- nodeF
    nodeII --- nodeR
    nodeIII --- nodeF
    nodeIII --- nodeL
```

## 4.4 Figure 4: ECG Filtering Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRaw["Raw ECG Signal - 0.05 to 150 Hz plus noise"]
    nodeHPF["High Pass Filter - Cutoff 0.05 Hz - Removes baseline wander"]
    nodeAmp["Amplifier with Gain 1000 - Inverts and amplifies"]
    nodeLPF["Low Pass Filter - Cutoff 150 Hz - Removes high freq noise"]
    nodeNotch["Notch Filter - 50 Hz or 60 Hz - Removes powerline interference"]
    nodeOut["Clean ECG Output - Suitable for ADC and display"]

    nodeRaw --> nodeHPF
    nodeHPF --> nodeAmp
    nodeAmp --> nodeLPF
    nodeLPF --> nodeNotch
    nodeNotch --> nodeOut
```

## 4.5 Figure 5: Workflow — ECG Lead Placement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["Step 1: Patient preparation - clean skin with alcohol"]
    nodeS2["Step 2: Apply gel to electrodes for low contact impedance"]
    nodeS3["Step 3: Place limb electrodes - RA, LA, RL, LL"]
    nodeS4["Step 4: Place precordial electrodes - V1 to V6"]
    nodeS5["Step 5: Connect patient cable to ECG machine"]
    nodeS6["Step 6: Calibrate 1 mV standard signal"]
    nodeS7["Step 7: Acquire 12 lead ECG over 10 seconds"]
    nodeS8["Step 8: Print, store, and interpret the tracing"]

    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with reference to ECG: (i) P wave, (ii) PR interval, (iii) QRS complex.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) P Wave:** It is a small, smooth, rounded, **upright** deflection produced by **atrial depolarization** — the electrical activation that triggers atrial contraction. Normal duration is **< 0.12 seconds** with amplitude **< 2.5 mm**.

**(ii) PR Interval:** Measured from the **beginning of the P wave to the beginning of the QRS complex**. It represents the time taken for the impulse to travel from the SA node, through the atria, and through the AV node (where the conduction delay occurs). Normal range is **0.12 to 0.20 seconds** (4 to 5 small boxes at 25 mm/s).

**(iii) QRS Complex:** A sharp, high-amplitude deflection representing **ventricular depolarization**. It consists of a Q wave (initial downward), R wave (tall upward), and S wave (downward). Normal duration is **0.06 to 0.10 seconds**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*State Einthoven's Law and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer:**

**Einthoven's Law** states that the algebraic sum of the voltages recorded by Leads I and III at any instant equals the voltage recorded by Lead II:

$$V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$$

**Significance:**
- It validates the **vectorial nature** of cardiac electrical activity.
- It confirms that the three bipolar limb leads form a **closed equilateral triangle** (Einthoven's triangle) with the heart at its center.
- It allows **redundancy checking** — if $V_I + V_{III} \neq V_{II}$, electrode placement is faulty or a lead is broken.
- It forms the mathematical basis for the **calculation of the mean electrical axis** of the heart.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse PR interval with PR segment.** PR interval includes the P wave; PR segment does not.
> - **Always state standard values with units** (e.g., "0.12 s" not "small").
> - **In numerical problems, do not skip the substitution step** — write the numbers *into* the formula.
> - **In lead configuration questions, label polarity (+/−) of both electrodes.**
> - **A common error is stating QRS duration as 0.04 s** — this is **wrong**. Correct value is 0.06–0.10 s.
> - **V1 and V2 are placed on the RIGHT and LEFT respectively** — students often swap them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a modern ECG machine. List the key specifications of an ECG amplifier.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* An ECG is recorded at a paper speed of 25 mm/s. The R–R interval measures 20 mm and the R wave amplitude is 15 mm. Calculate (i) the time interval between successive R waves, (ii) the heart rate, and (iii) the voltage of the R wave. Standard sensitivity is 10 mm/mV.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of ECG Machine:**

The ECG machine consists of the following functional blocks in series:

1. **Patient Cable and Electrodes:** 10 electrodes pick up bioelectric potentials from the limbs and chest. Skin-electrode contact impedance is reduced using conductive gel.
2. **Lead Selector Network:** A switching matrix that connects the appropriate electrode pairs to the amplifier for the chosen lead (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6).
3. **Calibration Circuit:** Provides a **1 mV reference pulse** that produces a 10 mm vertical deflection — used for standardization.
4. **Preamplifier:** High-gain, high-input-impedance (> 10 MΩ), low-noise differential amplifier. Typically uses an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620).
5. **Isolation Amplifier:** Provides galvanic isolation (> 5000 V) between patient and mains-powered circuits for safety.
6. **Main Amplifier:** Provides the bulk of the voltage gain (≈ 1000).
7. **Filter Bank:**
   - High-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 0.05 Hz) — removes baseline wander.
   - Low-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 150 Hz) — removes high-frequency noise.
   - Notch filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz) — eliminates power-line interference.
8. **Driver Amplifier and Display:** Drives the stylus of a thermal printer, the LCD, or the ADC for digital storage.
9. **Memory and Communication:** Stores recordings (typically 10 s × 12 leads) and transmits via USB/Ethernet/HL7.

**Key Specifications of ECG Amplifier:**
- Input impedance: **> 10 MΩ** (preferably 100 MΩ).
- CMRR: **> 100 dB** (often > 110 dB).
- Frequency response: **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (–3 dB).
- Gain: **1000** (variable).
- Sensitivity: **10 mm/mV** (standard).
- Noise: **< 10 μV (p-p)** referred to input.
- Defibrillator protection: Must withstand 5 kV, 360 J discharge.
- Patient leakage current: **< 10 μA (normal condition)**, **< 50 μA (single fault)**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Paper speed, $v = 25\ \text{mm/s}$
- R–R distance, $d = 20\ \text{mm}$
- R wave amplitude, $h = 15\ \text{mm}$
- Sensitivity, $S = 10\ \text{mm/mV}$

**(i) Time interval (T) between successive R waves:**

$$
T = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{20\ \text{mm}}{25\ \text{mm/s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 0.80\ \text{s}}
$$

**(ii) Heart Rate (HR):**

$$
HR = \frac{60}{T} = \frac{60}{0.80}
$$

$$
\boxed{HR = 75\ \text{ beats per minute}}
$$

**(iii) Voltage of the R wave:**

$$
V = \frac{h}{S} = \frac{15\ \text{mm}}{10\ \text{mm/mV}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 1.5\ \text{mV}}
$$

> The calculated heart rate of **75 BPM** lies within the normal resting range of 60–100 BPM.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the 12-lead ECG system. Differentiate between bipolar, augmented, and precordial leads with electrode placements.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Derive Einthoven's Law using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law. Given the following lead voltages, calculate the mean electrical axis of the heart: $V_I = +1.0\ \text{mV}$, $V_{III} = +0.5\ \text{mV}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 12-Lead ECG System:**

The 12-lead ECG is a standard non-invasive diagnostic recording that provides **12 different viewpoints** of the heart's electrical activity using **10 surface electrodes**. The 12 leads are categorized into 3 groups:

**1. Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Triangle) — 3 leads:**

| Lead | Positive Electrode | Negative Electrode | Records |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead I | Left Arm (LA) | Right Arm (RA) | Lateral wall |
| Lead II | Left Leg (LL) | Right Arm (RA) | Inferior wall |
| Lead III | Left Leg (LL) | Left Arm (LA) | Inferior wall |

**2. Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger) — 3 leads:**

Each lead is referenced to a **central terminal** formed by combining the other two limb electrodes through high-value resistors. The signal is then amplified (~1.4×) to compensate for the resistive attenuation.

| Lead | Active Electrode | Explores |
|---|---|---|
| aVR | Right Arm | Right-sided / Atrial activity |
| aVL | Left Arm | Lateral wall of LV |
| aVF | Left Leg | Inferior wall of LV |

**3. Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson) — 6 leads:**

The central terminal (combination of RA, LA, LL) is the negative pole. The chest electrode is the positive pole.

| Lead | Position |
|---|---|
| V1 | 4th ICS, right sternal border |
| V2 | 4th ICS, left sternal border |
| V3 | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| V4 | 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line |
| V5 | Anterior axillary line, level of V4 |
| V6 | Mid-axillary line, level of V4 |

**Clinical Importance:**
- **Inferior MI** is best seen in II, III, aVF.
- **Anterior MI** is best seen in V1–V4.
- **Lateral MI** is best seen in I, aVL, V5, V6.

**(b) Derivation and Calculation:**

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.1):**
By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL):

$$
V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}, \quad V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}, \quad V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}
$$

Adding $V_I$ and $V_{III}$:

$$
V_I + V_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}
$$

$$
\therefore \boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}}
$$

**Mean Electrical Axis Calculation:**

**Given:** $V_I = +1.0\ \text{mV}$, $V_{III} = +0.5\ \text{mV}$

**Step 1:** Use the axis formula:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{2 V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}\,(V_I + V_{III})}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{\sqrt{3}\,(1.0 + 0.5)} = \frac{1.0}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.5} = \frac{1.0}{2.598}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\tan\theta = 0.3849
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \arctan(0.3849) = 21.05°
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx +21°}
$$

**Step 5: Clinical Interpretation:**

| Axis Range | Category |
|---|---|
| $-30°$ to $+100°$ | **Normal** |
| $-30°$ to $-90°$ | Left axis deviation |
| $+100°$ to $+180°$ | Right axis deviation |
| $-90°$ to $-180°$ | Extreme / "Northwest" axis |

Since $\theta = +21°$ falls in the **normal range**, the heart's mean electrical axis is **normal**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for ECG — Module 2**

- ✅ **ECG** = recording of bioelectric potentials of the heart; **Einthoven** is the pioneer.
- ✅ **P wave** = atrial depolarization (< 0.12 s).
- ✅ **PR interval** = 0.12 to 0.20 s (P start to QRS start).
- ✅ **QRS complex** = ventricular depolarization (0.06 to 0.10 s).
- ✅ **T wave** = ventricular repolarization.
- ✅ **ST segment** = isoelectric; elevation/depression indicates ischemia/infarction.
- ✅ **QT interval** = 0.36 to 0.44 s (HR-dependent).
- ✅ **Standard paper speed:** **25 mm/s**; 1 small box = 0.04 s; 1 large box = 0.20 s.
- ✅ **Standard sensitivity:** **10 mm/mV**.
- ✅ **Einthoven's Law:** $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$.
- ✅ **12 leads** = 3 bipolar + 3 augmented + 6 precordial = 12 viewpoints.
- ✅ **Heart rate formula:** $\text{BPM} = \dfrac{60}{R\text{-}R \text{(s)}} = \dfrac{1500}{R\text{-}R \text{(small boxes)}}$.
- ✅ **Voltage formula:** $\text{mV} = \dfrac{\text{mm}}{10}$.
- ✅ **Mean axis formula:** $\theta = \arctan\!\left[\dfrac{2V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}(V_I+V_{III})}\right]$; normal range = $-30°$ to $+100°$.
- ✅ **ECG amplifier specs:** input Z > 10 MΩ, CMRR > 100 dB, freq 0.05–150 Hz, gain ≈ 1000.
- ✅ **Safety:** galvanic isolation, leakage current < 10 μA (normal), < 50 μA (single fault).
- ✅ **Filters used:** HPF (0.05 Hz) for baseline wander, LPF (150 Hz) for muscle noise, Notch (50/60 Hz) for power line.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for ECG Paper Speed:** *"At 25, every small box is 4 hundredths of a second."* (25 mm/s × 0.04 s = 1 mm).
> **Mnemonic for Mean Axis:** *"Lead I positive + aVF positive = Normal Axis."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ECG Machine - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ECG Machine - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electrocardiograph (ECG Machine)** is a bioelectrical signal acquisition and recording instrument that captures, conditions, processes, and displays the electrical activity of the human heart over time, typically presented as a voltage-versus-time waveform called the **electrocardiogram (ECG)**.

Per the KTU 2024 syllabus and IEC 60601-2-25 standards, an ECG machine is a multi-channel, isolated, Class I, Type CF (Cardiac Floating) medical device that senses differential biopotentials (typically **0.5 mV to 5 mV** peak-to-peak) from the body surface using surface electrodes, amplifies them by approximately **1000×**, filters out baseline wander and mains interference, digitizes them, and presents the result on a display/thermal recorder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — What Examiners Look For:**
> The examiner expects the student to draw a complete, *functionally annotated* block diagram and justify **why** each stage exists in terms of (a) signal quality, (b) patient safety, and (c) diagnostic accuracy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the heart as a small **3-D electrical battery** that fires ~60–100 times per minute. Each firing pumps a tiny pulse of current (only a few millivolts!) through the body tissues, which act like a giant bowl of salt-water "gel."

An ECG machine is essentially a **high-end stereo microphone placed on this bowl of salt water**:

| Real Microphone on a Drum | ECG Machine on the Body |
|---|---|
| Tiny drum vibrations | Tiny cardiac electrical signals (~1 mV) |
| Two microphones, one near, one far | Two electrodes, one near, one far from heart |
| Cables pick up electrical hum | Lead wires pick up 50 Hz mains hum |
| Mixer + amplifier + speaker | Instrumentation amp + filters + display |
| Volume knob | Gain control (1 mV → 10 mm deflection) |

The two-electrode *differential* sensing is the key trick — just like a noise-cancelling headphone uses two microphones to subtract background noise, the ECG uses two (or more) electrodes to cancel out ambient interference while preserving the heart's signal.

## 1.3 Standard ECG Metrics and Physical Constants

The KTU board expects students to memorize these **standard parameters** (these are NOT to be derived — they are specifications):

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ECG Recording Specifications (IEC/AHA Recommendations)**
>
> - **Paper Speed:** **25 mm/s** (sometimes 50 mm/s for high-resolution)
> - **Vertical Calibration:** **10 mm = 1 mV**
> - **Diagnostic Bandwidth:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (-3 dB)
> - **Monitoring Bandwidth:** **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz** (-3 dB)
> - **Sampling Rate:** **≥ 500 Hz** (per channel) per AHA recommendations
> - **ADC Resolution:** **≥ 12 bits** (ideally ≥ 16 bits)
> - **Input Impedance:** **> 10 MΩ** (typically 50 MΩ)
> - **CMRR:** **> 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz
> - **Patient Leakage Current:** **< 10 µA** (Type CF — cardiac applied part)
> - **Defibrillation Withstand Voltage:** **5 kV** across patient electrodes
> - **Galvanic Isolation:** **> 4000 V RMS** between patient and mains

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Signal Characteristics That Drive the Design

Before dissecting the block diagram, we must understand the **enemy** — what is the signal like, and what are the noise sources we must defeat?

| Parameter | Value | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| ECG Signal Amplitude | **0.5 mV – 5 mV** | Needs ~60 dB gain (1000×) |
| ECG Frequency Range | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | Needs band-pass filter |
| Electrode-Skin Contact | 10 kΩ – 1 MΩ | Input impedance must be **>10 MΩ** |
| Mains Interference (50/60 Hz) | ~1–10 mV | Needs notch + high CMRR + DRL |
| DC Electrode Offset | ±300 mV | Needs AC coupling (HPF) |
| Defibrillator Pulse | 5 kV, 5 ms | Needs surge protection |
| Patient Leakage Risk | Lethal at > 10 mA | Needs isolation + Type CF design |

## 2.2 Function of Each Block — The "Why"

The ECG machine is a **cascade of signal-conditioning and signal-processing stages**, each solving one specific problem:

### Stage 1: Electrodes & Patient Cable
- **Role:** Transduce ionic currents in the body into electronic currents at the cable.
- **Standard:** Ag/AgCl pre-gelled disposable surface electrodes (polarization voltage < 5 mV).
- **Why:** Without good electrodes, even the best amplifier fails. They form a half-cell potential that is suppressed by the high CMRR of the next stage.

### Stage 2: Defibrillator & Over-Voltage Protection
- **Role:** Survive a 5 kV defibrillator pulse without damaging the electronics or injuring the patient.
- **Circuit:** Neon lamps (or MOVs/TVS diodes) that clamp transients + series resistors + fast-blow fuses.
- **Why:** Hospital ECGs must remain *connected* to the patient even during defibrillation.

### Stage 3: Right Leg Drive Circuit (Driven Right Leg — DRL)
- **Role:** Actively cancel common-mode voltage by *driving* it back through a third electrode on the right leg.
- **Why:** Passive grounding (right leg to ground) only degrades CMRR; active DRL *improves* CMRR by 20–40 dB.

### Stage 4: Instrumentation Amplifier (Pre-amplifier)
- **Role:** Provide high differential gain, high CMRR, high input impedance.
- **Typical ICs:** AD620, INA128, INA333.
- **Why:** This is the *heart* of the analog front-end. It must reject 50/60 Hz while amplifying the millivolt ECG.

### Stage 5: Isolation Amplifier / Optocoupler Barrier
- **Role:** Provide galvanic isolation (> 4000 V) between patient side and the mains-powered digital/recording side.
- **Typical ICs:** ISO124, ADuM3150.
- **Why:** Patient safety — any mains fault on the recording side must not reach the patient.

### Stage 6: Filter Bank (HPF + LPF + Notch)
- **Role:** Remove baseline wander (HPF @ 0.05 Hz), high-frequency noise (LPF @ 150 Hz), and mains hum (Notch @ 50/60 Hz).
- **Why:** Clean signal = clean diagnosis. Filters also prevent aliasing in the ADC.

### Stage 7: Main Amplifier / Variable Gain
- **Role:** Provide adjustable gain (typically ×1 to ×10) and final voltage scaling for ADC input.
- **Why:** Different leads have different amplitudes; automatic or manual gain control normalizes them.

### Stage 8: Sample-and-Hold + ADC
- **Role:** Sample the analog signal at ≥ 500 Hz and digitize with ≥ 12-bit resolution.
- **Why:** All modern ECG processing is digital — arrhythmia detection, HR calculation, ST-segment analysis.

### Stage 9: Microcontroller / DSP
- **Role:** Run digital filters, detect QRS complexes, compute heart rate, drive display, store data.
- **Why:** Modern ECGs (post-2000) are essentially embedded computers.

### Stage 10: Display (LCD) + Thermal Recorder
- **Role:** Show real-time waveforms and print paper tracings.
- **Why:** The diagnostic output — what the doctor actually reads.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorize These for the 14-Mark Question**

| Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Differential gain of 3-op-amp IA | $A_d = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right)$ | 1000 (60 dB) |
| Common-mode gain (mismatch $\delta$ in $R_1$) | $A_{cm} \approx \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\cdot\dfrac{\delta}{2}\cdot\dfrac{R_g}{2R_1 + R_g}$ | 0.001 |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)$ dB | $\geq$ 100 dB |
| Total ECG Front-End Gain | $G_{total} = G_{IA} \cdot G_{main}$ | $\approx$ 1000 |
| Nyquist Sampling Frequency | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $\geq$ 300 Hz (use $\geq$ 500 Hz) |
| Defibrillation Pulse Energy (typical) | $E = \int_{0}^{t} V(t)\cdot I(t)\, dt$ | 360 J (adult) |
| Right-Leg Drive Feedback | $V_{RL} = -A_{DRL} \cdot V_{cm}$ | $A_{DRL}$ $\approx$ 100 |
| Effective Common-Mode Current | $i_{cm} = \dfrac{V_{cm} - V_{RL}}{Z_{body}}$ | < 10 µA |
| ADC Quantization Step (12-bit, 5 V FS) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{12}}$ | 1.22 mV |
| Time per mm on Paper (25 mm/s) | $T_{mm} = \dfrac{1}{25}$ s/mm | 40 ms/mm |

> **Never write absolute value bars `\|x\|` inside markdown tables.** Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Modern ECG machines are no longer just recorders — they are **edge-AI diagnostic platforms**. The same block diagram concept scales into:

- **Holter monitors** (24-hour ambulatory ECG)
- **Implantable loop recorders** (ILRs)
- **Smartwatch PPG/ECG** (Apple Watch, Fitbit Sense)
- **ICU multi-parameter monitors** (ECG + SpO₂ + EEG)

Every biomedical signal-acquisition instrument — EEG, EMG, EOG, ECoG — uses **the same block diagram**, just with different filter cutoffs and amplitudes. Mastering the ECG block diagram is mastering the universal biopotential front-end.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

The instrumentation amplifier (IA) is the centerpiece of any ECG front-end. We will derive its differential gain from first principles.

### Step 1 — Draw and label the standard topology

The IA consists of two input buffer stages (Op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$) followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$).

- Input voltages: $V_1$ (non-inverting), $V_2$ (inverting)
- Gain-setting resistor: $R_g$ (between the two op-amp inverting inputs)
- Feedback resistors: $R_1$ (each, equal)
- Difference-amp resistors: $R_2$ (input), $R_3$ (feedback) — both pairs equal

### Step 2 — Apply the virtual-short principle at the inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$

Because op-amp inputs draw no current and have equal voltages at their $\pm$ terminals:

$$
V_{A_1^-} = V_{A_1^+} = V_1
$$

$$
V_{A_2^-} = V_{A_2^+} = V_2
$$

### Step 3 — Find the current flowing through $R_g$

The voltage across $R_g$ is exactly $V_1 - V_2$ (because both terminals of $R_g$ sit at the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$, which are forced to $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively).

$$
I_{R_g} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

### Step 4 — This same current flows through both $R_1$ resistors

No current enters the op-amp inputs, so all the current through $R_g$ must flow through the two $R_1$ resistors (one above $R_g$, one below).

### Step 5 — Write the output voltages of $A_1$ and $A_2$

The output of $A_1$ is its inverting-input voltage ($V_1$) **plus** the IR-drop across the upper $R_1$ (current flows *down* through $R_1$ from $V_{o1}$ to $V_1$):

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + I_{R_g} \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

Similarly, the output of $A_2$ is its inverting-input voltage ($V_2$) **minus** the IR-drop across the lower $R_1$:

$$
V_{o2} = V_2 - I_{R_g} \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

### Step 6 — Take the difference $V_{o2} - V_{o1}$

$$
V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2) - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1) + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

$$
V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

### Step 7 — Apply the difference amplifier gain of $A_3$

For the standard difference amplifier with $R_2$ input resistors and $R_3$ feedback resistors:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1})
$$

### Step 8 — Combine Step 6 and Step 7 to obtain the differential gain

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_2 - V_1)}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)}
$$

### Numerical Sanity Check

For an ECG pre-amp, pick $R_1 = R_g = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 50\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
A_d = \frac{50\text{k}}{10\text{k}}\left(1 + \frac{2(10\text{k})}{10\text{k}}\right) = 5 \times 3 = 15
$$

For ECG, we need ~1000. Choose $R_g = 100\ \Omega$, $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
A_d = 5 \times \left(1 + \frac{20\text{k}}{100}\right) = 5 \times 201 = 1005
$$

✓ Matches the design target of ~1000.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR of the Instrumentation Amplifier

CMRR is the single most important specification for any ECG amplifier, because the **50/60 Hz mains interference is a common-mode signal**.

### Step 1 — Define the common-mode and differential-mode input voltages

$$
V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}, \qquad V_d = V_2 - V_1
$$

$$
V_1 = V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2}, \qquad V_2 = V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Introduce a resistor mismatch

Suppose $R_1$ on the upper side is $R_1$ but on the lower side is $R_1(1 + \delta)$, where $\delta \ll 1$ is the fractional mismatch.

### Step 3 — Recompute the buffer outputs with mismatch

The current through $R_g$ is unchanged (still $(V_1 - V_2)/R_g$), but the drops across the two $R_1$ resistors are now unequal:

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
V_{o2} = V_2 - \frac{R_1(1+\delta)}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

### Step 4 — Subtract and separate the common-mode and differential parts

After algebra (see KTU reference texts), the common-mode gain at the buffer stage is:

$$
A_{cm,\text{stage1}} = \frac{V_{o2} - V_{o1}}{2 V_{cm}} \approx \frac{\delta}{2}\cdot\frac{R_g}{2R_1 + R_g}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine with the difference-amplifier common-mode gain

The second stage has its own CMRR limited by resistor matching; assuming it is trimmed perfectly, the **total** common-mode gain is just the first stage:

$$
A_{cm,\text{total}} \approx \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot \frac{\delta}{2}\cdot\frac{R_g}{2R_1 + R_g}
$$

### Step 6 — Form the CMRR ratio

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert} = \frac{1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}}{\dfrac{\delta}{2}\cdot\dfrac{R_g}{2R_1 + R_g}}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to decibels

$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}}{\dfrac{\delta}{2}\cdot\dfrac{R_g}{2R_1 + R_g}}\right)}
$$

### Numerical Check

Take $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_g = 100\ \Omega$, $\delta = 0.001$ (0.1 % mismatch, achievable with modern thin-film resistors):

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{1 + 200}{0.0005 \times \dfrac{100}{20100}} = \frac{201}{0.0005 \times 0.004975} = \frac{201}{2.49 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8.08 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(8.08 \times 10^{7}) \approx 158\ \text{dB}
$$

This is the *theoretical* ceiling. Real-world IA ICs like AD620 achieve ~130 dB at DC.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Right Leg Drive (DRL) Feedback Equation

The DRL circuit **actively drives** the common-mode voltage back into the patient's right leg through an inverting amplifier, reducing common-mode interference by a feedback factor.

### Step 1 — Define the common-mode voltage as sensed by the IA

$$
V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the DRL amplifier gain $A_{DRL}$

The inverting amplifier with input resistor $R_a$ and feedback resistor $R_b$:

$$
A_{DRL} = -\frac{R_b}{R_a}
$$

### Step 3 — Write the driven right-leg voltage

$$
V_{RL} = A_{DRL} \cdot V_{cm} = -\frac{R_b}{R_a} \cdot V_{cm}
$$

### Step 4 — Write the common-mode current flowing through the patient body

The patient's body is modeled as impedance $Z_{body}$ (typically a few hundred ohms):

$$
i_{cm} = \frac{V_{cm} - V_{RL}}{Z_{body}} = \frac{V_{cm}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_b}{R_a}\right)}{Z_{body}}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $V_{cm}$ in terms of the source interference $V_{noise}$

Without DRL: $V_{cm} = V_{noise}$. With DRL applied through impedance $Z_{body}$ to a noise source of impedance $Z_s$:

$$
V_{cm} = \frac{V_{noise}}{1 + A_{DRL} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{body}}{Z_s + Z_{body}}}
$$

For $A_{DRL} \gg 1$, the denominator is large, so $V_{cm} \to 0$. **This is the magic of DRL** — it suppresses 50/60 Hz by 20–40 dB beyond what the IA's CMRR alone provides.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Filter Cutoffs

| Signal / Noise | Frequency Range | Filter Block | Cutoff Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC electrode offset | 0 Hz | 1st-order HPF | $f_c = 0.05$ Hz |
| Baseline wander (breathing) | 0.1 – 0.5 Hz | HPF (with DRL feedback) | $f_c = 0.05$ Hz |
| P wave | 0.5 – 5 Hz | Pass-band | — |
| QRS complex | 5 – 40 Hz | Pass-band | — |
| T wave | 0.5 – 8 Hz | Pass-band | — |
| Muscle artifact (EMG) | 20 – 500 Hz | 2nd-order Butterworth LPF | $f_c = 150$ Hz |
| Mains interference | 50 Hz (or 60 Hz) | Twin-T notch | $f_n = 50$ Hz, depth > 40 dB |
| Aliasing | $> f_s/2$ | Anti-alias LPF (before ADC) | $f_c \approx 0.4 \cdot f_s$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an ECG Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the signal path from the patient's body through every analog stage, the isolation barrier, and into the digital back-end. The Driven Right Leg (DRL) feedback path is shown as a separate control loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Body\nHeart Signal] --> A2[Surface Electrodes\nAgAgCl]
    A2 --> A3[Lead Wires\nPatient Cable]
    A3 --> A4[Defib Protection\nNeon Lamps and TVS]
    A4 --> A5[Instrumentation\nAmplifier]
    A5 -- CM Voltage --> A6[Driven Right Leg\nInverting Amp]
    A6 --> A7[Right Leg\nElectrode]
    A7 -.feedback.-> A1
    A5 --> A8[Isolation\nAmplifier]
    A8 --> A9[High Pass Filter\n0.05 Hz]
    A9 --> A10[Low Pass Filter\n150 Hz]
    A10 --> A11[Notch Filter\n50 Hz]
    A11 --> A12[Main Amplifier\nVariable Gain]
    A12 --> A13[Sample and Hold]
    A13 --> A14[ADC\n12 bit 500 Hz]
    A14 --> A15[Microcontroller\nDSP]
    A15 --> A16[Display\nLCD]
    A15 --> A17[Thermal\nRecorder]
    A15 --> A18[Memory\nStorage]

    classDef safety fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000;
    classDef analog fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000;
    classDef digital fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,color:#000;
    classDef output fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000;
    classDef source fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000;

    class A2,A3,A4,A7 safety;
    class A5,A6,A9,A10,A11,A12,A13 analog;
    class A14,A15 digital;
    class A16,A17,A18 output;
    class A1 source;
```

## 4.2 Signal-Level Block Diagram with Gain Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Signal Source\n0.5 to 5 mV\n0.05 to 150 Hz] --> G1
    G1[Stage 1 IA Gain\nx100 approx 40 dB] --> S2[0.05 to 0.5 V]
    S2 --> G2[Stage 2 Main Amp\nx10 approx 20 dB]
    G2 --> S3[0.5 to 5 V]
    S3 --> G3[Stage 3 ADC\n12 bit]
    G3 --> S4[Digital Domain\nQRS Detection HR Display]
    N1[Noise Sources\nDC offset 300 mV\nMains 1 to 10 mV\nEMG noise] -->|Suppressed by| G1
    N1 -->|Further Reduced by| G2

    classDef sig fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366;
    classDef gain fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600;
    classDef noise fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000;
    classDef out fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600;
    class S1 sig
    class G1,G2,G3 gain
    class N1 noise
    class S2,S3,S4 out
```

## 4.3 Power-Supply Isolation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[Mains 230V AC] --> XFMR[Medical Grade\nIsolation Transformer]
    XFMR --> DC1[DC Supply\nPatient Side]
    XFMR --> ISO[Galvanic Isolation\nBarrier 4 kV]
    ISO --> DC2[DC Supply\nDigital Side]
    DC1 --> IA[IA and Analog Front End]
    DC2 --> MCU[Microcontroller and Recorder]
    IA -->|Analog Signal\nAcross Barrier| MCU

    classDef mains fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000;
    classDef iso fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600;
    classDef side fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366;
    class AC,XFMR mains
    class ISO iso
    class DC1,DC2,IA,MCU side
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Tip for the Board Exam:**
> In the 14-mark question, the examiner will award 2 marks *just* for correctly drawing the **isolation barrier** as a separate boundary between "patient side" and "digital/recording side." Many students omit this — losing easy marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define CMRR. Why is high CMRR critical for ECG amplifiers?**

**Model Answer:**

CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) is the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_{cm}$ of an amplifier, expressed in decibels:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)
$$

It is critical for ECG amplifiers because **50/60 Hz mains interference appears as a common-mode signal** at both electrode terminals (it is approximately equal in amplitude and phase at both leads). A high CMRR (≥ 100 dB) ensures this interference is rejected while the millivolt-level differential ECG signal is amplified. Without high CMRR, the mains hum would completely swamp the ECG waveform, making diagnosis impossible.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the standard values of ECG paper speed and vertical calibration. What clinical measurements can be obtained from these?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Paper speed | **25 mm/s** |
| Vertical calibration | **10 mm = 1 mV** |

**Clinical Measurements Derived:**
- **Time intervals** (PR, QRS, QT): read directly off the horizontal axis. At 25 mm/s, each small box (1 mm) = **0.04 s**, each large box (5 mm) = **0.20 s**.
- **Amplitude**: each mm vertically = **0.1 mV**, so wave heights can be measured directly.
- **Heart Rate**: 1500 ÷ (number of small boxes between two consecutive R-waves) — this is the standard clinical formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the complete functional block diagram of a modern ECG machine and explain the function of **each block**. Clearly indicate the **galvanic isolation barrier**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the differential gain of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Why is this topology preferred over a simple difference amplifier for ECG signals?

#### Model Solution to Part A(a) — Block Diagram with Functions

**The diagram should contain the following 11 blocks, in order:**

1. **Surface Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** — Convert ionic currents in the body to electronic currents. Provide stable, low-polarization contact. Pairs are placed on the limbs and chest (12-lead configuration uses 10 electrodes).

2. **Patient Cable / Lead Wires** — Shielded twisted-pair cables that carry the weak signals to the amplifier while rejecting capacitive pickup.

3. **Defibrillator Protection Circuit** — Neon lamps (or MOVs/TVS diodes) plus current-limiting resistors that clamp the **5 kV defibrillator pulse** to a safe level, protecting downstream electronics.

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (Pre-amp)** — Provides high differential gain (~100), ultra-high CMRR (>100 dB), and ultra-high input impedance (>10 MΩ). Uses three op-amps (or a single IC like AD620).

5. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit** — Senses the common-mode voltage from the IA, inverts it, and feeds it back into the right-leg electrode. Actively reduces 50/60 Hz interference by an additional 20–40 dB.

6. **Isolation Amplifier / Optocoupler** — Provides **galvanic isolation > 4000 V RMS** between the patient-connected analog front-end and the mains-powered digital/recording circuitry. Critical for patient safety (Type CF rating).

7. **High-Pass Filter (HPF) at 0.05 Hz** — Removes DC electrode offset (±300 mV) and slow baseline wander caused by respiration.

8. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF) at 150 Hz** — Removes high-frequency muscle artifact (EMG) and limits bandwidth to prevent aliasing.

9. **Notch Filter at 50 Hz** — Twin-T or active notch filter that attenuates mains interference by > 40 dB. Some designs use adaptive notch filters that track the mains frequency.

10. **Main Amplifier (Variable Gain)** — Provides additional gain (~×10) and automatic or manual gain control. Also implements lead-fail detection and pacer-pulse detection.

11. **Sample-and-Hold + ADC + Microcontroller + Display/Recorder** — Digital back-end that samples at ≥ 500 Hz with ≥ 12-bit resolution, runs QRS detection (e.g., Pan-Tompkins algorithm), computes heart rate, and drives the LCD and thermal printer.

**Isolation Barrier Location:** Between the Instrumentation Amplifier (patient side) and the Main Amplifier + ADC + Digital section (recording side). This is shown as a dashed line in the answer diagram.

#### Model Solution to Part A(b) — Instrumentation Amplifier Derivation

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete 8-step derivation. The student should:

1. Draw the IA topology with two input buffers and one difference amplifier.
2. Apply virtual short at op-amp inputs.
3. Compute current through $R_g$.
4. Compute $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$.
5. Take the difference $V_{o2} - V_{o1}$.
6. Apply the difference-amp gain.
7. Combine to get:

$$
\boxed{A_d = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)}
$$

8. **Justify the choice** over a simple difference amplifier:

   - **Input Impedance:** Simple difference amp has $R_1$ directly at the source — loading the high-impedance electrode. IA has unity-gain buffers at the input, giving input impedance **> 1 GΩ**.
   - **Gain Adjustment:** In a simple diff amp, changing gain requires changing *two* matched resistors. In an IA, gain is set by **one** resistor $R_g$ — much easier to make variable.
   - **CMRR Sensitivity:** A simple diff amp's CMRR depends on **four** matched resistors ($R_1, R_1, R_2, R_3$). An IA's CMRR depends mainly on **two** matched $R_1$ resistors. Easier to trim and maintain.
   - **Noise:** The IA buffers provide current gain before the noise-sensitive difference stage, yielding lower overall input-referred noise.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the function of the **Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** in an ECG machine. Derive the expression for the suppressed common-mode voltage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-bit ADC with a full-scale range of **±5 V** samples an ECG signal amplified to a peak of **4.8 V**. The ECG signal has a maximum frequency component of **150 Hz**. Determine: (i) the minimum sampling frequency per the Nyquist criterion, (ii) the actual recommended sampling frequency per AHA guidelines, and (iii) the ADC's quantization step size in µV.

#### Model Solution to Part B(a) — DRL Circuit

Refer to **Section 3.3** for the full derivation. The student should:

1. Define the common-mode voltage $V_{cm} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$.
2. Draw the DRL circuit: a summing amplifier that senses $V_{cm}$ and inverts it with gain $A_{DRL} = -R_b/R_a$.
3. Show that $V_{RL} = -A_{DRL} \cdot V_{cm}$ is fed to the right-leg electrode.
4. Show that the effective common-mode voltage with DRL applied is:

$$
V_{cm,\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{noise}}{1 + A_{DRL}\cdot\dfrac{Z_{body}}{Z_s + Z_{body}}}
$$

5. Conclude that for $A_{DRL} \gg 1$, $V_{cm,\text{eff}} \to 0$, giving 20–40 dB extra rejection.

**Functional Block Diagram (text version, since students usually draw it on paper):**

[Electrode 1, Electrode 2] → Summing Junction → Common-Mode Voltage $V_{cm}$ → Inverting Amplifier (gain $A_{DRL}$) → Right-Leg Electrode → Patient Body (closes the loop).

#### Model Solution to Part B(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- ADC resolution: $N = 12$ bits
- Full-scale range: $V_{FS} = \pm 5\text{ V}$, so total span = 10 V
- ECG peak amplitude: $V_{peak} = 4.8\text{ V}$
- Maximum ECG frequency: $f_{max} = 150\text{ Hz}$

**(i) Minimum sampling frequency per Nyquist:**

$$
f_{s,\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300\ \text{Hz}
$$

**(ii) Recommended sampling frequency per AHA:**

The American Heart Association recommends sampling at **≥ 500 Hz** (some sources state 1000–2000 Hz for pediatric ECGs). For an adult diagnostic ECG, the standard is:

$$
\boxed{f_{s,\text{recommended}} = 500\ \text{Hz}}
$$

**(iii) Quantization step size:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10\text{ V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096}\text{ V} = 2.441 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V}
$$

Converting to microvolts:

$$
\boxed{\Delta V = 2441\ \mu\text{V} \approx 2.44\ \text{mV}}
$$

**Bonus Discussion (for full marks):**
The quantization noise is $\Delta V / \sqrt{12} \approx 705\ \mu\text{V}_{\text{RMS}}$. Compare this to the original ECG amplitude of 1 mV — the quantization noise is comparable to the signal! This is why modern ECGs use **16-bit or 24-bit sigma-delta ADCs** (effective resolution $\geq 0.3\ \mu\text{V}$) rather than 12-bit successive-approximation ADCs. A 12-bit ADC is acceptable only **after** the 1000× analog pre-amplification, where the signal at the ADC input is ~1 V — making quantization noise (2.44 mV) a much smaller relative error.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Read Carefully**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the isolation barrier.** Many students draw the IA, filters, ADC, etc., in a single line *without* showing the galvanic isolation barrier between patient side and mains side. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**
>
> 2. **Wrong filter cutoffs.** Writing "low-pass at 40 Hz" is for *monitoring* grade only; diagnostic ECG requires **150 Hz**. Examiners distinguish. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
>
> 3. **No mention of CMRR.** Whenever you write "Instrumentation Amplifier" in an answer, immediately follow with "high CMRR (>100 dB)" or "rejects mains interference." Skipping this loses you a "complete" answer mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing $R_g$ location.** In the IA derivation, $R_g$ is the resistor *between the two inverting inputs* of the input buffers — NOT a feedback resistor. Drawing it wrong loses the entire 7-mark derivation.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Driven Right Leg.** Many textbook block diagrams omit the DRL block. Real clinical ECGs *always* have it. Including it earns a "beyond-textbook" bonus mark.
>
> 6. **Wrong paper speed.** ECG paper speed is **25 mm/s**, not 10 mm/s and not 50 mm/s. Confusing this with EEG paper speed (30 mm/s) is a common slip.
>
> 7. **Unit confusion in numericals.** ADC quantization step: convert to µV at the end. Many students leave the answer in volts or in "mV" when the question asks for "µV."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — ECG Machine Block Diagram**
>
> - ☐ ECG signal: **0.5–5 mV**, **0.05–150 Hz**, taken from body surface via Ag/AgCl electrodes.
> - ☐ **11 essential blocks**: Electrodes → Cable → Defib Protection → IA → DRL → Isolation → HPF (0.05 Hz) → LPF (150 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → Main Amp → ADC → MCU → Display/Recorder.
> - ☐ **Standard recording parameters**: 25 mm/s paper speed, 10 mm/mV vertical calibration.
> - ☐ **Front-end gain** ≈ **1000×** (IA provides ~100, main amp provides ~10).
> - ☐ **IA differential gain** formula: $A_d = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$.
> - ☐ **CMRR formula**: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\vert A_d/A_{cm}\vert$, target **> 100 dB**.
> - ☐ **Input impedance**: > 10 MΩ (achieved by IA's input buffers).
> - ☐ **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** provides an *active* common-mode feedback that reduces 50/60 Hz by 20–40 dB.
> - ☐ **Galvanic isolation** (> 4000 V) between patient and mains is mandatory for Type CF (cardiac) safety.
> - ☐ **Patient leakage current** must be < 10 µA (Type CF).
> - ☐ **Sampling rate** ≥ 500 Hz (AHA), ≥ 300 Hz (Nyquist minimum).
> - ☐ **ADC resolution** ≥ 12 bits; modern designs use 16–24 bit sigma-delta.
