> **Clinical Pitfall:** The huge impedance mismatch at air–skin and bone–soft tissue is why **ultrasound gel** is mandatory — without it, 99.9% of the beam reflects back and no image forms.

## 2.4 MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) System

### 2.4.1 Larmor Equation — The Heart of MRI

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

where:
- $\omega_0$ = Larmor (precession) frequency in **rad/s**, or $f_0$ in **Hz**
- $\gamma$ = gyromagnetic ratio (**42.58 MHz/T** for ¹H)
- $B_0$ = static magnetic field strength (**Tesla**)

For a **1.5 T** scanner:

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

For a **3.0 T** scanner:

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 3.0 = 127.74 \text{ MHz}$$

### 2.4.2 The Two Relaxation Times

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Range (ms) |
|-----------|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Longitudinal (T1) | $T_1$ | Recovery of $M_z$ along $B_0$ | 250–2500 |
| Transverse (T2) | $T_2$ | Decay of $M_{xy}$ in the xy-plane | 50–1000 |

### 2.4.3 Free Induction Decay (FID) Signal Equation

$$S(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right) e^{-t/T_2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** By varying repetition time (TR) and echo time (TE), MRI scanners can be "tuned" to be **T1-weighted** (good for anatomy) or **T2-weighted** (good for pathology/edema).

## 2.5 PET Scanning System

### 2.5.1 Positron Annihilation Detection

A positron emitted by a radiotracer (e.g., ¹⁸F-FDG) travels a few millimetres and annihilates with an electron, producing **two 511 keV gamma photons emitted at 180° (coincident pair)**.

The line of response (LOR) is determined by simultaneous detection in opposing detectors.

### 2.5.2 PET Reconstruction

The line of response satisfies:

$$t_1 = t_2 \quad \text{(coincidence timing window, typically } \pm 10 \text{ ns)}$$

The 3-D distribution of tracer concentration is reconstructed iteratively (e.g., MLEM or OSEM algorithm).

## 2.6 SPECT (Single Photon Emission CT)

Uses gamma-emitting radiotracers (⁹⁹ᵐTc, ¹²³I). A **gamma camera** rotates around the patient. Single photons (140 keV for ⁹⁹ᵐTc) are detected through a lead collimator that defines the projection geometry.

## 2.7 Comparison Table — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | CT | MRI | Ultrasound | PET |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----------|-----|
| Energy | X-ray (60–120 keV) | RF (MHz) | Sound (2–15 MHz) | Gamma (511 keV) |
| Resolution | 0.5 mm | 1 mm | 0.5–2 mm | 4–6 mm |
| Scan Time | 5–30 s | 20–60 min | Real-time | 20–30 min |
| Radiation | Yes (ionizing) | None | None | Yes (ionizing) |
| Best For | Bone, lung, trauma | Soft tissue, brain | Foetus, vascular, cardiac | Cancer, metabolism |
| Cost (approx.) | Medium | High | Low | Very High |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility of These Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **Radiotherapy planning:** CT-derived electron density maps calibrate linear accelerators.
> 2. **Image-guided surgery:** Intra-operative MRI enables brain tumour resection with millimetre accuracy.
> 3. **Telemedicine:** DICOM-standard scans transmitted across continents for expert opinion.
> 4. **AI diagnosis:** Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on CT/MRI detect lung cancer, diabetic retinopathy, etc.
> 5. **Cardiology:** Echocardiography quantifies ejection fraction in real time.
> 6. **Musculoskeletal:** DEXA scan measures bone mineral density for osteoporosis screening.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hounsfield Unit Conversion for a Tissue

**Problem:** A CT scan measures the linear attenuation coefficient of liver tissue as $\mu_{\text{liver}} = 0.207 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. The linear attenuation coefficient of water at the same effective energy is $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.193 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Calculate the Hounsfield Unit value for liver tissue.

### Step 1 — Recall the Hounsfield Unit formula

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values into the equation

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{0.207 - 0.193}{0.193}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the numerator (the difference of attenuation coefficients)

$$0.207 - 0.193 = 0.014 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the ratio

$$\frac{0.014}{0.193} = 0.07254$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by 1000 to convert to HU units

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times 0.07254 = 72.54$$

### Step 6 — Clinical interpretation

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Interpretation:** Liver tissue typically measures between **+40 and +60 HU** in healthy patients. A value of **+72 HU** is *higher than normal* and may suggest hepatic **steatosis (fatty liver)** if the patient is on a non-contrast scan, or it could indicate elevated iron/copper deposition. This demonstrates the diagnostic power of the HU scale.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Depth Calculation in Ultrasound

**Problem:** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into soft tissue and the echo from a tumour is received after a round-trip time of $130 \,\mu s$. Using $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$ (speed of sound in soft tissue), calculate the depth of the tumour.

### Step 1 — Recall the basic depth formula

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

The factor of 2 appears because the pulse travels *to* the target and *back* — total path = 2d.

### Step 2 — Convert the time to SI units (seconds)

$$t = 130 \,\mu s = 130 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the values into the formula

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the numerator

$$1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2002 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Divide by 2 (because of round-trip)

$$d = \frac{0.2002}{2} = 0.1001 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to clinically meaningful units (cm)

$$d = 0.1001 \text{ m} = 10.01 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Takeaway:** A round-trip time of 130 µs corresponds to a depth of approximately **10 cm**, which is at the upper limit of abdominal ultrasound imaging. Beyond this depth, attenuation and scattering typically degrade the image.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Larmor Frequency for a 3 T MRI Scanner

**Problem:** Calculate the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons (¹H) in a 3.0 T MRI scanner.

### Step 1 — Recall the Larmor equation

$$f_0 = \gamma \cdot B_0$$

### Step 2 — Identify the gyromagnetic ratio for hydrogen

$$\gamma_{H} = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the field strength

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 3.0 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the final value

$$f_0 = 127.74 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The RF transmit coil of a 3 T MRI scanner must be tuned to **exactly 127.74 MHz** (with bandwidth ± a few kHz for slice selection). Any mismatch causes signal loss, image artefacts, and patient safety concerns (RF burns if power is reflected back).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient at an Interface

**Problem:** An ultrasound beam strikes the boundary between muscle ($Z_1 = 1.65 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$) and bone ($Z_2 = 7.8 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$). Calculate the intensity reflection coefficient.

### Step 1 — Recall the reflection coefficient formula

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the impedance values

$$R = \left(\frac{7.8 \times 10^{6} - 1.65 \times 10^{6}}{7.8 \times 10^{6} + 1.65 \times 10^{6}}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the numerator (difference of impedances)

$$7.8 \times 10^{6} - 1.65 \times 10^{6} = 6.15 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the denominator (sum of impedances)

$$7.8 \times 10^{6} + 1.65 \times 10^{6} = 9.45 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$$

### Step 5 — Form the ratio and square it

$$R = \left(\frac{6.15}{9.45}\right)^{2} = (0.6508)^{2} = 0.4235$$

### Step 6 — Express as a percentage

$$R = 42.35\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Clinical Implication:** About **42% of the ultrasound intensity** is reflected at the muscle–bone interface. This is why ultrasound *cannot* image through bone (e.g., adult brain) — most of the beam bounces back at the skull.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Typical Numerical Value | Clinical / Engineering Use |
|---|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | X-ray attenuation | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $\mu_{\text{bone}} \approx 0.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ @ 70 keV | CT imaging |
| 2 | Hounsfield Unit | $\text{HU} = 1000(\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | Bone: +700 to +3000 | Tissue classification |
| 3 | Ultrasound depth | $d = ct/2$ | $c = 1540$ m/s in tissue | Real-time imaging |
| 4 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | Fat: $1.38 \times 10^6$ | Echo generation |
| 5 | Reflection coefficient | $R = [(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)]^2$ | Air–skin: ~99.9% | Gel coupling essential |
| 6 | Larmor frequency | $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ | 63.87 MHz @ 1.5 T | MRI RF tuning |
| 7 | MRI signal | $S(t) = M_0(1-e^{-t/T_1})e^{-t/T_2}$ | T1 fat ≈ 250 ms | Contrast weighting |
| 8 | PET annihilation | $E_{\gamma} = 511$ keV | 2 photons at 180° | Functional imaging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Biomedical CT Scanning System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Voltage Generator 80 to 140 kVp] --> B[X ray Tube Rotating Anode]
    B --> C[Patient on Motorized Couch]
    C --> D[Attenuated X ray Fan Beam]
    D --> E[Curved Detector Array 700 to 1000 elements]
    E --> F[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter]
    G --> H[Computer with Reconstruction Algorithm]
    H --> I[Filtered Back Projection FBP]
    I --> J[Image Matrix 512 x 512 HU Map]
    J --> K[Display Monitor DICOM Viewer]
    K --> L[Radiologist Interpretation]
    H --> M[Image Archive PACS Server]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MRI Scanning System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Main Magnet Superconducting 1.5T or 3T] --> B[Shim Coists B0 Homogenizer]
    B --> C[Gradient Coils X Y Z Direction]
    C --> D[RF Transmit Coil]
    D --> E[Patient Bore]
    E --> F[RF Receive Coil Phased Array]
    F --> G[Preamplifier and Filter Bank]
    G --> H[Analog to Digital Converter]
    H --> I[Fourier Transform K space to Image]
    I --> J[Image Reconstruction Console]
    J --> K[Display and DICOM Storage]
    C --> L[Pulse Sequence Generator TR TE TI Flip Angle]
    L --> D
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Ultrasound B-mode Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser 200V ns] --> B[Piezoelectric Transducer 2 to 15 MHz]
    B --> C[Patient via Coupling Gel]
    C --> D[Returning Echoes]
    D --> E[Transmit Receive Switch TGC]
    E --> F[Time Gain Compensation Amplifier]
    F --> G[Bandpass Filter and Log Compression]
    G --> H[Analog to Digital Converter]
    H --> I[Scan Converter Polar to Cartesian]
    I --> J[Display Monitor B mode Image]
    J --> K[Operator Adjustment Depth Gain Focus]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — PET Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Injected 18F FDG] --> B[Tracer Uptake in Tissue]
    B --> C[Positron Emission and Annihilation]
    C --> D[Two 511 keV Photons at 180 degrees]
    D --> E[Ring of Scintillation Detectors BGO or LYSO]
    E --> F[Coincidence Timing Circuit 10 ns Window]
    F --> G[Line of Response LOR]
    G --> H[Reconstruction Engine OSEM MLEM]
    H --> I[3D Tracer Distribution]
    I --> J[Fused with CT or MRI]
    J --> K[Display for Oncology Diagnosis]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Flow — Application-Based Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Question] --> B{Anatomy or Function}
    B -- Anatomy --> C{Soft Tissue or Bone}
    C -- Bone and Lung --> D[Choose CT Scan]
    C -- Soft Tissue and Brain --> E[Choose MRI Scan]
    B -- Function and Metabolism --> F{Acute or Chronic}
    F -- Acute Perfusion --> G[Choose SPECT Scan]
    F -- Chronic Oncology --> H[Choose PET CT Scan]
    A --> I{Real Time Bedside Needed}
    I -- Yes --> J[Choose Ultrasound Scan]
    I -- No --> K[Continue with CT MRI or PET]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this Block-Level Architecture:** These diagrams follow the KTU 2024 prescribed block-diagram style for medical imaging systems — emphasizing signal flow from **energy source** → **patient interaction** → **detection** → **signal processing** → **image reconstruction** → **display**. Students must learn to draw these blocks in the examination.

## 4.6 Cross-Sectional Anatomy Mapping (CT Slice Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRANSVERSE_SLICE_OF_THORAX
    A1[Skin and Subcutaneous Fat HU -100] --> A2[Muscle HU +50]
    A2 --> A3[Rib Bone HU +800]
    A2 --> A4[Lung Aerated HU -800]
    A2 --> A5[Heart Blood HU +35 to +45]
    A5 --> A6[Coronary Artery HU +35]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
Define **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**. Why is water assigned a value of 0 HU?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** is a quantitative scale used in CT imaging to express the linear attenuation coefficient of a tissue relative to that of water.
>
> $$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
>
> **Why water = 0 HU:** Water is the most abundant and biologically neutral substance in the human body (~60% of body mass). Assigning it 0 HU provides a **stable, universal reference** so that HU values for other tissues (e.g., air = −1000, fat = −100, muscle = +50) are reproducible across different scanners and energies.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List **three** differences between **CT** and **MRI** scanning systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | CT | MRI |
|---|-----|-----|
| 1 | Uses **ionizing X-ray radiation** | Uses **non-ionizing RF + magnetic field** |
| 2 | Excellent for **bone, lung, acute trauma** | Excellent for **soft tissue, brain, spinal cord** |
| 3 | Acquisition time is **short (seconds)** | Acquisition time is **long (20–60 min)** |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the working principle of a **CT scanning system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss image reconstruction using **filtered back-projection (FBP)**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and principle (2 marks):**
CT (Computed Tomography) is an X-ray imaging modality that produces cross-sectional (transverse) images of the body. It is based on the principle that different tissues attenuate X-rays by different amounts, and by measuring this attenuation from many angles, a 2-D map of the **linear attenuation coefficient μ(x,y)** can be reconstructed.

**2. Block-diagram working (3 marks):**

- A high-voltage generator feeds an **X-ray tube** producing a fan-beam.
- The tube and detector array rotate synchronously around the patient on a **gantry**.
- The detector array (700–1000 elements) records residual intensities.
- The **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** digitises these signals.
- The computer stores them as a **sinogram** (each row = one projection).
- **Filtered back-projection (FBP)** converts the sinogram into a 512 × 512 image matrix.
- The image is displayed on a monitor and archived to **PACS**.

**3. Filtered Back-Projection — Mathematical detail (2 marks):**

Each projection is first **convolved** with a high-pass filter (e.g., Shepp-Logan or Ram-Lak filter) to remove the blurring inherent in simple back-projection. The filtered projections are then "smeared back" across the image plane at their original angle. The sum of all such back-projected, filtered projections yields the final image. Mathematically:

$$f(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} Q_{\theta}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)\, d\theta$$

where $Q_\theta$ is the **filtered** projection at angle $\theta$.

---

#### (b) A CT scanner measures the linear attenuation coefficient of a kidney stone as $\mu = 1.74 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ and the surrounding renal tissue as $\mu = 0.200 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. If $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.193 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, calculate the HU values and identify the stone type. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — HU for kidney stone:**

$$\text{HU}_{\text{stone}} = 1000 \times \frac{1.74 - 0.193}{0.193} = 1000 \times \frac{1.547}{0.193}$$

$$\text{HU}_{\text{stone}} = 1000 \times 8.0155 = 8015.5 \text{ HU}$$

*(Note: HU is normally clipped to ~+3000 in clinical displays; the actual value is +8015.)*

**Step 2 — HU for renal tissue:**

$$\text{HU}_{\text{renal}} = 1000 \times \frac{0.200 - 0.193}{0.193} = 1000 \times 0.0363 = 36.3 \text{ HU}$$

**Step 3 — Clinical identification:**

A kidney stone with HU > 800 is most likely a **calcium oxalate stone** (the most common type, ~75% of all kidney stones). 
- Calcium oxalate: **600–1200 HU**
- Uric acid: **200–450 HU**
- Struvite: **600–900 HU**
- Cystine: **600–1100 HU**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this calculation matters:** Pre-operative CT HU measurement helps urologists decide whether a stone is amenable to **Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy (ESWL)** — stones with HU > 1000 are often resistant to ESWL and may require surgical removal.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the **working principle of an MRI scanning system** with a block diagram. State the **Larmor equation** and compute the Larmor frequency for a **1.5 T scanner**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working principle (3 marks):**
MRI uses the magnetic property of hydrogen nuclei (protons) in the body. When placed in a strong static magnetic field $B_0$, protons align either parallel or anti-parallel to the field, and precess around it at the **Larmor frequency**. A radiofrequency (RF) pulse at the Larmor frequency tips the net magnetisation into the transverse plane. When the pulse is switched off, the magnetisation relaxes back, emitting an **FID signal** that is detected by receiver coils. By applying magnetic-field gradients, the Larmor frequency becomes position-dependent, allowing spatial encoding.

**2. Block diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram)*

Main magnet → Shim coils → Gradient coils → RF transmit coil → Patient → RF receive coil → Pre-amplifier → ADC → Fourier transform (k-space → image) → Display.

**3. Larmor equation and calculation (2 marks):**

$$f_0 = \gamma B_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

---

#### (b) An ultrasound scan shows a returning echo **78 µs** after transmission. The acoustic impedances of two tissues are $Z_1 = 1.5 \times 10^6$ and $Z_2 = 1.7 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$. Calculate: (i) the depth of the reflecting interface, and (ii) the intensity reflection coefficient. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Depth of the interface (3 marks):**

Using the round-trip formula:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 78 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{0.12012}{2} = 0.06006 \text{ m} = 6.006 \text{ cm}$$

The interface is at a depth of **6.0 cm**.

**(ii) Reflection coefficient (4 marks):**

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1.7 \times 10^{6} - 1.5 \times 10^{6}}{1.7 \times 10^{6} + 1.5 \times 10^{6}}\right)^{2}$$

$$R = \left(\frac{0.2 \times 10^{6}}{3.2 \times 10^{6}}\right)^{2} = (0.0625)^{2} = 0.00391$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$R = 0.391\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Interpretation:** Only **0.4% of the ultrasound intensity** is reflected at this fat–muscle interface. This *low* reflection explains why soft-tissue ultrasound images have a characteristic **speckled, low-contrast appearance** — most of the energy passes through, with only small fractions returning to the probe. Bone, by contrast, reflects ~40%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasound depth formula — Examiners specifically look for $d = ct/2$. Writing $d = ct$ loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up B-mode and A-mode** — A-mode is a 1-D amplitude plot; B-mode is a 2-D brightness map. Stating "B-mode is 1-D" is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong units for γ in Larmor equation** — Some students use 42.58 Hz/T instead of MHz/T. Always verify: for 1 T, hydrogen resonates at 42.58 **MHz**, not Hz.
> 4. **Not stating the Larmor equation explicitly** in part (a) of MRI questions — examiners allocate 1 mark purely for the formula.
> 5. **Confusing HU of fat vs air** — Air = −1000, fat = −100. Reversing these is a 1-mark cut.
> 6. **Skipping the clinical interpretation step** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards answers that connect physics to medicine. A calculation without a clinical remark loses application marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Module 4: Scanning System and Applications**

### Core Principles
- ✅ **CT** uses **X-rays + filtered back-projection**; Hounsfield scale is the universal density reference (air −1000 → bone +3000).
- ✅ **MRI** uses **strong magnetic field + RF pulses**; governed by the Larmor equation $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ (42.58 MHz/T for ¹H).
- ✅ **Ultrasound** uses **piezoelectric transducers** at 2–15 MHz; depth = $ct/2$ with $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- ✅ **PET** detects **511 keV annihilation photons** in coincidence; reveals metabolic function.
- ✅ **SPECT** uses **single-photon emitters** (e.g., ⁹⁹ᵐTc at 140 keV) with a rotating gamma camera.

### Critical Numbers to Memorize
- ✅ $c_{\text{soft tissue}} = 1540$ m/s
- ✅ $\gamma_H = 42.58$ MHz/T
- ✅ $E_{\text{annihilation}} = 511$ keV
- ✅ $Z_{\text{air-skin}}$ causes ~99.9% reflection (gel required)
- ✅ 1.5 T MRI → 63.87 MHz RF; 3 T MRI → 127.74 MHz RF

### Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ (Beer–Lambert law)
- ✅ $\text{HU} = 1000 (\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ (Hounsfield scale)
- ✅ $d = ct/2$ (Ultrasound depth)
- ✅ $Z = \rho c$ (Acoustic impedance)
- ✅ $R = [(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)]^2$ (Reflection coefficient)
- ✅ $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ (Larmor frequency)
- ✅ $S(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})e^{-t/T_2}$ (MRI signal)

### Engineering & Clinical Take-aways
- ✅ CT is fastest, MRI is softest-tissue-best, Ultrasound is safest, PET is most functional.
- ✅ Hybrid systems (PET-CT, PET-MRI) combine **anatomy + function**.
- ✅ Coupling gel is mandatory in ultrasound (acoustic-impedance matching).
- ✅ MRI uses no ionising radiation but cannot image patients with ferromagnetic implants.
- ✅ Image reconstruction algorithms: **FBP (CT)**, **Fourier Transform (MRI)**, **Iterative OSEM (PET)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, always pair a *diagram* with a *mathematical derivation* and a *clinical interpretation*. Examiners reward answers that bridge physics, engineering, and medicine — the hallmark of a 2024-scheme outcome-based education (OBE) response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Imaging Systems: Basic Pulse Echo System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Imaging Systems: Basic Pulse Echo System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse-Echo System (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Imaging System** is a non-invasive diagnostic modality that operates on the principle of **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** measurement. It transmits a short-duration, high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically **1 MHz – 20 MHz**) into biological tissue via a **piezoelectric transducer**, and then detects the reflected echoes from acoustic impedance mismatches at tissue interfaces. The system uses the round-trip travel time of the pulse to determine the depth (range) of internal anatomical structures, forming a one-dimensional map of tissue boundaries known as an **A-scan (Amplitude scan)**.

The fundamental physical constant governing the system is the **average speed of ultrasound in soft tissue**, which is standardized at:

$$c \approx 1540 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1540 m/s?**
> The KTU syllabus (and international standards like AIUM) adopt **1540 m/s** as the canonical average velocity because it represents a reasonable mid-point across diverse soft tissues (fat ≈ 1450 m/s, muscle ≈ 1580 m/s, blood ≈ 1570 m/s). This single value is used in all range-equation calculations to avoid tissue-specific calibration.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat in a Dark Cave"

Imagine you are standing at the mouth of a **completely dark cave**. You cannot see anything inside. However, you can shout loudly and listen carefully to the **echoes that bounce back** off the cave walls, stalactites, and hidden boulders.

- If an echo returns **very quickly** → the object is **close**.
- If an echo returns **after a noticeable delay** → the object is **far away**.
- If multiple echoes return at **different times** → there are **multiple objects at different depths**.

A **pulse-echo ultrasound system works on exactly this principle** — except instead of sound waves in air, it uses **ultrasonic (mechanical) waves in tissue**, and instead of your voice, a **piezoelectric crystal** acts as both the "shouter" and the "listener."

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this analogy:** A bat navigating darkness = A pulse-echo system navigating the human body. The "shouts" are ultrasound pulses, the "echoes" are reflections, and the "time delay" reveals the depth of structures.

---

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric Effect** | Property of certain crystals (e.g., PZT, lead zirconate titanate) to convert electrical energy ↔ mechanical (acoustic) energy. |
| **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** | Product of tissue density and acoustic velocity: $Z = \rho c$. Units: **Rayl (kg/m²·s)**. |
| **Reflection Coefficient (R)** | Fraction of incident intensity reflected at an interface: $R = \left[\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right]^2$. |
| **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** | Number of ultrasonic pulses transmitted per second (typically **1 kHz – 10 kHz**). |
| **Pulse Repetition Period (PRP)** | Time between consecutive pulses: $PRP = 1/PRF$. |
| **A-Scan** | One-dimensional display showing echo amplitude vs. depth. |
| **B-Scan** | Two-dimensional brightness-mode image formed by scanning the A-scan beam laterally. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The pulse-echo system executes a strictly **sequential cycle** for every image line:

1. **Pulse Generation (Transmitter Stage):**
   The system's pulser applies a brief high-voltage spike (typically **100 – 400 V**, lasting ~1 µs) to the piezoelectric crystal. This excites the crystal into mechanical vibration, producing a short ultrasonic pulse.

2. **Transmission (Acoustic Coupling Stage):**
   The pulse travels through a **coupling gel** (acoustic impedance ~1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl, matching skin) into the body. The gel eliminates the air-skin interface that would otherwise reflect 99.9% of the energy.

3. **Propagation & Reflection (Interaction Stage):**
   As the pulse propagates, it encounters interfaces between tissues of differing **acoustic impedance (Z)**. At each interface, a fraction of the energy is reflected back (echo), and the rest continues deeper.

4. **Reception (Echo Detection Stage):**
   The same transducer (acting in **receive mode**, switched by a T/R switch) detects the returning echoes. The crystal converts the mechanical echo pressure back into a tiny electrical voltage.

5. **Signal Processing (Receiver Stage):**
   The receiver **amplifies** (TGC — Time Gain Compensation, to compensate for attenuation), **envelopes** (rectification + low-pass filtering), and **compresses** the dynamic range of the signal.

6. **Display (Image Formation Stage):**
   The processed signal is raster-displayed as an **A-scan** (1D: amplitude vs. time/depth) or, when beam-scanned laterally, as a **B-scan** (2D: brightness vs. position).

---

### 2.2 The Heart of the System — The Range Equation

The single most important equation in the entire ultrasonic imaging module is the **Range Equation**, which relates the round-trip echo time to the depth of the reflector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Range Equation (Derivation in Section 3):**
> $$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
> where $d$ = depth of reflector, $c$ = speed of sound in tissue (**1540 m/s**), $t$ = round-trip echo time, and the factor of **2** accounts for the pulse traveling *to* the reflector AND *back* to the transducer.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print This!)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner can ask under this topic. The vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | **Range Equation** — Depth from round-trip time | $d$ [m], $c$ [m/s], $t$ [s] |
| 2 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | **Acoustic Impedance** | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 3 | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | **Reflection Coefficient (Intensity)** | Dimensionless (0 → 1) |
| 4 | $T = 1 - R$ | **Transmission Coefficient** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\alpha_{\text{dB}} = \dfrac{20}{x}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_x}\right)$ | **Attenuation Coefficient** | dB/cm |
| 6 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | **Doppler Shift** (for completeness) | Hz |
| 7 | $PRP = \dfrac{1}{PRF}$ | **Pulse Repetition Period** | s |
| 8 | $\Delta x = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | **Axial Resolution** (bandwidth-limited) | m |
| 9 | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | **Maximum Unambiguous Range** | m |
| 10 | $\theta = 1.22 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | **Beam Divergence Angle** | radians |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pulse-echo ultrasound is the **workhorse of modern radiology and obstetrics** because it is:
- **Non-ionizing** (unlike X-ray/CT — safe for fetuses).
- **Real-time** (frame rates up to **60 fps** in echocardiography).
- **Portable and inexpensive** (a handheld unit costs less than a high-end stethoscope).
- **Versatile** — used in obstetrics, cardiology, vascular imaging, abdominal imaging, and emergency medicine (eFAST scans).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection:** The pulse-echo principle is the direct biomedical cousin of **SONAR (submarine navigation)** and **RADAR (aircraft detection)** — all three rely on the same ToF physics. This is a favorite 2-3 mark question for KTU examiners ("Compare ultrasound with radar/sonar").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Range Equation (The KTU "Money" Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed question** in every KTU ESE. Memorize the logic, not just the formula.

**Given:**
- An ultrasonic pulse is emitted at time $t = 0$ from the transducer surface.
- The pulse travels through tissue at speed $c$ (a known constant, $1540$ m/s).
- The pulse hits a tissue interface at depth $d$ and is reflected back.
- The echo returns to the transducer at time $t = t_{\text{echo}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the one-way distance.**

The pulse travels from the transducer to the reflector — a one-way distance of exactly $d$ meters. The time required for this one-way trip is:

$$t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Reasoning:** This is simply the kinematic relation $d = c \cdot t$, rearranged for $t$. It is the most basic uniform-motion formula in physics.

**Step 2 — Define the return (echo) journey.**

After reflection, the echo travels the *same* distance $d$ back to the transducer, at the *same* speed $c$. The time for the return trip is therefore identical:

$$t_{\text{return}} = \frac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two legs to get the total round-trip time.**

$$t_{\text{echo}} = t_{\text{one-way}} + t_{\text{return}} = \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the relation to solve for $d$ (the unknown depth).**

Multiplying both sides by $c$:

$$c \cdot t_{\text{echo}} = 2d$$

Dividing both sides by $2$:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}}$$

This is the **Pulse-Echo Range Equation** — a cornerstone of KTU Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight on the "÷2":** Ultrasound is a *two-way* trip. The pulse must leave *and* come back, so the same physical depth $d$ is traversed **twice**. Forgetting the factor of 2 is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU numericals — it doubles the apparent depth.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Unambiguous Range

**Step 1 — Define the constraint.**

A pulse-echo system cannot "see" deeper than the distance sound can travel in one **Pulse Repetition Period (PRP)**. If a pulse takes longer than PRP to return, the system will incorrectly assign the late echo to the *next* pulse cycle → **range ambiguity**.

**Step 2 — Express the maximum one-way travel distance.**

The sound can travel outward for at most half the PRP (the other half is the return trip):

$$d_{\max} = c \cdot \frac{PRP}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $PRP = 1/PRF$.**

$$d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check (typical KTU value).**

For a typical diagnostic scanner with $PRF = 5 \text{ kHz}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times 5000 \text{ s}^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{1540}{10000} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.154 \text{ m} \\
&= 15.4 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the typical abdominal imaging depth — a useful KTU exam sanity check.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Axial Resolution

**Step 1 — Define axial resolution.**

Axial resolution $\Delta x$ is the **minimum separation along the beam axis** at which two reflectors can be distinguished as separate echoes.

**Step 2 — Relate resolution to pulse spatial length.**

The minimum resolvable distance equals half the spatial length of the transmitted pulse. The spatial pulse length is the speed of sound times the pulse duration $\tau$:

$$L_{\text{pulse}} = c \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — Relate pulse duration to bandwidth.**

The pulse duration is approximately the inverse of the transducer bandwidth $\Delta f$:

$$\tau \approx \frac{1}{\Delta f}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain axial resolution.**

$$\Delta x = \frac{L_{\text{pulse}}}{2} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Higher transducer bandwidth → shorter pulse → better axial resolution. Modern broadband probes target $\Delta f$ of 4–8 MHz to achieve sub-millimeter axial resolution.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An ultrasound pulse-echo system uses a transducer operating at $f = 5 \text{ MHz}$ in soft tissue. An echo from a tissue interface returns after $t = 26 \text{ µs}$. Determine:
(a) The depth of the interface.
(b) The wavelength in tissue.
(c) The maximum unambiguous range if $PRF = 8 \text{ kHz}$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Depth:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{c \cdot t}{2} \\
&= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s} \times 26 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1540 \times 26}{2} \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= \frac{40040}{2} \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= 20020 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.02002 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 2.00 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Wavelength:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{c}{f} \\
&= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \\
&= 308 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.308 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} \\
&= \frac{1540}{2 \times 8000} \\
&= \frac{1540}{16000} \\
&= 0.09625 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 9.6 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

Since the reflector at 2 cm lies well within $d_{\max} = 9.6$ cm, the system will produce an **unambiguous** reading.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Basic Pulse-Echo System

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of a pulse-echo system, illustrating how electrical signals, mechanical (acoustic) signals, and display signals interact. This block diagram is the single most-asked KTU diagram for this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pulser and Transmitter] --> A2[T and R Switch]
    A2 --> A3[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    A3 -- "Ultrasonic Pulse OUT" --> A4[Tissue Medium with Reflectors]
    A4 -- "Echo IN" --> A3
    A3 -- "Electrical Echo Signal" --> A2
    A2 --> A5[RF Amplifier]
    A5 --> A6[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    A6 --> A7[Envelope Detector Rectifier and LPF]
    A7 --> A8[Scan Converter and Display]
    A8 --> A9[A-Scan or B-Scan Image]
    A1 -- "Sync Trigger" --> A8

    classDef stageA fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000
    classDef stageB fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef stageC fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 stageA
    class A5,A6,A7 stageB
    class A8,A9 stageC
```

### 4.2 Annotated Description of Each Block (KTU Examiner Expectation)

| Block | Function | Key Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Pulser / Transmitter** | Generates the high-voltage excitation spike | Voltage: 100–400 V; Duration: ~1 µs |
| **T/R Switch** | Routes the same transducer between TX and RX modes | Critical for isolation of high TX voltage from sensitive RX |
| **Piezoelectric Transducer** | Converts electrical ↔ acoustic energy | Material: PZT (lead zirconate titanate) |
| **Tissue Medium** | Path of acoustic propagation; contains reflectors | $c \approx 1540$ m/s assumed uniform |
| **RF Amplifier** | Boosts the weak returning echo voltage | Gain: 40–100 dB |
| **TGC (Time Gain Compensation)** | Compensates for depth-dependent attenuation | Deeper echoes get higher gain |
| **Envelope Detector** | Extracts the amplitude envelope (rectifier + LPF) | Removes the high-frequency carrier |
| **Scan Converter / Display** | Renders the A-scan or B-scan on the monitor | CRT, LCD, or OLED |
| **Sync Trigger** | Synchronizes the transmitter with the display sweep | Ensures echo aligns with correct depth on screen |

### 4.3 The A-Scan Display — Conceptual Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Transducer Surface t = 0] --> B2[First Echo Skin-Fat Interface t1]
    B2 --> B3[Second Echo Muscle-Liver Interface t2]
    B3 --> B4[Third Echo Liver-Diaphragm Interface t3]
    B1 -.-> B5[Horizontal Axis Depth d]
    B2 -.-> B5
    B3 -.-> B5
    B4 -.-> B5

    classDef echo fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef axis fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 echo
    class B5 axis
```

> [!NOTE]
> In an A-scan, the **horizontal axis represents depth (time)** and the **vertical axis represents echo amplitude**. Each spike corresponds to a tissue interface. The amplitude of the spike is proportional to the acoustic impedance mismatch (i.e., reflection coefficient $R$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define acoustic impedance. Why is the acoustic impedance of soft tissue important in the design of ultrasonic imaging systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!TIP]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Acoustic impedance $Z$ of a medium is the product of its density $\rho$ and the speed of sound $c$ in that medium:
> $$Z = \rho \cdot c$$
> It is measured in **Rayl** (kg/m²·s).

> **Importance (2 marks):** The acoustic impedance mismatch between two adjacent tissues determines the fraction of the incident ultrasound pulse that is reflected at their interface, as given by:
> $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$
> - If $Z_1 = Z_2$ (no mismatch) → $R = 0$ → no echo, structure is invisible.
> - Large mismatch (e.g., soft-tissue-to-bone, or soft-tissue-to-air) → $R \to 1$ → near-total reflection.
> In a pulse-echo system, the magnitude of each received echo depends directly on $R$, so $Z$ is fundamental to **image contrast** and **detectability** of structures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State and explain the range equation used in a pulse-echo ultrasonic imaging system. Why is there a factor of 2 in the denominator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** The depth $d$ of a tissue interface is given by:
> $$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$
> where $c = 1540$ m/s is the speed of ultrasound in soft tissue and $t$ is the round-trip echo time.

> **Explanation (2 marks):** The pulse travels from the transducer to the reflector (one-way distance $d$) and back again to the transducer (return distance $d$), for a total round-trip distance of $2d$. The factor of 2 in the denominator correctly accounts for this **two-way (round-trip) propagation** so that the calculated depth equals the true one-way distance to the reflector. Forgetting this factor leads to a depth value that is **twice the actual depth** — a common and penalized error in KTU numericals.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question offers an internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **basic pulse-echo ultrasonic imaging system**. Label all blocks and describe the function of the transducer, T/R switch, and TGC amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into soft tissue. An echo returns after $t = 39 \text{ µs}$. Calculate the depth of the reflector and the wavelength of the ultrasound in tissue, given that the operating frequency is $f = 3.5 \text{ MHz}$ and the speed of sound in tissue is $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks):**

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the block diagram (use the Mermaid-rendered diagram from Section 4.1 as a reference; on paper, a clean rectangular block diagram with arrows is required). The blocks are:
>
> 1. **Pulser / Transmitter** — generates high-voltage spike.
> 2. **T/R (Transmit/Receive) Switch** — routes the transducer to either TX or RX mode.
> 3. **Piezoelectric Transducer** — converts electrical ↔ acoustic.
> 4. **Tissue Medium** — propagation path with reflectors.
> 5. **RF Amplifier** — amplifies the weak echo voltage.
> 6. **Time Gain Compensation (TGC)** — depth-dependent gain to compensate for attenuation.
> 7. **Envelope Detector** — extracts amplitude envelope via rectification and low-pass filtering.
> 8. **Display** — presents the A-scan or B-scan image.

> **Working Description (2 marks):** The pulser excites the transducer with a short high-voltage pulse, producing a brief ultrasonic wave. This wave propagates into the body, where it is partially reflected at tissue interfaces. The reflected echoes return to the same transducer, which converts them back into electrical signals. These are amplified (with TGC), processed (envelope detection), and displayed.

> **Specific Block Functions (2 marks):**
> - **Transducer (PZT crystal):** Exploits the piezoelectric effect to convert the pulser's electrical energy into an outgoing acoustic pulse, and the returning echo's mechanical pressure into a corresponding electrical voltage.
> - **T/R Switch:** During transmission, it connects the transducer to the pulser (which can supply hundreds of volts). During reception, it disconnects the pulser and routes the tiny echo voltage to the sensitive receiver amplifier, protecting the receiver from the destructive high-voltage TX pulse.
> - **TGC Amplifier:** Acoustic attenuation in tissue (typically $0.5$–$1$ dB/cm/MHz) causes echoes from deeper structures to be weaker. The TGC applies a time-varying gain that increases with elapsed time (i.e., with depth), compensating for this attenuation and producing a uniform image brightness.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

> **Depth Calculation (4 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> d &= \frac{c \cdot t}{2} \\
> &= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s} \times 39 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{2} \\
> &= \frac{1540 \times 39}{2} \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
> &= \frac{60060}{2} \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
> &= 30030 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
> &= 0.03003 \text{ m} \\
> &\approx 3.00 \text{ cm}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Wavelength Calculation (3 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \lambda &= \frac{c}{f} \\
> &= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{3.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \\
> &= 440 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
> &= 0.44 \text{ mm}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Final Answer Box:**
> $$\boxed{d \approx 3.00 \text{ cm} \quad ; \quad \lambda = 0.44 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **acoustic impedance** and the **reflection coefficient** at a tissue interface. Calculate the reflection coefficient at a soft-tissue-to-bone interface, given:
- $Z_{\text{soft tissue}} = 1.63 \times 10^{6} \text{ Rayl}$
- $Z_{\text{bone}} = 7.80 \times 10^{6} \text{ Rayl}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulse-echo system has a maximum imaging depth of $20 \text{ cm}$ in soft tissue. Calculate the **maximum Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** and the **Pulse Repetition Period (PRP)**. If the transducer bandwidth is $\Delta f = 4 \text{ MHz}$, calculate the **axial resolution**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Acoustic Impedance and Reflection Coefficient (7 marks):**

> **Concept (3 marks):**
> **Acoustic Impedance (Z):** Defined as $Z = \rho c$, where $\rho$ is the medium density and $c$ is the speed of sound. It represents the resistance of a medium to the propagation of an acoustic wave. Units: Rayl (kg/m²·s).
>
> **Reflection Coefficient (R):** When an ultrasound wave traveling in medium 1 (impedance $Z_1$) strikes an interface with medium 2 (impedance $Z_2$), the fraction of the incident **intensity** that is reflected back is:
> $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$
> It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1. The remaining fraction $T = 1 - R$ is transmitted into medium 2.

> **Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**
> Given: $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl, $Z_2 = 7.80 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl.
> $$\begin{aligned}
> Z_2 - Z_1 &= 7.80 \times 10^{6} - 1.63 \times 10^{6} \\
> &= 6.17 \times 10^{6} \text{ Rayl} \\
> Z_2 + Z_1 &= 7.80 \times 10^{6} + 1.63 \times 10^{6} \\
> &= 9.43 \times 10^{6} \text{ Rayl} \\
> \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} &= \frac{6.17 \times 10^{6}}{9.43 \times 10^{6}} \\
> &= 0.6543 \\
> R &= (0.6543)^2 \\
> &= 0.4281 \\
> &\approx 0.428 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 42.8\%
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Physical Interpretation (included in the 3-mark concept portion):** About 42.8% of the incident intensity is reflected at the soft-tissue-to-bone interface. This is why bones appear as bright (highly echogenic) boundaries in ultrasound images.

**(b) Maximum PRF, PRP, and Axial Resolution (7 marks):**

> **Maximum PRF (3 marks):** From the maximum unambiguous range formula:
> $$d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF_{\max}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PRF_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot d_{\max}}$$
> $$\begin{aligned}
> PRF_{\max} &= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times 0.20 \text{ m}} \\
> &= \frac{1540}{0.40} \\
> &= 3850 \text{ Hz} \\
> &= 3.85 \text{ kHz}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **PRP (2 marks):**
> $$PRP = \frac{1}{PRF_{\max}} = \frac{1}{3850} \approx 2.60 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 260 \text{ µs}$$

> **Axial Resolution (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \Delta x &= \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f} \\
> &= \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \\
> &= \frac{1540}{8 \times 10^{6}} \text{ m} \\
> &= 192.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
> &\approx 0.193 \text{ mm}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Final Answer Box:**
> $$\boxed{PRF_{\max} \approx 3.85 \text{ kHz} \;;\; PRP \approx 260 \text{ µs} \;;\; \Delta x \approx 0.193 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgot the "÷2" in the range equation** → You will lose **2 of 3 marks** instantly on any depth numerical. The single most frequent mistake in KTU answer sheets.
> 2. **Used $c = 340$ m/s (speed of sound in air)** instead of $1540$ m/s (speed in tissue) → Entire answer is wrong. Always default to $c = 1540$ m/s for any *tissue* ultrasound problem.
> 3. **Confused Reflection Coefficient (intensity) with Reflection Coefficient (pressure/amplitude)** → For *intensity* use $R = [(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)]^2$; for *pressure amplitude* use $r_p = (Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)$ (no square). KTU questions almost always ask for *intensity* $R$.
> 4. **Did not draw the T/R switch in the block diagram** → Mandatory block; expect 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipped units in the final answer** → 0.5 mark deduction per missing unit. Always write "**m**", "**cm**", "**µs**", "**kHz**" etc.
> 6. **Omitted the function of the TGC** → KTU examiners specifically test this — they want "compensates for depth-dependent attenuation" verbatim.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **The 60-Second Revision List — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall!**

- ⭐ **Standard speed of ultrasound in soft tissue:** $c = 1540$ m/s (always; never use 340 m/s).
- ⭐ **Range Equation (the king formula):** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ — *always* divide by 2.
- ⭐ **Acoustic Impedance:** $Z = \rho c$, measured in **Rayl**.
- ⭐ **Reflection Coefficient (intensity):** $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$.
- ⭐ **Transmission Coefficient:** $T = 1 - R$.
- ⭐ **Block Diagram Order:** Pulser → T/R Switch → Transducer → Tissue → T/R Switch → RF Amp → TGC → Envelope Detector → Display.
- ⭐ **Piezoelectric Material:** Lead Zirconate Titanate (**PZT**).
- ⭐ **TGC Purpose:** Compensates for attenuation; deeper echoes get higher gain.
- ⭐ **A-Scan:** 1-D amplitude vs. depth (time).
- ⭐ **B-Scan:** 2-D brightness image (multiple A-scans stacked laterally).
- ⭐ **Axial Resolution:** $\Delta x = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ — *higher bandwidth = better resolution*.
- ⭐ **Maximum Unambiguous Range:** $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ — *higher PRF = shallower imaging*.
- ⭐ **Wavelength:** $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ — determines beam focusability.
- ⭐ **Coupling Gel:** Eliminates the air-skin impedance mismatch (which would otherwise reflect 99.9%).
- ⭐ **Diagnostic Frequency Range:** **1 MHz – 20 MHz** (lower for deep imaging, higher for shallow high-resolution).
- ⭐ **Biomedical Cousins:** SONAR (submarines) and RADAR (aircraft) use the *exact same* ToF principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Ultrasonic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Different Types of Ultrasonic Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Ultrasonic System** is a non-invasive biomedical diagnostic device that operates on the principle of **acoustic wave propagation** at frequencies exceeding the upper audible limit of human hearing (**> 20 kHz**). Clinical diagnostic ultrasound typically operates in the range of **1 MHz to 20 MHz**, with the most common frequencies being **2 MHz, 3.5 MHz, 5 MHz, 7.5 MHz, and 10 MHz** for various tissue penetration depths. The fundamental principle relies on the **piezoelectric effect**, wherein a piezoelectric crystal (commonly **Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT**) converts high-frequency electrical pulses into mechanical pressure waves, and vice versa. The system uses the **pulse-echo technique** to determine the depth, structure, and motion of internal body tissues based on the time-of-flight and intensity of reflected echoes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the classification of ultrasonic systems based on **display mode (A, B, M)** and **signal processing technique (Doppler)**. Students must master the operating principle, display characteristics, and clinical applications of each modality. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bat and Submarine" Intuition Imagine a **bat flying in a dark cave**. The bat cannot see, but it screams inaudible high-frequency sound waves, and listens carefully for the echo. From the **time delay** between the shout and the echo, it calculates the **distance** to the wall. From the **pitch (frequency) of the returning echo**, it determines whether the wall is moving closer (higher pitch) or moving away (lower pitch). An **ultrasonic diagnostic system works identically**: - The **transmitter** is the "bat's mouth" (piezoelectric crystal emitting sound) - The **tissue interfaces** are the "cave walls" (boundaries between organs) - The **receiver** is the "bat's ears" (same crystal detecting echoes) - The **time delay** tells us **how deep** the tissue is - The **frequency shift** (Doppler effect) tells us **how fast blood is flowing** > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Pulse-Echo Principle:** Ultrasound is generated, transmitted into the body, and the echoes returning from tissue interfaces are processed to construct a diagnostic image or waveform. The round-trip time of the echo determines the depth of the reflecting structure. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Diagnostic ultrasound frequency | **1 – 20** | MHz | | Velocity of sound in soft tissue | **1540** | m/s | | Velocity in fat | 1450 | m/s | | Velocity in bone | 4080 | m/s | | Velocity in blood | 1570 | m/s | | Density of soft tissue (avg) | 1060 | kg/m³ | | Acoustic impedance of soft tissue | **1.63 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) (Rayl) | | Human hearing limit | 20 | kHz | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Ultrasonic Systems Ultrasonic systems used in biomedical engineering are broadly classified into **two major families**: #### **Family A — Pulse-Echo Imaging Systems (Time-Domain)** These systems rely on the **time of flight** of the reflected echo to map tissue boundaries. #### **Family B — Doppler Flowmetry Systems (Frequency-Domain)** These systems rely on the **frequency shift** between transmitted and reflected waves to measure velocity of moving reflectors (typically red blood cells). --- ### 2.2 Detailed Analysis — A-Scan (Amplitude Mode) **Principle of Operation:** A single ultrasonic beam is transmitted into the body along a single line. As the beam crosses tissue interfaces, partial reflections occur. The returning echoes are amplified and displayed as **vertical spikes (deflections)** on a horizontal time-base. The **amplitude (height)** of each spike represents echo strength, and the **horizontal position** represents the time-of-flight (depth). **Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. Transmitter sends a short **pulse** (typically 2 cycles, lasting ~1 μs) 2. Pulse travels through tissue at velocity **c = 1540 m/s** 3. At each tissue interface (with acoustic impedance mismatch), part of the energy is **reflected**, part is **transmitted** 4. The reflected echo returns to the transducer after a round-trip time **t** 5. Depth is calculated as: **d = (c × t) / 2** 6. Echo amplitude is proportional to the **reflection coefficient R** at the interface **Clinical Applications:** - Ophthalmology (eye measurements — axial length of the eye) - Echoencephalography (brain midline shift detection) - Fetal cephalometry (skull diameter measurement) > [!NOTE] > **One-dimensional system** — Provides depth information only, no cross-sectional image. The display is a waveform on a CRT or LCD. --- ### 2.3 Detailed Analysis — B-Scan (Brightness Mode) **Principle of Operation:** A **B-Scan (Brightness Modulation)** system mechanically or electronically scans the ultrasonic beam across a 2D plane of the body. Each echo is displayed as a **dot whose brightness is proportional to echo amplitude**. As the beam sweeps, thousands of dots accumulate to form a **2D cross-sectional anatomical image** of the tissue. **Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. The transducer is moved along the body surface (linear, sector, or convex motion) 2. At each beam position, an echo profile is generated 3. Echoes are converted into **brightness-modulated dots** on a 2D display 4. The X-axis represents lateral position, Y-axis represents depth 5. The image is built up line-by-line, frozen, and displayed in real-time at frame rates of **15–60 frames per second** **Clinical Applications:** - Abdominal imaging (liver, kidneys, spleen) - Obstetric imaging (fetal development) - Cardiac imaging (when combined with M-mode) - Vascular imaging > [!IMPORTANT] > **B-Scan vs A-Scan Comparison:** A-Scan displays echoes as **amplitude spikes** (1D waveform). B-Scan displays echoes as **brightness dots** (2D image). The 'B' stands for **Brightness**, not "B-mode" — both terms are used interchangeably in KTU exams. --- ### 2.4 Detailed Analysis — M-Mode (Motion Mode) **Principle of Operation:** M-Mode (Motion Mode) is a **time-motion display** used primarily in **cardiology** to record the motion of moving structures (heart valves, heart walls). The transducer is held stationary, emitting pulses along a single line, and the echoes are plotted as a function of time. **Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. Transducer is held **fixed** at one position (e.g., along the heart's long axis) 2. Pulses are emitted at a high **pulse repetition frequency (PRF)** 3. Echoes from moving structures (valves, walls) are displayed as **vertical brightness dots** 4. The horizontal axis represents **time**, the vertical axis represents **depth** 5. Moving structures produce **wavy/undulating patterns** as a function of time **Clinical Applications:** - Echocardiography (heart valve motion, wall thickness measurement) - Fetal heart rate monitoring > [!NOTE] > **M-Mode is essentially an A-Scan with the time-base sweeping horizontally** instead of a single shot. It excels at capturing **high-speed motion** with excellent temporal resolution (~1 ms). --- ### 2.5 Detailed Analysis — Doppler Ultrasound Systems **Principle of Operation:** When ultrasound strikes a **moving target** (such as red blood cells), the frequency of the reflected wave is **shifted**. This **Doppler frequency shift** is directly proportional to the velocity of the moving target. There are **three major subtypes** of Doppler ultrasound: #### **2.5.1 Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler** - Uses **two separate crystals** — one continuously transmitting, one continuously receiving - No depth discrimination (receives echoes from all depths along the beam) - Can measure **very high velocities** without aliasing - Used in: peripheral vascular studies, fetal heart monitors #### **2.5.2 Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler** - Uses a **single crystal** operating in pulse-echo mode - Provides **range gating** — can select echoes from a specific depth - Limited maximum velocity due to **Nyquist limit** (PRF must be > 2 × Doppler shift) - Used in: cardiac flow studies at specific locations #### **2.5.3 Color Doppler Flow Imaging** - Combines B-Scan imaging with Doppler velocity information - Blood flow is **color-coded**: typically **red** for flow toward the transducer, **blue** for flow away (BART convention — **B**lue **A**way, **R**ed **T**oward) - Provides a **2D real-time flow map** overlaid on the B-mode image - Used in: cardiac valve regurgitation, vascular stenosis detection > [!IMPORTANT] > **BART Convention (KTU Frequently Asked):** Blue Away, Red Toward. This is a **standardized international convention** in color Doppler imaging. The convention is reversed in some Asian systems, but KTU exams follow the BART rule. --- ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth of reflecting interface (round-trip) | m | | 2 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation (velocity = frequency × wavelength) | m/s | | 3 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance (Rayl) | kg/(m²·s) | | 4 | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Reflection coefficient (intensity) | dimensionless | | 5 | $T = 1 - R$ | Transmission coefficient | dimensionless | | 6 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift equation | Hz | | 7 | $v = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Velocity from Doppler shift | m/s | | 8 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-2\alpha x}$ | Attenuation (intensity vs depth) | W/m² | | 9 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot f^n$ | Attenuation coefficient (n ≈ 1 for soft tissue) | dB/cm | | 10 | $PRF_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}}$ | Maximum PRF (unambiguous range) | Hz | **Where:** - $c$ = velocity of sound in tissue (1540 m/s) - $f_0$ = transmitted frequency - $v$ = velocity of blood/target - $\theta$ = angle between beam and flow direction - $\rho$ = density of medium - $Z_1, Z_2$ = acoustic impedances of adjacent tissues - $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient - $PRF$ = Pulse Repetition Frequency ### 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Safe, non-ionizing** alternative to X-ray and CT (especially for pregnancy) - **Real-time imaging** at 30+ frames per second - **Bedside portability** for emergency and ICU use - **Cost-effective** compared to MRI and CT - **No known harmful effects** at diagnostic intensities (unlike X-rays) - Used in: **cardiology, obstetrics, radiology, vascular surgery, ophthalmology, emergency medicine** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Depth Calculation in Pulse-Echo Systems **Given:** A pulse-echo ultrasonic system transmits a 3 MHz pulse into soft tissue. The receiver detects an echo 26 μs after transmission. **Find:** The depth of the reflecting interface. **Derivation:** The ultrasonic pulse travels from the transducer to the tissue interface and back. The **total distance traveled** is $2d$ (where $d$ is the one-way depth). The relationship between distance, velocity, and time is: $$c = \frac{\text{Total Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{2d}{t}$$ Rearranging to solve for depth: $$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$ **Substituting values** (using $c = 1540$ m/s for soft tissue, $t = 26 \times 10^{-6}$ s): $$d = \frac{1540 \, \text{m/s} \times 26 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{1540 \times 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{40040 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$d = 20020 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{d = 0.02002 \, \text{m} \approx 2.0 \, \text{cm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check:** Round-trip time of 26 μs in tissue yields a one-way depth of 2 cm. This is consistent with a near-surface reflector (e.g., the front wall of a small organ). --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Wavelength from Frequency **Given:** Diagnostic ultrasound operates at 5 MHz in soft tissue. **Find:** The wavelength of the ultrasound. **Derivation:** The fundamental wave equation is: $$c = f \cdot \lambda$$ Solving for wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$ **Substituting** ($c = 1540$ m/s, $f = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz): $$\lambda = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6}$$ $$\lambda = 308 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda = 0.308 \, \text{mm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check:** Wavelengths in soft tissue at diagnostic frequencies range from **0.08 mm (at 20 MHz)** to **1.54 mm (at 1 MHz)**. Smaller wavelengths (higher frequencies) provide better resolution but poorer penetration. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Acoustic Impedance Mismatch **Given:** Acoustic impedance of fat $Z_1 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl, muscle $Z_2 = 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl. **Find:** Reflection coefficient at the fat-muscle interface. **Derivation:** The intensity reflection coefficient is: $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$ **Substituting values:** $$R = \left(\frac{1.70 \times 10^6 - 1.38 \times 10^6}{1.70 \times 10^6 + 1.38 \times 10^6}\right)^2$$ $$R = \left(\frac{0.32 \times 10^6}{3.08 \times 10^6}\right)^2$$ $$R = (0.1039)^2$$ $$R = 0.0108$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 1.08\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check:** Only ~1% of the intensity is reflected at a fat-muscle interface, meaning 99% is transmitted. This is why ultrasound **penetrates deeply** through soft tissue. Bone-soft tissue interfaces have much higher reflection (~40%), making bone a strong reflector. --- ### 3.4 Derivation — Doppler Frequency Shift **Given:** A 5 MHz Doppler probe is used to measure blood flow. The probe-beam angle is $\theta = 60°$. The detected Doppler shift is $\Delta f = 1.5$ kHz. **Find:** The velocity of blood flow. **Derivation:** The Doppler shift equation (for a **reflecting target** like red blood cells) is: $$\Delta f = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$$ Solving for velocity: $$v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}$$ **Substituting** ($\Delta f = 1500$ Hz, $c = 1540$ m/s, $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\cos 60° = 0.5$): $$v = \frac{1500 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5}$$ $$v = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{5 \times 10^6}$$ $$v = 0.462 \, \text{m/s}$$ $$\boxed{v = 46.2 \, \text{cm/s}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check:** Normal blood flow velocity in major arteries ranges from **20 to 100 cm/s**. The calculated value of 46.2 cm/s falls within the physiological range, confirming the result. --- ### 3.5 Derivation — Attenuation in Tissue **Given:** An ultrasound beam of 5 MHz intensity $I_0 = 10$ mW/cm² enters tissue with attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 1$ dB/cm/MHz. **Find:** Intensity at 4 cm depth. **Derivation:** The attenuation coefficient for a 5 MHz beam is: $$\alpha = 1 \times 5 = 5 \, \text{dB/cm}$$ Total attenuation over 4 cm: $$A_{total} = \alpha \cdot x = 5 \times 4 = 20 \, \text{dB}$$ Converting dB attenuation to intensity ratio: $$\frac{I}{I_0} = 10^{-A/10} = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$ Therefore: $$I = 0.01 \times 10 = 0.1 \, \text{mW/cm}^2$$ $$\boxed{I = 0.1 \, \text{mW/cm}^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic check:** 20 dB attenuation reduces intensity by a factor of 100. This is a realistic value for diagnostic ultrasound imaging. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ultrasonic Systems | Feature | A-Scan | B-Scan | M-Mode | CW Doppler | PW Doppler | Color Doppler | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Display Type** | 1D waveform | 2D image | Time-motion | Audio/FFT spectrum | Spectral display | 2D color overlay | | **Dimension** | Depth only | Depth + Lateral | Depth + Time | Time only | Depth + Time | 2D + Velocity | | **Information** | Boundary depth | Anatomical image | Structure motion | Velocity (any depth) | Velocity at depth | Velocity map | | **Real-time** | Yes | Yes (15–60 fps) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Clinical Use** | Ophthalmology, echoencephalography | Abdominal, obstetric | Cardiac | Vascular, fetal HR | Cardiac flow | Valvular disease | | **Transducer** | Single fixed | Scanning array | Single fixed | Two crystals | Single crystal | Phased array | | **Depth Selection** | No | No | No | No (whole beam) | Yes (range gate) | Yes | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — General Ultrasonic System Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A[Pulser
Transmitter] --> B[Transducer
PZT Crystal] B --> C[Patient
Tissue Interface] C --> D[Echo Return
Time and Amplitude] D --> E[Receiver
Amplifier TGC] E --> F[Signal Processor
Detector] F --> G[Display
CRT or LCD] G --> H[Image or
Waveform Output] I[Master Clock
Synchronizer] --> A I --> G J[Time Gain
Compensation] --> E style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style F fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c style G fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17 style H fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#558b2f style I fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0 style J fill:#fbe9e7,stroke:#bf360c ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — Different Ultrasonic System Modalities ```mermaid graph TB A[Ultrasonic
System Input] --> B{Signal
Processing Type} B -->|Time Domain
Pulse Echo| C[Pulse Echo Systems] B -->|Frequency Domain
Doppler Shift| D[Doppler Systems] C --> E[A Scan
Amplitude Mode] C --> F[B Scan

Motion Mode] D --> H[Continuous Wave
CW Doppler] D --> I[Pulsed Wave
PW Doppler] D --> J[Color Doppler
Flow Imaging] E --> K[1D Depth
Waveform] F --> L[2D Anatomical
Cross Section] G --> M[Time Motion
Cardiac Display] H --> N[Audio Output

At Specific Depth] J --> P[2D Color Coded
Flow Map BART] K --> Q[Clinical
Diagnostics] L --> Q M --> Q N --> Q O --> Q P --> Q style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style E fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a style F fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a style G fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a style H fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style I fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style J fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style Q fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Doppler Signal Processing Chain ```mermaid graph LR A[Transmitted
Signal f0] --> B[Tissue
Interaction] B --> C[Reflected
Signal f0 df] C --> D[Mixer
Demodulator] A --> D D --> E[Low Pass

Amplifier] E --> G[FFT
