<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
> *List and briefly explain any **three diagnostic imaging modalities** used in biomedical engineering.*

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

1. **X-ray Radiography (1 mark):** Uses 20–150 keV X-ray photons. Differential attenuation by tissues produces a 2-D projection; bone (high $\mu$) appears white, air (low $\mu$) appears black. Application: fracture detection, dental imaging.
2. **MRI (1 mark):** Uses strong static field $B_0$ + RF pulses at Larmor frequency. Hydrogen protons resonate, and the decaying signal is mapped to image intensity. Provides superior soft-tissue contrast.
3. **Ultrasound (1 mark):** Uses piezoelectric transducer emitting 2–15 MHz pulses. Reflected echoes from acoustic-impedance boundaries yield real-time images; depth $d = ct/2$. Application: obstetric, cardiac, abdominal.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
> *Define **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**. State the HU value of air, water, fat, muscle, and bone.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Hounsfield Unit (1 mark) is a normalized linear-attenuation scale used in CT, defined as:
>
> $$HU \;=\; 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
>
> Standard values (2 marks): **Air = −1000, Fat ≈ −100, Water = 0, Muscle ≈ +40, Bone ≈ +1000.**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In ECG questions, students often **forget Einthoven's law** $V_I + V_{II} + V_{III} = 0$. Always state it explicitly.
> 2. In MRI numericals, students confuse **MHz/T (γ)** with **the Larmor frequency in Hz**. Write units clearly.
> 3. In ultrasound questions, the factor of **2** in $d = ct/2$ is missed almost every year — the pulse must travel **down and back up**.
> 4. For defibrillator energy questions, the formula is $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ — do **not** use $E = CV^2$.
> 5. In dialysis clearance, students use $C_{out}$ in the denominator instead of $C_{in}$. Use the **inlet** concentration as the reference.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Diagnostic & Monitoring Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Question A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a CT scanner**. Derive the expression for the **Hounsfield Unit** and show that air and water map to −1000 and 0 respectively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient's heart is imaged with ultrasound. An echo from a cardiac structure returns to the transducer in **52 µs**. Given $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue, calculate the **depth of the structure**. If the same probe has a bandwidth $\Delta f = 2$ MHz, compute the **axial resolution**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram + working of CT (4 marks):**

A CT scanner consists of a rotating X-ray tube and an arc of detectors. As the gantry rotates, multiple 1-D projections of attenuation values $\mu(x)$ are acquired at different angles. The Radon transform of the object is built from these projections. An **inverse Radon transform (filtered back-projection)** then reconstructs a 2-D slice of attenuation coefficients $\mu(x, y)$, which is converted to a Hounsfield image. The patient is translated incrementally along the z-axis (helical/cone-beam) to acquire a 3-D volume.

**Derivation of HU (3 marks):**

The Hounsfield Unit is defined as:

$$HU \;=\; 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

For air ($\mu \approx 0$): $HU = 1000 \times (0 - \mu_w)/\mu_w = -1000$.
For water ($\mu = \mu_w$): $HU = 1000 \times 0 = 0$. **Hence derived.**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Depth calculation (4 marks):**

$$d \;=\; \frac{c\, t}{2} \;=\; \frac{1540 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d \;=\; \frac{1540 \times 52}{2 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; \frac{80080}{2 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 0.04004 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 4.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Axial resolution (3 marks):**

$$\Delta x_{\text{axial}} \;=\; \frac{c}{2\, \Delta f} \;=\; \frac{1540}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\Delta x_{\text{axial}} \;=\; \frac{1540}{4 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 3.85 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \;\approx\; 0.385 \text{ mm}$$

The cardiac structure lies at **≈ 4 cm depth** and the probe can resolve structures as small as **≈ 0.385 mm** apart along the beam axis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Therapeutic & Prosthetic Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Question B]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)** with a functional block diagram. Derive the **energy stored** in its output capacitor, and calculate it for $C = 120\ \mu F$ and $V = 700\ V$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **working principle of a cochlear implant** with a block diagram. Explain the function of the **electrode array** and how **frequency mapping** is achieved tonotopically. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ICD working + diagram (3 marks):**

An ICD is a small, battery-powered device implanted under the skin near the collarbone with leads running into the heart. It continuously senses the ventricular electrogram. When the internal algorithm detects ventricular fibrillation (VF) or fast ventricular tachycardia (VT), it:
1. Charges a high-voltage capacitor to 600–750 V.
2. Confirms the arrhythmia.
3. Delivers a **biphasic truncated-exponential shock** of 5–35 J through the leads, depolarizing the entire myocardium and allowing the sinus node to resume normal rhythm.

**Energy derivation (2 marks):**

$$E \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\, C\, V^{2}$$

**Numerical (2 marks):**

$$E \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times 120 \times 10^{-6} \times (700)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times 120 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.9 \times 10^{5}$$

$$E \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times 58.8 \;=\; 29.4 \text{ J}$$

The ICD stores **≈ 29.4 J**, sufficient for direct defibrillation.

**(b) Cochlear implant working (4 marks):**

A microphone captures sound → speech processor filters and analyzes the signal (typically in 16–22 frequency bands) → sound coding strategy (e.g., CIS = Continuous Interleaved Sampling, or ACE = Advanced Combination Encoder) converts band energies to electrical pulse trains → radio-frequency telemetry transmits the data across the skin → implanted receiver-stimulator decodes it and delivers biphasic current pulses to the **electrode array** (12–22 electrodes in modern devices) inserted into the scala tympani of the cochlea.

**Electrode array & tonotopic mapping (3 marks):**

The cochlea is tonotopically organized: basal end (near oval window) encodes **high frequencies**; apical end encodes **low frequencies**. The electrode array is inserted along this tonotopic axis, with **basal electrodes stimulating high-frequency neurons** and **apical electrodes stimulating low-frequency neurons**. By selecting which electrode is pulsed and at what rate, the device emulates the natural frequency-to-place mapping of a healthy cochlea, restoring the sensation of pitch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Applications**

- ☐ The **four pillars** of biomedical applications are: **Diagnostic, Monitoring, Therapeutic, Rehabilitative**.
- ☐ X-ray attenuation follows **Beer–Lambert law** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — remember the **negative sign**.
- ☐ **Hounsfield scale** is fixed: air = **−1000 HU**, water = **0 HU**, dense bone ≈ **+1000 HU**.
- ☐ **Larmor frequency** for hydrogen: $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ MHz (with $B_0$ in Tesla) — give result in **MHz** for MRI.
- ☐ Ultrasound depth: **always $d = ct/2$** because of round-trip travel; $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- ☐ Axial resolution: $\Delta x = c / (2 \Delta f)$; **higher bandwidth → finer resolution**.
- ☐ **Einthoven's law**: $V_I + V_{II} + V_{III} = 0$.
- ☐ **MAP** = $DBP + \tfrac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$, not the simple arithmetic mean.
- ☐ **Defibrillator energy** is $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; typical ICD energy is **25–40 J**, external biphasic is **120–200 J**.
- ☐ **Dialyzer clearance** $K = Q_B (C_{in} - C_{out})/C_{in}$, with **$C_{in}$ in the denominator**.
- ☐ **Pulse oximetry** uses **660 nm (red)** and **940 nm (IR)**; oxygenated blood absorbs more IR, deoxygenated absorbs more red.
- ☐ **Pacemaker** = demand type, delivers pulse **only when intrinsic rate is too low**; typical pulse is **2–5 V, 0.5–1 ms**.
- ☐ **Cochlear implant** bypasses damaged hair cells and stimulates the **auditory nerve** directly; tonotopy = frequency-to-place.
- ☐ **Myoelectric prosthetic** uses surface EMG as the control signal — typically rectified and smoothed.
- ☐ **FES** uses low-level pulses (**20–50 mA, 20–300 µs**) to produce functional movement in paralyzed muscles.
- ☐ **Lithotripsy** uses focused shock waves at **30–100 MPa** peak pressure; **0.2–0.4 mJ/mm²** energy density at focus.
- ☐ **Ventilator tidal volume** standard: **6–8 mL/kg** of ideal body weight (lung-protective strategy).
- ☐ **Materials** for implants: **Ti-6Al-4V** (titanium alloy) and **Co–Cr alloys** — biocompatible, fatigue-resistant.
- ☐ **Telemedicine** = clinical care at a distance; must comply with **data security** norms.
- ☐ Be able to **draw and label** the block diagrams of: ECG machine, EEG machine, CT scanner, MRI system, ultrasound scanner, pacemaker, ICD, hemodialysis machine, cochlear implant.

> **Final Examiner's Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, KTU examiners allocate **3 marks for diagram**, **3–4 marks for derivation**, **3–4 marks for numerical**, and **2–3 marks for explanation/examples**. Always include a labelled block diagram — it is the single highest-yield investment of your exam time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging Systems and Probes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging Systems and Probes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-time Ultrasonic Imaging Systems** are diagnostic medical devices that generate two-dimensional cross-sectional images of internal body structures at frame rates high enough to display moving tissues (e.g., heart, blood flow, fetus) without perceptible delay. The system achieves this by **electronically steering and focusing an ultrasound beam** through multi-element piezoelectric transducer probes, processing the returning echoes, and presenting them on a display monitor in real time (typically **15–60 frames per second**).

A **probe** (also called a **transducer** or **scanhead**) is the hand-held device that converts electrical pulses into acoustic energy and back, while simultaneously determining the spatial origin of returning echoes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a real-time ultrasound system as a **flashlight and echo-location crew working in a dark room**:

- The **probe (transducer)** is the flashlight that sends out very short pulses of high-frequency sound (not light) into the body.
- When the sound pulse hits an organ boundary (e.g., liver edge), part of it **bounces back** (echo).
- The system measures the **time delay** between sending and receiving the echo → this tells **how deep** the structure is.
- By sweeping the beam across many lines, a **2D image** is built line-by-line, similar to how an old TV builds a picture one scanline at a time — but **thousands of times faster**, so you see movement live.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time** simply means the image is updated so quickly (typically ≥ **15 fps**) that motion appears smooth and continuous to the human eye.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- Speed of sound in soft tissue: **c ≈ 1540 m/s** (used universally as the calibration value)
- Diagnostic frequency range: **1 MHz – 20 MHz**
- Frame rate for real-time imaging: **15–60 frames/second**
- Typical pulse repetition frequency (PRF): **1–10 kHz**
- Acoustic impedance of soft tissue: **Z ≈ 1.6 × 10⁶ rayl**
- Acoustic impedance of PZT (lead zirconate titanate): **Z ≈ 30 × 10⁶ rayl**

> [!NOTE]
> Real-time ultrasonic imaging is also called **B-mode (Brightness mode) imaging** because echo amplitudes are displayed as varying gray-scale brightness levels on the screen.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The real-time B-mode scanner executes the following sequence, repeated thousands of times per second:

1. **Pulse Generation** — The system’s transmitter sends a short, high-voltage electrical pulse (typically **±100 V**, 0.1–1 µs duration) to a selected group of elements in the probe.
2. **Piezoelectric Conversion** — The PZT elements vibrate at their resonant frequency, converting electrical energy into a short burst of acoustic energy (the ultrasound pulse).
3. **Beam Steering & Focusing** — By applying **time-delays** to different elements, the acoustic wavefront is electronically steered and focused along a specific scan line. (This is the heart of real-time imaging — explained in §2.3 below.)
4. **Echo Reception** — Returning echoes vibrate the same (or different) elements, producing tiny electrical signals (microvolts range).
5. **Beamforming (Receive)** — The receive path applies **inverse time-delays** to align echoes from a single focal point, amplifying the signal.
6. **Signal Processing** — Echoes undergo **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)**, **envelope detection**, and **logarithmic compression** to fit the wide dynamic range (~100 dB) into a viewable gray scale (~40 dB).
7. **Scan Conversion** — Polar-coordinate echo data (range, angle) is converted to Cartesian (x, y) pixel coordinates for standard video display.
8. **Display** — The frame is painted on the monitor. The system immediately moves to the next scan line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why step 7 (scan conversion) is needed:** Ultrasound data is naturally acquired in **polar coordinates** (distance from probe, beam angle), but TV monitors use **Cartesian (raster) format**. Without conversion, the image would be a pie-slice instead of a rectangle.

## 2.2 B-Mode Image Formation — The Pixel Logic

Each displayed pixel represents:

- **Brightness (B)** ∝ amplitude of returning echo (after compression)
- **Position** determined by (i) **time of flight** → depth = c·t / 2, and (ii) **beam angle** → lateral position

> The factor **½** in the depth equation is because sound travels **to the target and back** through tissue.

## 2.3 Electronic Beam Steering — The Core of Real-Time Imaging

In a **linear array** with N elements spaced at distance d, to steer the beam by angle θ from the normal, the **time delay** between successive elements is:

> **Δτ = d · sin θ / c**

For **dynamic focusing**, additional delays are applied on receive so that echoes from a chosen depth all arrive in phase at the summing amplifier. This is called **dynamic receive focusing**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Depth of reflector | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | meters | c = 1540 m/s in soft tissue |
| 2 | Round-trip time | $t = \dfrac{2d}{c}$ | seconds | Used to gate the receiver |
| 3 | Steering delay | $\Delta\tau = \dfrac{d \sin\theta}{c}$ | seconds | Linear/phased array |
| 4 | Focusing delay (near field) | $\tau_f = \dfrac{r_f}{c}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{n \cdot d}{r_f}\right)^2 - \dfrac{2 n d \sin\theta}{r_f}}\right)$ | seconds | r_f = focal range |
| 5 | Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) | $PRF = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot D_{max}}$ | Hz | D_max = max imaging depth |
| 6 | Frame rate | $F_{rate} = \dfrac{PRF}{N_{lines}}$ | fps | N_lines = lines per frame |
| 7 | Frame rate (with sector) | $F_{rate} = \dfrac{PRF \cdot \text{angular step}}{\pi}$ | fps | Phased array sector |
| 8 | Resonant frequency of PZT | $f_0 = \dfrac{v_p}{2 t_p}$ | Hz | v_p = sound velocity in PZT (~4000 m/s), t_p = element thickness |
| 9 | Axial (range) resolution | $\delta_{ax} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | meters | BW = pulse bandwidth |
| 10 | Lateral resolution | $\delta_{lat} \approx \lambda \cdot \dfrac{r}{D_a}$ | meters | r = depth, D_a = aperture, λ = wavelength |
| 11 | Wavelength in tissue | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | meters | f = operating frequency |
| 12 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | rayl (kg/m²·s) | ρ = density |
| 13 | Intensity Reflection Coeff. | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Pressure coeff: $r_p = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ |
| 14 | Intensity Transmission Coeff. | $T = 1 - R$ | dimensionless | Assuming no absorption |
| 15 | Attenuation in tissue | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha f x}$ | dB | α ≈ 0.5 dB/cm/MHz average soft tissue |
| 16 | Near-field length (Fresnel) | $N = \dfrac{D_a^2}{4\lambda}$ | meters | D_a = aperture diameter |
| 17 | Frame rate limit (sector) | $F_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot D_{max} \cdot N_{lines}}$ | fps | For real-time constraint |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — KTU board pitfall:** Use **c = 1540 m/s** for soft tissue unless a specific medium is named. Also remember the **factor of 2** for round-trip time; examiners frequently deduct marks when students forget it.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Real-time ultrasonic systems are the **workhorse of radiology, cardiology (echocardiography), obstetrics, and emergency medicine** because they are:
- **Non-ionizing** (safe for fetus and repeated use)
- **Real-time** (shows motion — valves, blood flow with Doppler)
- **Portable** (handheld units now common in ambulances)
- **Cost-effective** compared to CT/MRI

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth from Time of Flight

**Problem:** An ultrasound pulse is sent into tissue and the echo from a target returns after **t = 52 µs**. Find the depth of the target.

**Step 1: Identify governing equation.**
The pulse travels **to the target** and **back**, total distance = 2d. With speed c:

$$2d = c \cdot t$$

**Step 2: Solve for depth.**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute c = 1540 m/s and t = 52 × 10⁻⁶ s.**

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Perform the arithmetic.**

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 52}{2} \times 10^{-6}$$

$$d = 770 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$d = 40{,}040 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

$$d = 0.04004\ \text{m} \approx 4.0\ \text{cm}$$

**Answer:** The target is at a depth of **4.0 cm**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steering Delay for a Linear Array

**Problem:** A 64-element linear array has element pitch d = 0.30 mm. Operating frequency f = 5 MHz (λ ≈ 0.308 mm in tissue). Find the inter-element time delay to steer the beam at θ = 15°.

**Step 1: Governing equation.**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{d \sin\theta}{c}$$

**Step 2: Compute sin(15°).**

$$\sin(15^\circ) = 0.2588$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{0.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2588}{1540}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator.**

$$0.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2588 = 7.764 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Divide by c.**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{7.764 \times 10^{-5}}{1540} = 5.04 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s} = 50.4\ \text{ns}$$

**Answer:** The inter-element delay is **≈ 50.4 ns**.

> [!NOTE]
> Such nanosecond-scale delays are generated digitally using **high-speed shift registers or analog delay lines** in modern beamformers. KTU problems often expect you to compute these in **nanoseconds (ns)**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Frame Rate (Real-Time Constraint)

**Problem:** A real-time scanner images to a maximum depth of **15 cm** with **128 scan lines per frame**. Find the maximum frame rate.

**Step 1: Find PRF first.**

$$PRF = \frac{c}{2 D_{max}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.15}$$

$$PRF = \frac{1540}{0.30} = 5133\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2: Compute frame rate.**

$$F_{rate} = \frac{PRF}{N_{lines}} = \frac{5133}{128} = 40.1\ \text{fps}$$

**Answer:** The maximum frame rate is **≈ 40 frames per second** — well above the 15 fps real-time threshold.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Axial and Lateral Resolution

**Problem:** A probe operates at f = 5 MHz with a fractional bandwidth of 60%. Aperture = 20 mm. Find the resolution at depth r = 8 cm.

**Step 1: Wavelength.**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 0.308\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Pulse bandwidth (Hz).**

$$BW = 0.60 \times 5 \times 10^{6} = 3 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3: Axial resolution.**

$$\delta_{ax} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot BW} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{6}} = 2.57 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.257\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Lateral resolution.**

$$\delta_{lat} \approx \lambda \cdot \frac{r}{D_a} = 0.308 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{0.08}{0.020}$$

$$\delta_{lat} = 0.308 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 = 1.232 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 1.23\ \text{mm}$$

**Answer:** Axial resolution = **0.26 mm**, Lateral resolution = **1.23 mm**. Note that **lateral resolution is always poorer than axial** — a common KTU question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numericals

| Input | Computation | Result |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| t = 13 µs | d = 1540 × 13e-6 / 2 | d = 1.0 cm |
| f = 3.5 MHz | λ = 1540/3.5e6 | λ = 0.44 mm |
| D = 20 cm | PRF = 1540/(2×0.20) | PRF = 3.85 kHz |
| N = 200 lines | Frame rate = 3850/200 | ≈ 19.3 fps |
| Z₁ = 1.6 MRayl, Z₂ = 1.7 MRayl | R = [(1.7−1.6)/(1.7+1.6)]² | R = 0.000945 ≈ 0.09% |
| f = 7.5 MHz, BW = 4.5 MHz | δ_ax = 1540/(2×4.5e6) | δ_ax = 0.17 mm |
| d = 0.2 mm, θ = 30° | Δτ = 0.2e-3 × 0.5/1540 | Δτ = 64.9 ns |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Real-Time B-Mode Ultrasonic Imaging System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** of a real-time ultrasound scanner, showing how signals travel from the probe to the display.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulser Transmitter] -->|HV pulse| B[Transducer Probe]
    B -->|Acoustic burst| C[Patient Tissue]
    C -->|Echoes| B
    B -->|RF electrical signal| D[Receive Beamformer]
    D --> E[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    E --> F[Envelope Detector]
    F --> G[Log Compression]
    G --> H[Scan Converter Polar to Cartesian]
    H --> I[Video Display Monitor]
    I --> J[Operator Visualization]
    D -.->|Delay control| K[System Clock and Controller]
    A -.->|Trigger| K
    H -.->|Frame sync| K
    K -->|Master timing| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for exams:** Always label arrows with the **form of energy/signal** (HV pulse, RF signal, etc.) — this is a common KTU marking point. The **dashed lines** indicate control/timing signals; solid lines are data/signal flow.

## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram — Multi-Element Probe Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Connector Cable] --> P2[Backing Material Damper]
    P2 --> P3[Piezoelectric Elements PZT Array]
    P3 --> P4[Matching Layer 1]
    P4 --> P5[Matching Layer 2]
    P5 --> P6[Acoustic Lens]
    P6 --> P7[Patient Skin Surface]
    P3 -.->|Electrodes| P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional role of each layer (favourite KTU 2-mark question):**
> - **Backing material (damping block):** Absorbs backward-radiated energy to **shorten the pulse** → improves axial resolution. Typically tungsten-epoxy or rubber composite.
> - **Piezoelectric elements:** Convert electrical ↔ acoustic energy (transmit & receive).
> - **Matching layers (1 or 2):** Intermediate acoustic impedance (e.g., epoxy with metal powder) to **reduce reflection at the tissue-transducer interface** by quarter-wave impedance matching.
> - **Acoustic lens (convex):** Focuses the beam in the **elevation plane** (the plane perpendicular to the scan plane). The electronic beamformer focuses in the azimuth plane.

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Beam Steering Using Delays

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Element 1] --> D1[Delay 0 ns]
    A2[Element 2] --> D2[Delay 50 ns]
    A3[Element 3] --> D3[Delay 100 ns]
    A4[Element 4] --> D4[Delay 150 ns]
    D1 --> S[Common Summing Amplifier]
    D2 --> S
    D3 --> S
    D4 --> S
    S --> O[Constructive Interference Steered Wavefront]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For **receive beamforming**, the delays are applied in the **reverse order** so that echoes from a single focal point arrive **simultaneously** at the summer.

## 4.4 Types of Real-Time Probes — Comparative Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Ultrasound Probe Types] --> P1[Linear Array]
    P --> P2[Curvilinear Convex Array]
    P --> P3[Phased Array Sector]
    P --> P4[2D Matrix Array 4D]
    P1 --> P1A[Rectangular image, high freq, vascular MSK]
    P2 --> P2A[Pie shaped image, abdominal OB]
    P3 --> P3A[Sector pie image, cardiac TEE]
    P4 --> P4A[Volume 3D 4D imaging]
```

| Probe Type | Image Shape | Frequency | Typical Application |
|------------|-------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Linear | Rectangular | 5–15 MHz | Vascular, MSK, small parts |
| Curvilinear (Convex) | Pie (curved base) | 2–5 MHz | Abdominal, obstetric |
| Phased Array | Sector (small apex) | 2–8 MHz | Cardiac, transcranial |
| 2D Matrix | Pyramid (volume) | 2–8 MHz | 3D/4D obstetric, cardiac |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define real-time B-mode ultrasound imaging. List any two advantages of real-time imaging over static imaging.**

**Model Answer:**
Real-time B-mode (Brightness mode) ultrasound imaging is a diagnostic technique in which **two-dimensional cross-sectional images of internal body structures are continuously displayed** at a frame rate of **15–60 frames per second**, allowing visualization of moving structures such as the heart and fetus.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Motion visualization** — moving tissues (heart valves, blood flow, fetal movement) can be observed live, which is impossible in static (still-frame) imaging.
2. **Dynamic focusing** — the focal point can be electronically adjusted at multiple depths during receive, giving **uniform image quality** from near field to far field.
3. **Guided procedures** — real-time imaging enables the physician to guide needles, catheters, and biopsies accurately during interventions.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the function of the backing material and matching layers in an ultrasound probe.**

**Model Answer:**
**Backing material (Damping block):**
The backing material is attached to the rear face of the piezoelectric element. Its function is to **absorb the acoustic energy radiated backward** from the PZT element, thereby **shortening the pulse duration**. A shorter pulse has a **wider bandwidth**, which improves **axial (range) resolution**. It is typically made of tungsten-epoxy or rubber composite with high attenuation.

**Matching layers:**
The matching layers are placed between the PZT element and the patient’s skin. Because the acoustic impedance of PZT (~30 MRayl) is much higher than that of soft tissue (~1.6 MRayl), most of the acoustic energy would otherwise be reflected at the interface. The matching layer(s) provide a **gradual impedance transition** (using quarter-wave transformer principle), so that **more acoustic energy enters the tissue** and a stronger echo returns. Typically one or two layers of materials like epoxy filled with metal powder are used.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a real-time B-mode ultrasonic imaging system. Describe the function of each block.**

**Model Answer:**

*(Block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 above.)*

**Block-by-block explanation:**

1. **Pulser / Transmitter:** Generates a high-voltage (typically ±100 V) short-duration (0.1–1 µs) electrical pulse at a pulse repetition frequency (PRF) of 1–10 kHz. This pulse excites the transducer elements.

2. **Transducer Probe:** Contains an array of piezoelectric (PZT) elements. On transmit, the elements vibrate and emit an ultrasound burst into the tissue. On receive, returning echoes vibrate the elements, producing tiny RF electrical signals.

3. **T/R Switch (Transmit-Receive Switch):** A critical protection circuit that isolates the sensitive receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse (which could damage the front-end electronics). It activates the receiver only after the transmit pulse is over.

4. **Receive Beamformer:** Applies precise time delays (and sometimes variable gain) to the signals from individual elements so that echoes from a single focal point add **constructively**, while off-axis signals cancel. This process is called **dynamic receive focusing**.

5. **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC):** Echoes from deeper tissues are weaker due to attenuation. TGC amplifies later-arriving (deeper) signals more than early-arriving ones, so that echoes from equal reflectors at different depths appear equally bright on the display.

6. **Envelope Detector:** The high-frequency RF echo is rectified and low-pass filtered to extract the **envelope** (slowly varying amplitude) — discarding the high-frequency carrier information.

7. **Logarithmic Compression:** The dynamic range of echoes (~100 dB) is compressed to the displayable range (~40 dB) using a log amplifier. This allows weak and strong echoes to be visible simultaneously.

8. **Scan Converter:** Converts the **polar-coordinate** echo data (depth, angle) into **Cartesian pixel coordinates** (x, y) suitable for a standard raster video display.

9. **Display Monitor:** Displays the B-mode image as a 2D gray-scale map. Brightness of each pixel = echo amplitude; position = depth and lateral angle.

10. **Master Clock / Controller:** Synchronizes the entire system — it triggers the pulser, sets the beamformer delays, controls scan conversion, and updates the display at the required frame rate.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Derive the relationship between maximum imaging depth, PRF, and the number of scan lines per frame. A real-time scanner images to a maximum depth of 20 cm with 200 lines per frame. Find the maximum achievable frame rate. Assume c = 1540 m/s.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Maximum PRF is set by the round-trip time to maximum depth.**

The scanner must wait for the deepest echo to return before sending the next pulse (to avoid range ambiguity):

$$t_{max} = \frac{2 D_{max}}{c}$$

$$PRF_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{max}} = \frac{c}{2 D_{max}}$$

**Step 2: Frame rate is PRF divided by the number of scan lines per frame.**

$$F_{rate} = \frac{PRF_{max}}{N_{lines}} = \frac{c}{2 D_{max} \cdot N_{lines}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute D_max = 0.20 m, c = 1540 m/s, N_lines = 200.**

$$F_{rate} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.20 \times 200} = \frac{1540}{80} = 19.25\ \text{fps}$$

**Answer:** Maximum frame rate ≈ **19.25 fps**, which is above the **15 fps real-time threshold**, so the system qualifies as a real-time imager.

**KTU Examiner’s Note:** Always state whether the computed frame rate satisfies the **real-time criterion (≥ 15 fps)**. Many students compute the number but forget the interpretation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks] Compare linear, curvilinear, and phased array probes in terms of construction, image shape, frequency range, and clinical application. Add a note on the role of acoustic lens in an ultrasound probe.**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative table:**

| Feature | Linear Array | Curvilinear (Convex) Array | Phased Array |
|---------|--------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| **Element arrangement** | Elements in a straight line, pitch ~0.2–0.5 mm | Elements on a curved surface, convex | Small elements, all near a single point |
| **Image shape** | Rectangular | Pie-shaped (curved top, wide base) | Sector / pie slice (narrow apex at probe) |
| **Frequency** | 5–15 MHz (high resolution, shallow) | 2–5 MHz (deeper penetration) | 2–8 MHz |
| **Footprint** | Large flat | Medium curved | Small (fits between ribs) |
| **Clinical use** | Vascular, MSK, thyroid, breast | Abdominal, obstetric, pelvic | Cardiac, TEE, emergency (FAST) |
| **Beam steering** | Sequential sub-aperture scanning; minimal steering | Sequential sub-aperture scanning on curved surface | All elements used, large steering angles possible |

**Acoustic lens (additional 2–3 marks):**
The acoustic lens is a concave plastic lens attached to the front face of the array. Its function is to **focus the ultrasound beam in the elevation plane** (the plane perpendicular to the scan plane), because the electronic beamformer can only focus in the **azimuth (scan) plane**. The lens ensures uniform slice thickness across the imaging depth, improving image quality.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Explain the principle of electronic beam steering in a linear phased array. A 64-element linear array with element pitch d = 0.3 mm is to be steered at an angle of 20° in soft tissue. Compute the inter-element time delay. Assume c = 1540 m/s.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
In an electronically steered array, the same electrical pulse is applied to all elements, but with **progressively increasing time delays** across the array. The first element to be excited is the one that should send out the wavefront earliest. By selecting the delay pattern, the wavefront emerges as a plane wave propagating at an angle θ to the array normal — this is **electronic beam steering**.

For a desired steering angle θ, the path difference between two adjacent elements is d sin θ, and the corresponding time delay is:

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{d \sin\theta}{c}$$

**Numerical computation:**

**Step 1: sin(20°).**

$$\sin(20^\circ) = 0.3420$$

**Step 2: Substitute d = 0.30 mm = 0.30 × 10⁻³ m.**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{0.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3420}{1540}$$

**Step 3: Numerator.**

$$0.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3420 = 1.026 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Divide by c.**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{1.026 \times 10^{-4}}{1540} = 6.66 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s} = 66.6\ \text{ns}$$

**Answer:** The inter-element delay is **≈ 66.6 ns**. This delay must be applied progressively to elements 1, 2, 3, …, 64, i.e., total delay across the array = 63 × 66.6 ns ≈ 4.2 µs.

**On receive**, the same delays are applied in **reverse order** (or a new set tuned to a chosen focal depth) to dynamically focus the receive beam.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in depth = ct/2 → marks deducted.
> 2. **Using c = 1500 m/s** instead of the KTU standard **1540 m/s** for soft tissue → partial deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up transmit and receive focusing** — transmit has a single fixed focal depth; receive uses dynamic focusing at every depth.
> 4. **Confusing axial and lateral resolution** — axial depends on pulse bandwidth; lateral depends on aperture and wavelength.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the T/R switch** in the block diagram — examiners specifically look for it.
> 6. **Stating “real-time = fast”** without giving the **15 fps threshold** — partial credit only.
> 7. **Confusing probe types** — curvilinear = abdominal, phased = cardiac, linear = vascular is the standard KTU answer pattern.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Real-time B-mode imaging** = 2D gray-scale image updated at **≥ 15 fps**; brightness = echo amplitude.
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue c = 1540 m/s** is the **KTU standard** — use it unless told otherwise.
- **Depth equation:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ — always include the **factor of 2** for round trip.
- **Steering delay:** $\Delta\tau = \dfrac{d \sin\theta}{c}$ — produces the same in **nanoseconds** for typical arrays.
- **PRF max:** $PRF_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 D_{max}}$ — set by maximum imaging depth.
- **Frame rate:** $F_{rate} = \dfrac{PRF}{N_{lines}}$ — must be **≥ 15 fps** for real-time.
- **Axial resolution:** $\delta_{ax} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ — improve by widening bandwidth (shorter pulse, more damping).
- **Lateral resolution:** $\delta_{lat} \approx \lambda \cdot \dfrac{r}{D_a}$ — improve with **larger aperture, higher frequency**.
- **Resonant frequency of PZT:** $f_0 = \dfrac{v_p}{2 t_p}$ — half-wavelength resonator; t_p is element thickness.
- **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$ — large mismatch between PZT (~30 MRayl) and tissue (~1.6 MRayl) requires **matching layers**.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ — small impedance difference → small echo (e.g., soft tissue–soft tissue ~0.1 %).
- **Probe construction layers (front to back):** Acoustic lens → matching layer(s) → PZT element → backing material → cable/connector.
- **Backing material** shortens pulse → better axial resolution; **Matching layers** transmit more energy → better sensitivity.
- **Acoustic lens** focuses beam in the **elevation plane**; **electronic beamformer** focuses in the **azimuth plane**.
- **T/R switch** protects receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse.
- **TGC** compensates for tissue attenuation (~**0.5 dB/cm/MHz**).
- **Scan converter** maps polar (r, θ) data → Cartesian (x, y) for video display.
- **Probe types & uses:** Linear (vascular/MSK, 5–15 MHz) • Curvilinear (abdominal/OB, 2–5 MHz) • Phased (cardiac, 2–8 MHz).
- **Real-time criterion:** Frame rate ≥ **15 fps**; typical systems deliver 30–60 fps.
- **Dynamic receive focusing:** focal point moved electronically from near to far during reception — gives uniform lateral resolution at all depths.
- **Master clock/controller** is the “heart” of the system — synchronizes pulser, beamformer, scan converter, and display.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging - Basic NMR Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT613 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Resonance Imaging - Basic NMR Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** is the physical phenomenon in which nuclei possessing a non-zero net spin angular momentum (spin quantum number $I \neq 0$) absorb and re-emit electromagnetic energy at a specific resonance frequency when placed in a strong static magnetic field. The resonance condition is governed by the **Larmor equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Larmor Equation (KTU High-Yield):** 
> $$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$
> where $\omega_0$ is the angular Larmor precession frequency (rad/s), $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio (rad/s/T), and $B_0$ is the static magnetic flux density (Tesla).

**Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is the clinical imaging modality that spatially maps the NMR signal (principally from hydrogen-1 protons in tissue water and fat) to construct tomographic images of the human body.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Top in Earth's Field

Imagine a child's spinning top tilted slightly off the vertical in Earth's gravitational field. The top doesn't fall — it **wobbles** (precesses) around the vertical axis. 

In MRI, the proton acts like a tiny spinning top. When you place it inside the **Main Magnet's** field $B_0$:
- The proton's spin axis is not aligned with $B_0$ — it **precesses** around $B_0$ like the wobbling top.
- The wobble frequency is the **Larmor frequency** $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$.
- A short burst of **Radiofrequency (RF) energy** at exactly this Larmor frequency is like flicking the top — it "tips" the protons away from equilibrium.
- When the RF is switched off, the protons "relax" back, and as they relax they emit a faint radio signal — this is the **NMR signal (Free Induction Decay, FID)** that the scanner detects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hydrogen ($^1$H)?** The human body is ~63% water by mass. Each water molecule has **two hydrogen nuclei** (protons), giving an enormous, biologically abundant NMR signal source. For $^1$H, $\gamma = 42.577$ **MHz/T** — the standard MRI reference constant.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Kerala Board Frequently Quoted)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Gyromagnetic ratio of $^1$H | $\gamma$ | **42.577 MHz/T** |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Typical clinical $B_0$ field | $B_0$ | **1.5 T or 3.0 T** |
| Resulting Larmor frequency (1.5 T) | $f_0$ | **~63.87 MHz** |
| Body temperature (thermal eq.) | $T$ | **310 K** |

### 1.4 The Seven Basic NMR/MRI Hardware Components

An MRI scanner is essentially a sophisticated NMR spectrometer. The **KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus** specifically requires knowledge of the following seven fundamental building blocks:

1. **Main (Static) Magnet** — generates the uniform $B_0$ field.
2. **Gradient Coil System** — three orthogonal coils (X, Y, Z) producing linearly varying fields for spatial encoding.
3. **Radiofrequency (RF) Transmitter Coil** — delivers the $B_1$ excitation pulse.
4. **RF Receiver Coil** — detects the FID signal from the patient.
5. **Shim Coils** — correct $B_0$ field inhomogeneities.
6. **Computer / Pulse Sequencer** — orchestrates timing of gradients and RF pulses.
7. **Cryogenic Cooling System** — maintains superconducting magnet at ~4 K using liquid helium.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence — "The MRI Pulse Cycle"

The NMR process follows a precise four-stage cycle (sometimes called the **"MR Cycle"**):

**Stage 1 — Equilibrium Polarization (Patient at Rest in $B_0$)**
- At thermal equilibrium, the proton ensemble develops a net macroscopic magnetization vector $\vec{M}_0$ aligned **parallel** to $B_0$ (the z-axis).
- Only a tiny excess of protons align with $B_0$ (parallel) versus anti-parallel. The fractional excess is given by the Boltzmann ratio:
$$\frac{N_\uparrow - N_\downarrow}{N_\uparrow + N_\downarrow} \approx \frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T}$$
- This tiny excess is what produces the detectable signal — a fundamental sensitivity limit of MRI.

**Stage 2 — RF Excitation (Application of $B_1$ pulse)**
- A short RF pulse at frequency $\omega_0$ generates an oscillating magnetic field $B_1$ perpendicular to $B_0$ (conventionally along the x'-axis in the rotating frame).
- The flip angle $\theta$ is controlled by the pulse duration $\tau_p$ and amplitude $B_1$:
$$\theta = \gamma B_1 \tau_p$$
- A **90° pulse** tips $\vec{M}$ entirely into the transverse (xy) plane, maximizing detectable signal.
- A **180° pulse** inverts $\vec{M}$ to the -z direction — used in spin-echo sequences.

**Stage 3 — Free Precession and Relaxation (Signal Acquisition)**
- After the RF pulse, $\vec{M}$ precesses around $B_0$ at $\omega_0$ while relaxing back to equilibrium.
- Two simultaneous relaxation processes occur:
  - **T₁ (Spin-Lattice / Longitudinal) Recovery** of $M_z$ toward $M_0$.
  - **T₂ (Spin-Spin / Transverse) Decay** of $M_{xy}$ toward zero.
- The receiver coil picks up the decaying oscillating voltage — the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**.

**Stage 4 — Signal Detection and Image Reconstruction**
- The FID is digitized, Fourier-transformed (in k-space), and inverse-Fourier-transformed to form the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Different Relaxation Times?** 
> - $T_1$ describes **energy transfer** from excited protons to the surrounding molecular lattice (hence "spin-lattice"). Fat has short $T_1$, water has long $T_1$.
> - $T_2$ describes **dephasing** between neighboring protons due to local magnetic field fluctuations. Different tissues have characteristic $T_2$ values — this is the basis of $T_1$- and $T_2$-weighted imaging contrast.

### 2.2 Bloch Equations — The Master Equations of NMR

The time evolution of the magnetization vector $\vec{M} = (M_x, M_y, M_z)$ in a magnetic field is described by the **Bloch equations** (1946):

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{M} \times \vec{B})_x - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{M} \times \vec{B})_y - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{M} \times \vec{B})_z - \frac{M_1 - M_z}{T_1}$$

For the simple case of only $B_0$ along z (no RF, after a 90° pulse), the solutions are:

$$M_x(t) = M_0 \sin(\omega_0 t) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

$$M_y(t) = M_0 \cos(\omega_0 t) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-critical equations** for Module 4. Memorize the symbol, the formula, and the SI unit for each.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Larmor frequency (angular) | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | $\omega_0$ [rad/s], $\gamma$ [rad/s/T], $B_0$ [T] |
| 2 | Larmor frequency (cyclic) | $f_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | $f_0$ [Hz] |
| 3 | Resonant photon energy | $E = h f_0 = \hbar \omega_0$ | $E$ [J], $h$ [J·s], $\hbar$ [J·s/rad] |
| 4 | Boltzmann polarization | $\dfrac{\Delta N}{N} \approx \dfrac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{2 k_B T}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Flip angle | $\theta = \gamma B_1 \tau_p$ | $\theta$ [rad], $\tau_p$ [s] |
| 6 | FID transverse decay | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}$ | $T_2$ [s] |
| 7 | T₁ recovery | $M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$ | $T_1$ [s] |
| 8 | Equilibrium magnetization | $M_0 = \dfrac{N \gamma^2 \hbar^2 B_0}{4 k_B T}$ | $M_0$ [A/m] |
| 9 | SNR ∝ field strength | $\text{SNR} \propto B_0$ (approx., at 1.5T) | — |
| 10 | Gradient strength | $G = \dfrac{\partial B_z}{\partial x}$ | $G$ [T/m] |
| 11 | Receiver signal voltage (Faraday) | $V(t) = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $V$ [V], $\Phi$ [Wb] |

| **Diagnostic Radiology** | Brain, spine, joint, cardiac, abdominal imaging without ionizing radiation. |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Larmor Precession Frequency from Torque on a Magnetic Moment

**Starting Physical Law:** A magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$.

**Step 1 — Express the nuclear magnetic moment in terms of spin angular momentum.**

The nuclear magnetic moment is related to the spin angular momentum $\vec{S}$ by the gyromagnetic ratio $\gamma$:

$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's rotational equation.**

Torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum:

$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{S}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the torque expression.**

$$\frac{d\vec{S}}{dt} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B} = \gamma \vec{S} \times \vec{B}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $d\vec{S}/dt$ is perpendicular to $\vec{S}$.**

This is the kinematic condition for circular (precessional) motion about the direction of $\vec{B}$. The magnitude of $\vec{S}$ remains constant; only its direction changes.

**Step 5 — The precession angular velocity is given by the Larmor relation.**

The component of $\vec{S}$ in the plane perpendicular to $\vec{B}$ rotates with angular speed:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(for } \vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z} \text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The Larmor frequency is *independent* of the tilt angle of $\vec{S}$ from $\vec{B}$. All protons in a uniform field precess at exactly the same $\omega_0$ — this **coherent precession** is what creates the macroscopic measurable signal $M_{xy}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — T₁ and T₂ Relaxation Solutions to the Bloch Equations

**Step 1 — Simplify the Bloch equations for the post-90°-pulse case.**

After a 90° RF pulse tips $\vec{M}$ into the xy-plane, with $B_0$ only along z:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma B_0 M_y - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = -\gamma B_0 M_x - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \frac{M_0 - M_z}{T_1}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the z-component (T₁ recovery).**

This is a first-order linear ODE. At $t=0^+$, $M_z(0) = 0$ (just after 90° pulse).

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} + \frac{M_z}{T_1} = \frac{M_0}{T_1}$$

Integrating factor method yields:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the transverse components (T₂ decay).**

Define complex transverse magnetization $M_\perp = M_x + i M_y$. Multiplying the first two equations and combining:

$$\frac{dM_\perp}{dt} = -i \gamma B_0 M_\perp - \frac{M_\perp}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_\perp}{dt} = -M_\perp \left( \frac{1}{T_2} + i \gamma B_0 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with initial condition $M_\perp(0) = M_0$ (for a 90° pulse).**

$$M_\perp(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \, e^{-i \omega_0 t}$$

**Step 5 — Separate into real and imaginary parts.**

$$M_x(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \, \sin(\omega_0 t)$$

$$M_y(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \, \cos(\omega_0 t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The detected receiver voltage $V(t) \propto M_\perp(t)$ shows an exponentially decaying sinusoid — this is the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**. The decay envelope's time constant is **T₂**, while the oscillation frequency is **$\omega_0$**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio Scaling with Field Strength

**Step 1 — Equilibrium magnetization is proportional to $B_0$ (from Boltzmann).**

$$M_0 \propto B_0$$

**Step 2 — Induced receiver voltage is proportional to $dM/dt$, hence to $\omega_0 M_0$.**

$$V \propto \omega_0 M_0 \propto (\gamma B_0)(B_0) = \gamma B_0^2$$

**Step 3 — Thermal (Johnson) noise voltage is $\sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$, independent of $B_0$.**

**Step 4 — Combine for SNR.**

$$\text{SNR} \propto \frac{V}{V_{\text{noise}}} \propto B_0^2$$

In practice, body-coil dominated systems show SNR scaling closer to $B_0^{1.5}$ to $B_0^{1.65}$ at clinical field strengths.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tissue Relaxation Properties (Body @ 1.5 T)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often include a small table-comparison question. Memorize approximate magnitudes.

| Tissue | T₁ (ms) | T₂ (ms) | Proton Density (relative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSF (Cerebrospinal Fluid) | ~4000 | ~2000 | High |
| White Matter (Brain) | ~780 | ~90 | Medium |
| Gray Matter (Brain) | ~920 | ~100 | Medium-High |
| Fat (Subcutaneous) | ~250 | ~70 | High |
| Skeletal Muscle | ~870 | ~50 | Low-Medium |
| Liver | ~580 | ~50 | Medium |
| Blood (arterial) | ~1200 | ~200 | High |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** Calculate the Larmor frequency (in MHz) and resonant photon energy (in eV) for hydrogen-1 protons in a 1.5 T MRI scanner.

**Given:** $\gamma = 42.577 \times 10^6$ rad/s/T, $B_0 = 1.5$ T, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $1$ eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.

**Solution — Step 1 (Angular frequency):**

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 = (42.577 \times 10^6)(1.5) = 63.866 \times 10^6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Solution — Step 2 (Cyclic frequency):**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{63.866 \times 10^6}{2\pi} \approx 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

**Solution — Step 3 (Photon energy in Joules):**

$$E = h f_0 = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(63.87 \times 10^6) = 4.232 \times 10^{-26} \text{ J}$$

**Solution — Step 4 (Convert to eV):**

$$E = \frac{4.232 \times 10^{-26}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 2.64 \times 10^{-7} \text{ eV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The photon energy is **~10⁷ times smaller** than an X-ray photon. This is why MRI is considered non-ionizing radiation — it cannot break chemical bonds, making it biologically safer than CT.

### 4.3 The MR Signal Acquisition Cycle — Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1: Patient inserted into bore]
    step2[Step 2: B0 establishes equilibrium M0 along z]
    step3[Step 3: Gradient Gz selects slice]
    step4[Step 4: RF 90 degree pulse tips M into xy plane]
    step5[Step 5: Gradient Gx frequency encodes x position]
    step6[Step 6: Gradient Gy phase encodes y position]
    step7[Step 7: FID signal acquired during readout]
    step8[Step 8: T1 and T2 relaxation back to equilibrium]
    step9[Step 9: Repeat TR interval for next k-space line]
    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Linkage:** The blocks P3, P4, P5 (gradient coils) and P6 (RF transmitter) are all orchestrated by **P8 (pulse sequencer)**. This is why the sequencer is the "conductor of the orchestra" in an MRI scanner — without precise timing, image formation is impossible.
